name: Home
title: Home
description: Entry page to start electronic repair service searches, set or change location, access quick search shortcuts, browse categories, and see featured providers.

core_features: Load homepage data (current location, quick search examples, top service categories, featured providers) using getHomePageData on page load.
Load the single-user profile to prefill location and search inputs using getProfile.
Display the current resolved location and allow the user to update it via a location input that calls setCurrentLocation.
Provide a primary search form to search for repair services by keyword and location, navigating to search_results.html with the appropriate URL parameters.
Provide a 'near me' search action that starts a search in search_results.html using the current resolved location and an empty keyword.
Display quick search example shortcuts that, when clicked, navigate to search_results.html with both a prefilled query and serviceCode, using the current resolved location.
Display top service categories from getHomePageData and allow navigation to categories.html.
Display featured providers from getHomePageData for quick discovery of notable repair services.
user_workflows: Set or change current location: 1) User sees the current location loaded from getHomePageData (and optionally profile preferences) in the location panel. 2) User clicks a 'Change location' action to reveal a location input field. 3) User enters a location text (such as a postal code or city/state) and submits the form. 4) The component calls setCurrentLocation(location_text). 5) On success, the component updates the displayed current location with resolved_location.display_name from the response and clears any previous error messages. 6) Future searches from this page (main search, 'near me', quick search examples) use the updated display name for the location parameter in their navigation URLs. 7) On failure (success=false), the component keeps the previous location and shows the returned message as an error, allowing the user to try again.

Search by keyword and location using the main search form: 1) On page load, the main search form pre-populates its location field with the current resolved location’s display name from getHomePageData, or falls back to profile preferred location fields if available. 2) The user types a free-text search term describing the repair need into the query input. 3) The user may adjust the location field or leave the prefilled value. 4) The user submits the search form. 5) The form constructs a navigation URL to search_results.html with q set to the entered search term, location set to the location field value, and view set to 'list'. 6) The browser navigates to that URL, where search_results.html handles the search based on those parameters.

Run a generic 'near me' search: 1) On page load, the 'near me' button is enabled only if a current location display name is available from getHomePageData or from a successful setCurrentLocation call. 2) The user clicks the 'near me' button without entering a keyword. 3) The button constructs a navigation URL to search_results.html with q set to an empty string, location set to the current resolved location display name from the page state, and view set to 'list'. 4) The browser navigates to that URL so the search results page can show providers near that location.

Use quick search examples: 1) On page load, quick_search_examples is obtained from getHomePageData and rendered as a list of clickable items, each showing its label. 2) The user clicks a quick search example. 3) The click handler reads the selected example’s query and service_code along with the current resolved location display name. 4) It constructs a navigation URL to search_results.html with q set to the example.query, serviceCode set to the example.service_code, location set to the current resolved location display name, and view set to 'list'. 5) The browser navigates to that URL and the search results page performs a search tailored to that quick example.

Browse repair categories: 1) On page load, top_categories from getHomePageData is displayed as a list or grid of category shortcuts. 2) A separate call-to-action element (such as a 'Browse all services' link or button) is displayed within the same component. 3) The user either clicks one of the category shortcuts or the 'Browse all services' action. 4) The click handler navigates to categories.html without additional URL parameters, where the categories page can handle showing all categories and potentially highlighting the selected one based on its own logic.

Review featured providers: 1) On page load, featured_providers from getHomePageData is rendered as a list. 2) Each item displays structured provider information available in the Provider model (such as name, rating, and location fields) to help the user quickly scan options. 3) The user can scan this list before deciding whether to perform a search via the main search form, quick search examples, or the 'near me' action.
interactions: Page load interaction: When the Home page loads, a data-loader component calls getHomePageData and getProfile. Until these calls resolve, dependent components display loading states. Once data is returned, it is stored in in-memory page state and passed into child components (location panel, search form, quick search examples, categories, featured providers). On error, an error state is propagated to show a non-blocking message while still allowing partial functionality where possible.

Location input interaction: When the user focuses and types into the location change input, the new text is stored in the component’s internal state. On submission, the component disables the submit action, calls setCurrentLocation with the entered text, and then re-enables the action after the response. On a successful response, the current-location-display state is updated to the returned resolved_location.display_name and other resolved_location fields; on a failed response, an error message from the API is shown and the previous location remains active.

Main search form interaction: The user can type freely into the keyword input. The location input can be edited or left as the prefilled value from current_location or profile. When the form is submitted, default browser submission is prevented; instead the handler constructs a URL for search_results.html with q, location, and view=list encoded as URL query parameters, then performs a client-side navigation to that URL.

Near me button interaction: Clicking the 'near me' button checks whether a current location display name exists in the page state. If it exists, the button constructs a URL to search_results.html with q empty, location set to that display name, and view=list, then navigates. If it does not exist, the button instead focuses or reveals the location input in the current-location panel so the user can set a location first.

Quick search example interaction: Each quick search example item is clickable. On click, the handler reads the example’s query and service_code fields plus the current resolved location display name. It then builds a URL to search_results.html with q, serviceCode, location, and view=list as query parameters and navigates to that URL.

Category shortcut interaction: Each top category element is clickable, as is the 'Browse all services' action. On click, the handler navigates directly to categories.html (no additional query parameters), allowing that page to show all categories or handle selection as needed.

Header navigation interaction: Header navigation links (such as to favorites, profile, help, and categories) trigger simple navigations to their respective pages via standard URL-based routing. The Home link points to index.html, allowing users to return to this page at any time.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Home page itself does not require incoming URL parameters; it initializes its state entirely from API responses (getHomePageData and getProfile) and user actions. All navigation away from the Home page is performed via URL parameters as follows: (1) Main search form: search_results.html?q={encoded search term}&location={encoded location input}&view=list, where q and location are taken directly from user inputs in the form. (2) 'Near me' search: search_results.html?q=&location={encoded resolvedCurrentLocationDisplayName}&view=list, where resolvedCurrentLocationDisplayName is derived from getHomePageData.current_location.display_name or updated via a successful setCurrentLocation call. (3) Quick search examples: search_results.html?q={encoded quickSearch.query}&serviceCode={encoded quickSearch.service_code}&location={encoded resolvedCurrentLocationDisplayName}&view=list, where quickSearch values are provided by getHomePageData.quick_search_examples, and location uses the current resolved location in page state. (4) Categories navigation: categories.html (no query parameters) for both the 'Browse all services' action and individual top category shortcuts. Location and other user preferences are not persisted via URL parameters on the Home page; persistence is handled server-side by the setCurrentLocation interface and reflected on subsequent calls to getHomePageData and getProfile. The Home page keeps transient state (current resolved location, input field values, loading and error flags) only in memory for the duration of the session, and does not rely on localStorage or any other client-side persistent storage.

components.functionality: Initializes the Home page by calling getHomePageData and getProfile on page load, then distributes the resulting data (current location, quick search examples, top categories, featured providers, and profile info) to other components through shared in-memory state.

Displays the current resolved location and allows the user to update it. When the user submits a new location text, it calls setCurrentLocation to resolve and persist the location, then updates the page's current location state for use by search actions.

Provides the main keyword and location search interface. Prefills the location field from the current location or profile, captures the user's keyword, and on submit navigates to search_results.html with q, location, and view=list as URL parameters.

Triggers a generic search for providers near the current resolved location. When clicked, it constructs a URL to search_results.html with q empty, location set to the current resolved location display name, and view=list, then navigates to that URL. If no location is available, it prompts the user to set a location via the current-location-panel.

Lists quick search examples provided by getHomePageData. Each example shows its label and, when clicked, navigates to search_results.html with q set to the example's query, serviceCode set to the example's service_code, location set to the current resolved location display name, and view=list.

Displays top service categories from getHomePageData as shortcuts and exposes a 'Browse all services' action. Clicking any shortcut or the main categories action navigates to categories.html (no additional URL parameters).

Shows a list of featured providers loaded from getHomePageData.featured_providers so users can quickly see notable repair services. It displays key fields such as provider name, rating, and location details without performing additional API calls.
Provides access to primary site sections including Home, Categories, Favorites, Profile, and Help. Uses simple URL-based navigation so users can move between core sections of the site.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px). Nav, headline, search bar, and category pills all appear horizontally aligned to this central grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a linear, top‑to‑bottom structure anchored by a fixed header. The page opens with a full‑width hero that carries the background map image with a dark overlay. Inside that hero, a centered content column (max ~1100–1200px) holds, from top to bottom: the current-location panel (subtle), the main multi-line headline, then the primary search form (with integrated near‑me button), followed by a line of quick search examples, and finally a horizontal strip of category shortcut pills at the lower edge of the hero. Below the hero, the featured providers list sits in its own light section. All major foreground elements align to the same central grid to echo the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a linear, top‑to‑bottom structure anchored by a fixed header. The page opens with a full‑width hero that carries the background map image with a dark overlay. Inside that hero, a centered content column (max ~1100–1200px) holds, from top to bottom: the current-location panel (subtle), the main multi-line headline, then the primary search form (with integrated near‑me button), followed by a line of quick search examples, and finally a horizontal strip of category shortcut pills at the lower edge of the hero. Below the hero, the featured providers list sits in its own light section. All major foreground elements align to the same central grid to echo the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The fixed header is relatively shallow (~64–72px high). The hero underneath occupies roughly the full initial viewport height on desktop. Within the hero, the top ~35–40% is used for top padding, the current location panel, and the large headline; the middle ~30% is devoted to the prominent search form and quick search examples; the lower ~20% is occupied by the category shortcuts with generous bottom padding. The featured providers section begins right under the hero with a clear section break and has its own vertical padding (~64–80px top and bottom). Horizontal content is constrained to the central container while the background image spans full width.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately spacious layout: large tap targets, generous internal padding for interactive elements, and clear breathing room between stacked sections. The hero remains visually simple: one primary headline, one central search module, a thin line of quick examples, and a single row of category pills. Navigation is concise in the header with just a small set of links and a single standout CTA. The featured providers section is denser than the hero but still uses clear card spacing and consistent vertical rhythm.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual attention begins at the top-left logo in the fixed header, then scans right across the navigation and its CTA. From there, the eye drops into the hero, immediately catching the bold headline, with any highlighted words in blue reinforcing the tech/startup feel. The next strong focus is the high-contrast search bar centered beneath the headline. Within the search bar, attention moves left to right across the category input, location input (with near-me button), and ends at the bright blue search CTA. Below, the subtle quick search examples act as hints, followed by the more visually substantial category shortcut pills. After users understand how to search, the flow continues down into the featured providers grid as a natural “what you can get here” follow-up.

layout.overall_description: The page presents a modern, conversion-focused hero over a full-width map-of-Romania background darkened by a gradient. A fixed, semi-transparent navigation bar sits at the very top, spanning the width of the viewport, with a centered content container that keeps the logo, links, and CTA lined up with everything below. Just beneath this header, the hero content is vertically centered within a tall band that roughly fills the first screen. On the left side of the central column, a compact location pill quietly states the user’s current city, leading into a large, multi-line white headline that sells the value of finding electronic repair services. Directly beneath, a substantial pill-shaped search module stretches across most of the content width, with the category selector on the left, location field (with a near-me icon button embedded on its right edge) in the middle, and a bold blue search button on the right. Under this, a single line of quick search examples appears as understated, clickable text hints aligned with the left side of the search bar. Toward the bottom of the hero, spanning the same central width, a row of softly elevated, rounded category shortcut cards runs horizontally, visually bridging the hero and the content below. As the hero ends, the background shifts to a clean white (or very light) section where a “Featured providers” heading introduces a grid of provider cards, all aligned to the same 12‑column grid, reinforcing structure and continuity. Overall, the layout feels focused and guided: from navigation to headline, from headline to search, from search to shortcuts, and finally into concrete provider options.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader is not a persistent visual element but conceptually wraps the main hero and the featured providers section. When active (e.g., on first page load or while fetching location/providers), it temporarily overlays the central content area—from just below the fixed header down to the bottom of the viewport—with a subtle, semi-transparent dark veil and a centered spinner or skeleton placeholders that align to the same 1100–1200px content width. Horizontally, this overlay extends to full viewport width to match the hero, but all loading indicators remain centered within the content column so they feel integrated with the eventual layout rather than detached.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Absolutely positioned overlay, covering the hero and upper content area when active; otherwise non-visual and occupying no space in the normal layout flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header navigation is a fixed, full-width bar anchored to the very top of the viewport. It has a height of approximately 64–72px and rests above the hero background, using a semi-transparent dark backdrop (optionally with a subtle blur) so the logo and links remain legible over the map image. Within this bar, a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px) aligns all content with the hero below. The logo sits flush to the left, occupying roughly 15–20% of the bar’s width. To the right, a compact horizontal row of main navigation links sits mid-right, with a prominent, filled CTA button aligned to the far right edge of the container. Vertical padding (around 16px) above and below the nav items keeps the bar feeling light but distinct. A very subtle bottom border or drop shadow helps visually separate the header from the hero content as users scroll.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed at the top of the viewport, spanning full width; remains visible while the rest of the page scrolls underneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The current-location panel appears inside the hero, aligned with the left edge of the main content column. It sits just below the fixed header, with approximately 80–96px of top padding between the bottom of the header and the panel to give breathing room. Visually, it is a compact, pill-shaped container with slightly rounded corners and subtle elevation, about the height of a small button. It spans only as wide as its content (typically no more than 30–40% of the content width), and includes a small location pin icon on the far left, followed by the current city name and a lighter, clickable ‘Change’ label. This panel is positioned above the main hero headline, separated from it by about 16–24px of vertical space, so it reads as a contextual tag for the content rather than competing with the headline. The entire element remains anchored to the left side of the 12‑column grid, leaving ample negative space to the right to keep the hero feeling roomy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero’s content column, scrolling away with the hero as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary search form is the central interactive focus of the hero and sits directly beneath the main headline, separated by a vertical gap of roughly 24–32px. It is presented as a large, horizontally oriented pill-shaped container with a white or very light interior against the darkened hero background, giving it strong contrast and subtle drop shadow. The form is centered within the content column and spans about 80–90% of that width (max ~900–960px), ensuring it feels substantial but not edge-to-edge. Inside this container, content is laid out left to right in three main zones: on the left, a category input field (with label and placeholder) taking about 40% of the form’s width; in the middle, a location input field occupying another ~35–40%; on the right, a bold blue search CTA button occupying the remaining width (~20–25%), vertically stretching to match the height of the inputs for a unified pill appearance. Each input has 8–12px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding. There are internal gaps of about 8–16px between the category field, the location field, and the CTA button. The entire module sits roughly in the vertical middle of the hero, ensuring that, together with the headline, it occupies the central third of the screen.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, centered within the hero content; it scrolls naturally with the page and maintains alignment to the central grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The near-me search button is visually and functionally tied to the location portion of the primary search form rather than existing as a separate, detached button. It appears as a small circular or pill-shaped icon button embedded at the right edge inside the location input field. Within the search form’s horizontal layout, the location field reserves a narrow strip (about 10–15% of its own width) on the right for this button, with the text input area to its left. The near-me button shows a GPS/target-style icon and is slightly elevated, with a blue or outlined style that harmonizes with the main search CTA but is clearly secondary. It aligns vertically centered within the location field and does not extend beyond the overall search form container, preserving the simple three-part structure (category, location, main CTA). This placement allows users’ eyes to move naturally from ‘Location’ label to the near-me icon as an obvious shortcut to set their location.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and visually nested within the primary search form, occupying space inside the location input area rather than its own separate block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quick search examples list sits directly beneath the primary search form inside the hero, aligned to the left edge of the search bar to maintain a clean visual anchor. It is separated from the search form by a vertical gap of about 12–16px, keeping it visually related but clearly secondary. The list is rendered as a single line (or at most two lines) of compact text suggestions labeled with something like ‘Try:’ followed by 3–5 popular search phrases. Each example appears as a small, clickable text link or very subtle pill, with minimal padding and low visual weight. The list spans roughly 60–70% of the content width, leaving empty space on the right side to avoid crowding. Against the dark hero, the text uses a lighter but slightly muted color compared to the headline, indicating that these are hints rather than primary calls to action. Overall, it occupies only a thin band of vertical space in the middle of the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static text content, inline within the hero’s content column, moving with the page scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The top categories shortcuts form a horizontal row of rounded cards located near the bottom of the hero, serving as visually elevated shortcuts into common repair types. This row is centered within the same max-width content container and aligned to the 12‑column grid. It sits approximately 24–32px below the quick search examples list, but with enough additional bottom padding (around 64px) before the very bottom of the hero to keep the layout from feeling cramped. Each category card is pill- or capsule-shaped with soft shadows and semi-opaque or slightly blurred backgrounds that subtly show the hero’s dark gradient behind them. They are arranged in a single row on desktop (wrapping into multiple rows on narrower screens if necessary), each roughly 120–160px wide with 16–24px internal padding. Inside each card, an icon appears on the left with the category label to the right. The entire row visually reads as a band of clickable chips that guide users who prefer browsing to typing. The row’s overall width is governed by the content, typically filling the middle 70–80% of the container and centered horizontally.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero’s content area; cards are laid out in a flexible horizontal row that can wrap when space is constrained, but the block itself scrolls away with the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured providers list occupies the first major section immediately below the hero, sitting on a clean, white (or very light) background to clearly differentiate it from the dark hero. There is a distinct visual break: the hero ends and this section begins with a top margin/padding of about 64–80px from the bottom of the category shortcuts, creating a sense of stepping into content results. The section spans the full viewport width, but all provider content is constrained to the same central 1100–1200px container. At the top-left of this container is a section heading such as ‘Featured providers’, set with enough prominence to define the section but smaller than the hero headline. Optionally, light filters or a ‘View all’ link appear aligned to the top-right of this container. Below the heading, the providers appear as a grid of cards, typically 2–3 cards per row depending on the available width. Each card has consistent padding (16–24px), subtle shadow, and rounded corners, with enough horizontal and vertical gap (16–24px) between cards to maintain clarity and avoid visual overload. This section extends vertically according to the number of featured items and ends with matching bottom padding (~64–80px) before any subsequent sections, maintaining the established vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section within the normal document flow, anchored below the hero and scrolling naturally as a block.

name: Search Results
title: Search Results
description: Search, filter, sort, compare, and favorite electronic repair providers in list or map view based on query, location, and service-specific criteria.

core_features: Load and display providers that match the current query, location, and URL-based filters using searchProviders.
Fetch available filter and sort options for the current query/category using getSearchFilterOptions and bind them to filter controls.
Allow users to change location (ZIP or city/state) using setCurrentLocation and immediately re-run the search with the updated location.
Filter results by distance, rating, price caps for the focused service, service type/category, visit type/logistics, availability/hours, and fees/benefits, all mapped to URL parameters.
Sort providers by criteria such as rating, price, and distance using a sort dropdown bound to the sort URL parameter.
Toggle between list and map views by updating the view URL parameter and using searchProviders.map_pins for the map.
Select and manage providers for comparison using addProviderToCompare, removeProviderFromCompare, and clearCompareList, and show a compare bar with the current selection count.
Favorite/unfavorite providers directly from results using addProviderToFavorites and removeProviderFromFavorites, reflecting is_favorite state from searchProviders.
Navigate to provider_detail.html from list or map view with providerId and serviceCode URL parameters.
Use pagination (page URL parameter) to navigate through multiple pages of search results.
user_workflows:
Search and view results:
1. User arrives on search_results.html with URL parameters (e.g., q, location, serviceCode, categoryId, filters, sort, view, page).
2. Page reads URL parameters and calls getSearchFilterOptions with q, serviceCode, categoryId to populate filter and sort options.
3. Page constructs search parameters from URL (query, location_text, distance_miles_max, rating_min, service_code, service_category_id, price_filter, visit_type_filters, availability_filters, fee_filters, sort_by, view_mode, page) and calls searchProviders.
4. Page displays providers in list view if view is list, or in map view if view is map, along with total_results and applied_filters_summary.
5. User scrolls through results, can paginate via page parameter, and can click any provider to open provider_detail.html.

Adjust filters and sort:
1. User changes one or more filters in the filter-sidebar (distance, rating, price cap, service type, visit type, availability, fees).
2. Component updates the corresponding URL parameters (e.g., distanceMax, ratingMin, priceMax, visitInStore, visitHome, visitPickup, freePickup, openSaturday, closeAfter, is247, emergency, freeDiagnostic, noDiagnosticFee, noFixNoFee, serviceCode, categoryId) and resets page to 1.
3. Browser updates the URL and the page re-reads URL params.
4. Page calls getSearchFilterOptions (if q/serviceCode/categoryId changed) and then calls searchProviders with the updated parameters.
5. Results list or map updates to reflect the new filtered set, and applied-filters-bar updates its pills.

Toggle list/map view and interact with map:
1. User clicks the view toggle (list or map) in the sort-and-view-bar.
2. Component updates the view URL parameter to list or map, keeping all other URL parameters intact.
3. Page re-runs searchProviders with the new view_mode.
4. If view=map, map-panel renders map_pins from searchProviders.
5. User clicks a pin, map-pin-popup opens with basic provider info; user can favorite, add to compare, or click through to provider_detail.html.

Manage comparison list:
1. In list or map view, user toggles the "Add to Compare" control on a provider.
2. Component calls addProviderToCompare(providerId) when enabling, or removeProviderFromCompare(providerId) when disabling.
3. The compare APIs return an updated compare_list and providers plus message; page updates local UI selection state for those providers.
4. compare-bar shows the current compare_selection_count (from searchProviders and/or the compare API responses) and enables the "Compare (N)" button when count > 1.
5. User clicks "Compare (N)", navigating to compare.html (no additional URL params required as selection is managed via CompareList in the backend).
6. User can click "Clear all" in compare-bar, which calls clearCompareList and resets all local compare toggles.

Manage favorites from results:
1. User clicks the favorite icon/button on a provider card or map popup.
2. If provider is not currently a favorite (is_favorite is false), component calls addProviderToFavorites(providerId); otherwise it calls removeProviderFromFavorites(providerId).
3. The favorites API returns success and updated favorites_count; component updates the favorite state of that provider in the UI.
4. On subsequent calls to searchProviders, the is_favorite flags reflect persisted favorites state for each provider.

Change location and re-run search:
1. User edits the location field (e.g., ZIP or city/state) in the search-header-form or location control.
2. On submission, component calls setCurrentLocation(location_text) with the entered value.
3. If setCurrentLocation returns success, component updates the location URL parameter with resolved_location.display_name or a derived text and resets page to 1.
4. Page then calls searchProviders with the updated location_text and other filters from URL.
5. If setCurrentLocation fails, component displays message from the API and keeps existing location URL intact.

Pagination:
1. User reaches the bottom of the results list and uses pagination-controls to move to another page.
2. Component updates the page URL parameter (e.g., from 1 to 2), preserving all filter/sort/view parameters.
3. Page re-runs searchProviders with the new page value; page_size is kept consistent based on implementation.
4. results-list updates to show providers for the new page, and pagination-controls highlight the current page index.
interactions: Entering or editing search query in search-header-form updates the q URL parameter and triggers a new search via searchProviders.
Changing the location input triggers setCurrentLocation, then updates the location URL parameter and calls searchProviders.
Selecting a service type/category filter (e.g., smartphone repair, laptop data recovery) updates serviceCode and/or categoryId URL parameters and refreshes results.
Adjusting distance, rating, and price cap controls updates distanceMax, ratingMin, and priceMax URL parameters and re-runs searchProviders.
Toggling visit type options (in-store, home visit, pickup & delivery, free pickup) updates visitInStore, visitHome, visitPickup, freePickup URL parameters and refreshes results.
Toggling availability options (open Saturday, closeAfter, is247, emergency) updates corresponding URL parameters and refreshes results.
Toggling fee/benefit options (freeDiagnostic, noDiagnosticFee, noFixNoFee) updates URL parameters and refreshes results.
Changing sort order in sort-and-view-bar updates the sort URL parameter and refreshes results via searchProviders.
Clicking the list/map toggle updates the view URL parameter and switches between results-list and map-panel using the same searchProviders data.
Clicking a provider name or card area navigates to provider_detail.html with providerId, currentlyFocusedServiceCode, and from=search as URL parameters.
Clicking the favorite icon on a provider invokes addProviderToFavorites or removeProviderFromFavorites and updates the is_favorite state in the UI.
Toggling the "Add to Compare" control on a provider invokes addProviderToCompare or removeProviderFromCompare and updates compare selection state and compare_selection_count.
Clicking the "Compare (N)" button in compare-bar navigates to compare.html.
Clicking "Clear all" in compare-bar calls clearCompareList and resets compare selection states on all visible providers.
Clicking filter pills in applied-filters-bar removes the corresponding URL parameter(s) and re-runs searchProviders.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is fully driven by URL query parameters and the responses from the assigned interfaces.

1. Initial load:
- On page load or when the URL changes, the page reads all recognized querystring parameters: q, location, serviceCode, categoryId, distanceMax, ratingMin, priceMax, visitInStore, visitHome, visitPickup, freePickup, openSaturday, closeAfter, is247, emergency, freeDiagnostic, noDiagnosticFee, noFixNoFee, sort, view, page.
- The page calls getSearchFilterOptions with: query = q, service_code = serviceCode, service_category_id = categoryId. The response defines available distance_options_miles, rating_thresholds, sort_options, service_type_filters, visit_type_filters, availability_filters, fee_and_benefit_filters, and price_filter_presets. These options are used to build the filter UI dynamically (no hardcoded values).
- The page constructs parameters for searchProviders:
  - query = q (string or empty),
  - location_text = location (string or empty),
  - distance_miles_max = distanceMax (number or undefined),
  - rating_min = ratingMin (number or undefined),
  - service_code = serviceCode (string or undefined),
  - service_category_id = categoryId (string or undefined),
  - price_filter derived from priceMax and/or selected price preset (if any),
  - visit_type_filters built from visitInStore, visitHome, visitPickup, freePickup,
  - availability_filters built from openSaturday, closeAfter, is247, emergency,
  - fee_filters built from freeDiagnostic, noDiagnosticFee, noFixNoFee,
  - sort_by = sort (string or default),
  - view_mode = view (list or map or default),
  - page = page (number, defaulting to 1 if missing or invalid),
  - page_size determined by implementation (not hardcoded in this spec).
- searchProviders returns providers, total_results, page, page_size, applied_filters_summary, map_pins, and compare_selection_count. These values are bound to the respective components.

2. URL-driven filters and sort:
- Every filter or sort control change updates the corresponding URL parameter(s). The page does not use localStorage; the URL is the single source of truth for search state.
- After updating URL parameters (typically via history.pushState or a full reload), the page repeats the initial load logic: re-read URL, call getSearchFilterOptions as needed, then call searchProviders.
- If a filter is cleared, the related URL parameter is removed rather than set to a special value, so the absence of the parameter means 'no filter'.

3. View mode state:
- The view parameter controls whether the page renders list or map as the primary results representation.
- When view=list, results-list is shown and map-panel is either hidden or minimized; when view=map, map-panel is primary, and list may be hidden or secondary.
- Toggling view only changes the view parameter; all other parameters (filters, sort, page) remain unchanged.

4. Pagination state:
- The page parameter holds the current page index (1-based).
- Changing filters, sort, q, location, serviceCode, or categoryId automatically resets page to 1 before re-running searchProviders.
- Pagination controls only modify the page parameter and re-call searchProviders with the updated page.

5. Focused service price state:
- The priceMax URL parameter and serviceCode jointly determine the price_filter passed to searchProviders.
- If the user selects a price preset or enters a custom cap, the component updates priceMax accordingly, and the page recomputes price_filter when calling searchProviders.

6. Compare and favorites state:
- Favorites state is persisted via FavoriteProvider records using addProviderToFavorites and removeProviderFromFavorites. searchProviders returns is_favorite per provider to reflect this state. URL parameters do not store favorites.
- Compare state is persisted via CompareList using addProviderToCompare, removeProviderFromCompare, and clearCompareList. searchProviders returns compare_selection_count but not the full list of IDs. The page uses the API responses from compare operations to manage local selection indicators. URL parameters do not store compare selections; the backend CompareList is the source of truth.

7. Location state:
- The location URL parameter is the text sent as location_text to searchProviders.
- setCurrentLocation converts user-entered text into a resolved_location. On success, the component updates the location URL param (using resolved_location.display_name or equivalent) and refreshes the search. The resolved latitude/longitude are managed internally by setCurrentLocation and searchProviders; they are not exposed as URL params on this page.

By relying exclusively on URL parameters and API responses for state, the page ensures that reloading or sharing the URL reproduces the same search results, filters, and view mode (excluding compare selection and favorites, which are bound to backend state).

components.functionality: Accepts free-text search query (q) and location (location) from the user, optionally reflecting current serviceCode/categoryId context, and updates URL parameters before triggering a new search.
event_handlers: onInit: Populate form inputs from q, location, serviceCode, categoryId URL parameters.
onChangeQuery: Update internal query field; no API call until submit.
onSubmit: Update URL parameters q and location (and optionally reset page to 1), then trigger the page-level reload logic which calls getSearchFilterOptions and searchProviders.
onClearQuery: Remove q from URL parameters and re-run search.
onClearLocation: Remove location from URL parameters and re-run search.

components.functionality: Displays dynamic filter controls for distance, rating, price caps, service types, visit types, availability, and fees/benefits based on getSearchFilterOptions, and maps user selections to URL parameters for searchProviders.
data_binding: URL parameters: distanceMax, ratingMin, priceMax, serviceCode, categoryId, visitInStore, visitHome, visitPickup, freePickup, openSaturday, closeAfter, is247, emergency, freeDiagnostic, noDiagnosticFee, noFixNoFee
event_handlers: onInit: Call getSearchFilterOptions with q, serviceCode, categoryId and render filter controls based on returned options; initialize control states from current URL parameters.
onChangeDistance: Map selected distance option to distanceMax URL parameter, reset page to 1, update URL, then trigger searchProviders.
onChangeRating: Map selected rating threshold to ratingMin URL parameter, reset page to 1, update URL, then trigger searchProviders.
onChangePriceCap: Map selected or entered price cap to priceMax URL parameter, reset page to 1, update URL, then trigger searchProviders.
onChangeServiceType: Map selection to serviceCode (and potentially categoryId) URL parameters, reset page to 1, update URL, then trigger searchProviders.
onToggleVisitInStore: Toggle visitInStore URL parameter and re-run search with page reset to 1.
onToggleVisitHome: Toggle visitHome URL parameter and re-run search with page reset to 1.
onToggleVisitPickup: Toggle visitPickup URL parameter and re-run search with page reset to 1.
onToggleFreePickup: Toggle freePickup URL parameter and re-run search with page reset to 1.
onToggleOpenSaturday: Toggle openSaturday URL parameter and re-run search.
onChangeCloseAfterTime: Map time selection to closeAfter URL parameter and re-run search.
onToggle247: Toggle is247 URL parameter and re-run search.
onToggleEmergency: Toggle emergency URL parameter and re-run search.
onToggleFreeDiagnostic: Toggle freeDiagnostic URL parameter and re-run search.
onToggleNoDiagnosticFee: Toggle noDiagnosticFee URL parameter and re-run search.
onToggleNoFixNoFee: Toggle noFixNoFee URL parameter and re-run search.
onClearAllFilters: Remove all filter-related URL parameters and reset page to 1, then trigger searchProviders.

components.functionality: Provides sort selection, list/map view toggle, and displays total results and basic applied filter summary.
event_handlers: onInit: Initialize sort dropdown from getSearchFilterOptions.sort_options and set its selected value from sort URL parameter; set view toggle from view URL parameter.
onChangeSort: Update sort URL parameter, reset page to 1, and trigger searchProviders.
onToggleViewToList: Set view URL parameter to list and trigger searchProviders with view_mode = list.
onToggleViewToMap: Set view URL parameter to map and trigger searchProviders with view_mode = map.

components.functionality: Shows removable filter pills representing currently active filters derived from URL parameters and applied_filters_summary.
data_binding: URL parameters: distanceMax, ratingMin, priceMax, serviceCode, categoryId, visitInStore, visitHome, visitPickup, freePickup, openSaturday, closeAfter, is247, emergency, freeDiagnostic, noDiagnosticFee, noFixNoFee
event_handlers: onInit: Generate filter pills based on current URL parameters and applied_filters_summary.
onRemoveFilterPill: Identify which URL parameter(s) the pill represents, remove those parameters, reset page to 1, and trigger searchProviders.
onClearAllPills: Remove all filter-related URL parameters, reset page to 1, and trigger searchProviders.

components.functionality: Displays a paginated list of providers with key details, each as a provider-card, when view=list.
event_handlers: onInit: Render provider-card components for each entry in searchProviders.providers when view is list.
onUpdateData: Re-render the list whenever searchProviders response or view URL parameter changes.
onProviderCardClick: Delegate to provider-card onClick to navigate to provider_detail.html.

components.functionality: Represents a single provider in the list view, showing name, rating, distance, matched services, price range for the focused service, favorite toggle, and compare toggle, with navigation to the provider detail page.
event_handlers: onClickName: Same as onClickCard, navigate to provider_detail.html with providerId, serviceCode, from=search.

onToggleFavorite: If is_favorite is false, call addProviderToFavorites(provider.id); if true, call removeProviderFromFavorites(provider.id). On success, update the card's favorite state and optionally refresh this provider's data if searchProviders is re-invoked.
onToggleCompareOn: Call addProviderToCompare(provider.id); on success, update local compare-selected state for this provider and update compare-bar using returned compare_list and providers.
onToggleCompareOff: Call removeProviderFromCompare(provider.id); on success, update local compare-selected state and compare-bar.
onViewDetailsAction: Explicit button that triggers the same navigation as onClickCard if provided as a separate control.

components.functionality: Displays providers as map pins using map_pins from searchProviders when view=map, and allows selection of a provider to show a map-pin-popup with actions.
event_handlers: onInit: When view is map, initialize map with center and bounds derived from searchProviders.map_pins and/or applied_filters_summary.location_display_name.
onUpdateData: Re-render pins whenever searchProviders.map_pins or view URL parameter changes.
onClickPin: Identify the pin's providerId, find the corresponding provider data from searchProviders.providers (if available), and open map-pin-popup for that provider.

onMapMoveOrZoom: Optionally trigger a new searchProviders call with the same URL parameters; only if the application chooses to link map bounds to search (the URL remains the single source of filter state).

components.functionality: Shows a compact card for a provider selected from a map pin, including basic info, favorite toggle, compare toggle, and navigation to provider_detail.html.
data_binding: Provider (from searchProviders.providers or derived from the clicked pin)
event_handlers: onOpen: Populate popup with provider details associated with the selected pin.
onToggleFavorite: Call addProviderToFavorites or removeProviderFromFavorites for this provider and update popup UI.
onToggleCompareOn: Call addProviderToCompare(provider.id) and update popup compare state and compare-bar.
onToggleCompareOff: Call removeProviderFromCompare(provider.id) and update popup compare state and compare-bar.
onClose: Hide popup without altering URL parameters.

components.functionality: Provides controls to move between pages of results by manipulating the page URL parameter.
event_handlers: onInit: Compute total pages from total_results and page_size and highlight the current page from searchProviders.page.
onClickPageNumber: Update page URL parameter to the selected page index and trigger searchProviders.
onClickNext: Increment page URL parameter (up to max page) and trigger searchProviders.
onClickPrev: Decrement page URL parameter (down to 1) and trigger searchProviders.

components.functionality: Persistent bar showing the number of providers selected for comparison and providing actions to open compare.html or clear the comparison list.
event_handlers: onInit: Initialize displayed comparison count using searchProviders.compare_selection_count and any existing CompareList from previous API calls.
onCompareClick: If the comparison count indicates at least the minimum required providers, navigate to compare.html (selection is retrieved server-side via CompareList).
onClearAllClick: Call clearCompareList; on success, reset local compare states on all provider-card and map-pin-popup instances and update displayed comparison count.

components.functionality: Allows users to explicitly change the search location, calls setCurrentLocation, and updates the location URL parameter before re-running search.
data_binding: setCurrentLocation.resolved_location (result of the last call when changing location)
event_handlers: onInit: Populate the location input with the current location URL parameter.
onChangeInput: Update local location text value.

onSubmitLocation: Call setCurrentLocation with the entered location_text. If success is true, update the location URL parameter using the resolved_location.display_name or equivalent, reset page to 1, and trigger searchProviders. If success is false, show message and keep URL unchanged.
onClearLocation: Clear the location URL parameter and re-run search with no explicit location_text.

components.functionality: Small indicator associated with provider-card and map-pin-popup to reflect and update favorite status using addProviderToFavorites and removeProviderFromFavorites.
data_binding: favorites_count (from favorites APIs for analytical display if needed)
event_handlers: onClickFavoriteOnCard: Triggered from provider-card; calls addProviderToFavorites(provider.id) or removeProviderFromFavorites(provider.id) and updates the specific provider's favorite state.
onClickFavoriteOnPopup: Same as onClickFavoriteOnCard but from map-pin-popup context.

components.functionality: Orchestrates reading URL parameters, calling getSearchFilterOptions and searchProviders, and distributing data to child components. Ensures the page reacts to URL changes instead of using localStorage.
data_binding: URL parameters: q, location, serviceCode, categoryId, distanceMax, ratingMin, priceMax, visitInStore, visitHome, visitPickup, freePickup, openSaturday, closeAfter, is247, emergency, freeDiagnostic, noDiagnosticFee, noFixNoFee, sort, view, page
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Read all URL parameters, call getSearchFilterOptions (with q, serviceCode, categoryId) then call searchProviders with parameters derived from URL. Pass responses to filter-sidebar, sort-and-view-bar, results-list, map-panel, pagination-controls, applied-filters-bar, compare-bar, and location-control.
onUrlChange: When the URL querystring changes (via navigation or programmatic updates), repeat the onPageLoad sequence to keep the UI in sync with URL parameters.
onFilterOrSortChange: Central handler invoked by child components (through a routing mechanism) to update URL parameters; after updating, triggers onUrlChange logic.
onViewModeToggle: Update view parameter and call onUrlChange logic.
onPageChange: Update page parameter and call onUrlChange logic.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px). Nav, headline, search bar, and category pills all appear horizontally aligned to this central grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a full-width, hero-like header band at the top of the Search Results page that visually matches the homepage hero: a map-of-Romania background with a dark gradient overlay. Inside this band, center the search-header-form within the same 1100–1200px max-width grid, stacked vertically with a concise page title/context if needed. Below this hero band, switch into a two-column results layout: a left filter-sidebar and a right main results area. The right column stacks, from top to bottom: sort-and-view-bar, applied-filters-bar, results-list (or map-panel depending on view), and pagination-controls at the bottom. A compare-bar floats at the lower edge of the viewport. Inline elements (location-control and favorites-indicator-inline) live inside their parent components rather than as standalone blocks.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a full-width, hero-like header band at the top of the Search Results page that visually matches the homepage hero: a map-of-Romania background with a dark gradient overlay. Inside this band, center the search-header-form within the same 1100–1200px max-width grid, stacked vertically with a concise page title/context if needed. Below this hero band, switch into a two-column results layout: a left filter-sidebar and a right main results area. The right column stacks, from top to bottom: sort-and-view-bar, applied-filters-bar, results-list (or map-panel depending on view), and pagination-controls at the bottom. A compare-bar floats at the lower edge of the viewport. Inline elements (location-control and favorites-indicator-inline) live inside their parent components rather than as standalone blocks.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Top hero band with the search-header-form occupies roughly the top 25–30% of the viewport height on desktop, echoing the substantial hero of the mockup but slightly compressed to prioritize results. The content container below takes the remaining 70–75% of the viewport. Inside this container, allocate about 3/12 of the width to the filter-sidebar on the left and 9/12 to the main results column on the right. Within the results column, reserve a shallow band (~80–96px) for the sort-and-view-bar plus applied-filters-bar, then allow the results-list or map-panel to fill most of the remaining height. The compare-bar is a thin strip (~56–72px) pinned to the bottom edge of the viewport, spanning the same max-width container. Vertical gaps between major sections follow the 24–32px rhythm; outer section padding top/bottom follows 64px–80px.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately spacious feel consistent with the hero mockup: generous breathing room around the search-header-form and toolbar, with clear separation between groups of controls. The search-header-form uses chunky, high-contrast inputs and a standout CTA button similar to the hero search bar, with 16–24px internal padding. The filter-sidebar is denser than the hero but organized into clear, padded filter groups separated by 24px vertical spacing. The results-list uses card-based provider-card elements with 16–24px internal padding and 16–24px gaps between cards, avoiding visual clutter while accommodating multiple entries per viewport. The map-panel and map-pin-popup remain visually clean, with minimal chrome and clear focus on the map and pins.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Reading flow mirrors the mockup: the eye starts at the top-left logo and nav (global, implied), then immediately drops into the dark hero band where the bright search-header-form is the primary focal point. From there, the user’s attention moves straight down into the sort-and-view-bar, which introduces controls for view (list vs map) and sorting. Next, the eye naturally scans slightly left to notice the filter-sidebar and then right/down through the results-list or across the map-panel, moving card by card or pin by pin. High-contrast blues highlight interactive elements: the primary search button, active sort/view toggles, selected filters, compare states, and favorites indicators. The compare-bar at the bottom is a subtle but persistent call to action that becomes visually prominent only when items are selected.

layout.overall_description: The Search Results page reads as an extension of the homepage hero: a fixed top navigation sits above a full-width header band with a dark, gradient-overlay map of Romania stretching edge to edge. Centered within this band, on a 1100–1200px max-width grid, the search-header-form feels like a compact hero search module, horizontally spanning about 70–80% of the content width and floating above the background with a soft shadow and rounded corners. Immediately beneath this hero band, the page transitions into a two-column layout on a white background. The left third of the grid holds a tall, slightly elevated filter-sidebar with stacked filter groups, while the right two-thirds form the main results area. At the top of this right column sits a crisp sort-and-view-bar that aligns with the search header above, followed by a slim applied-filters-bar showing active chips. Below, the space is dominated either by a vertical list of generous provider-card components or by a map-panel, depending on list/map view. Pagination-controls sit at the bottom of the results column, aligning with the grid. A compare-bar hugs the bottom of the viewport, spanning the same central width and appearing as a subtle, semi-floating pill that becomes more visually assertive as providers are selected. Inline, the location-control appears both in the top search header and optionally in the toolbar, while favorites-indicator-inline icons sit inside each provider-card. The overall effect is modern, balanced, and conversion-focused, guiding users smoothly from refining their search to choosing and comparing providers.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Placed in a full-width header band immediately below the fixed site navigation, the search-header-form sits centered on the page within a max-width container of about 1100–1200px. Vertically, it is located in the middle of a 25–30% viewport-height header area that uses the dark gradient-over-map background, visually echoing the homepage hero. The form itself appears as a wide, pill-like card spanning roughly 70–80% of the container width (about 8–10 of 12 columns), horizontally centered. Internally, fields are arranged in a single horizontal row: on the left, a category/service input with a dropdown icon; in the middle, the location-control; and on the right, a bold blue search button occupying about 20–25% of the form width. Each input has 12–16px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding, with 8–16px gaps between fields. The entire search-header-form sits on a subtle elevation (shadow and slightly lighter background) to distinguish it from the darker hero backdrop and is separated from the content below by about 32–48px of whitespace before the start of the two-column results area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero band; visually anchored by the underlying full-width background. On scroll, it moves away with the header band (no sticky behavior by default), letting the results area take over the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-sidebar begins directly below the hero header band, aligned to the left edge of the central content container. It occupies roughly 3 of 12 columns (about 25–30% of the content width) and extends vertically from just under the sort-and-view-bar’s horizontal line down toward the bottom of the page, often filling most of the viewport height on initial load. The sidebar is contained within a light card-like panel with a subtle shadow and 16–24px internal padding on all sides, separated from the main results column on the right by a 24–32px horizontal gap. Inside, filters are grouped into clearly labeled sections (e.g., service type, price range, rating, turnaround time), each section separated by 24px vertical spacing. Individual filter controls (checkboxes, sliders, toggles) follow the 8px spacing grid. A reset/clear button sits near the top or bottom, aligned to the right within the panel. The entire sidebar sits on a plain white or slightly tinted surface to contrast with the darker hero above and visually anchor the left side of the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the viewport on desktop: once the user scrolls past the hero, the filter-sidebar remains pinned to the top offset of the visible area (just below the global nav) until its content is exhausted, allowing filters to remain accessible while scrolling through the results on the right. On smaller viewports, this behavior can convert to a collapsible panel or drawer, but desktop behavior is anchored and sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-and-view-bar sits at the top of the main results column, directly below the bottom edge of the hero header band and horizontally aligned to the same central max-width container. It spans the entire width of the right column (about 9 of 12 grid columns) and is horizontally aligned flush with the right edge of the filter-sidebar plus the 24–32px gap. Vertically, it is a shallow bar, about 56–72px tall, with 16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding inside. On the left side of the bar, there is a succinct description of the results (e.g., '24 repair services near Cluj') and perhaps a smaller label. On the right, clustered controls include a sort dropdown (e.g., 'Sort by: Rating'), view toggles (list vs map icons) styled as pill buttons, and possibly a compact location-control shortcut. Elements inside the bar are arranged on a single horizontal baseline, with 8–16px gaps. The bar’s background is solid white or very light gray to cleanly separate it from both the dark hero above and the content below. A subtle bottom border or shadow visualizes it as a toolbar band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky relative to the scrolling results: after scrolling past the hero, the sort-and-view-bar can remain fixed at the top of the main results column (just under the global nav), providing constant access to sort and view switches while the list or map content scrolls beneath it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The applied-filters-bar is positioned immediately below the sort-and-view-bar, sharing the same width as the main results column and aligning to the same left and right edges. It is a compact horizontal strip, approximately 40–56px tall, with a light background (white or very pale blue/gray) and minimal vertical padding (8–12px). Within the bar, applied filter chips are arranged from left to right in a single line or wrapping onto a second line if necessary, maintaining 8px gaps between chips. Each chip is pill-shaped with soft borders and a small 'x' to remove the filter. On the far right, a 'Clear all' action is aligned horizontally with the chips. The bar itself is separated from the list below by 16–24px of vertical space, keeping it visually distinct but closely tied to the results it influences.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main results column, scrolling away with the results. It does not stick independently; instead, its visibility is maintained indirectly if the sort-and-view-bar above is sticky, keeping filter context near the top of the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results-list occupies the majority of the right-hand main content area beneath the applied-filters-bar when the user is in list view. It spans the full width of the right column (approximately 9 of 12 columns) and begins about 16–24px below the applied-filters-bar. Vertically, it extends downwards, filling all remaining space until the pagination-controls at the bottom, and typically continues beyond the initial viewport height, making scrolling natural. Inside, provider-card components are stacked vertically in a single column, each card separated by 16–24px vertical spacing. The list sits on a white background with generous left and right padding aligning to the main container; there is no additional framing around the entire list beyond the main page background, allowing individual cards to be the primary visual units. When switched to map view, the results-list either collapses or is replaced in this area by the map-panel, while the surrounding layout remains consistent.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls vertically as the main content of the page. Its vertical start point is fixed below the applied-filters-bar, but the list itself is free-flowing, extending as long as there are results. In map-only mode, this region’s behavior changes to host the map-panel instead.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each provider-card is a horizontally oriented card within the results-list, spanning nearly the full width of the main results column (minus standard inner padding of 16–24px). The card height is auto but typically around 160–220px, depending on content. The card surface is white with a subtle shadow and 16–24px internal padding, and rounded corners consistent with the search bar and hero elements. Content is arranged left-to-right: on the far left, a logo or thumbnail image and provider name sit near the top; below the name, key metadata (category, city, distance) are displayed in a compact row. To the right of the name, rating and review count appear with star icons, and below that, service tags or badges may appear as small pills. On the far right of the card, aligned vertically, are primary actions (e.g., 'View details', 'Call') plus a favorites-indicator-inline icon near the top-right corner of the card. Compare checkboxes or toggles sit near the bottom-right or in the main action area. Internal vertical spacing follows a 8–16px rhythm, ensuring the card feels information-rich but not crowded.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked within the results-list. Each card is fully clickable in certain areas (e.g., title thumbnail) but remains within the flow of the list. On hover, a slight elevation and shadow increase its prominence without changing its layout footprint.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The map-panel shares the same horizontal footprint as the results-list, occupying the full width of the main results column beneath the sort-and-view-bar and applied-filters-bar when map view is active. Vertically, it fills the remaining viewport height from its top edge down to above the compare-bar, minus standard layout padding, giving a strong, immersive map experience. The map canvas itself is edge-to-edge within this region, with a slim top overlay for map controls (zoom, current location, map type) and possibly a compact inline legend or clustering indicator at the bottom-left. When used in a split-screen configuration (optional), the map-panel could take up the right half of the main content area while a condensed results-list occupies the left half; in that case, it would span approximately 4–5 of the 9 columns, from the middle of the page to the right edge, and extend vertically parallel to the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within its allocated region but visually dominant when active. The map-panel scrolls only if larger than the viewport; otherwise, it remains in view while the page content above (hero, toolbar) scrolls out. Map-specific overlays like map-pin-popup appear positioned above this panel in a z-layer but do not alter layout dimensions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The map-pin-popup appears as a floating card anchored to a specific pin location inside the map-panel. It does not occupy a fixed place in the global grid; instead, it is positioned relative to the pin, typically slightly above and to the right so the pin tip remains visible. The popup is a small rectangular or pill-shaped card with rounded corners, roughly 260–320px wide, and auto height depending on content (usually 120–180px). It features 12–16px internal padding and a white background with a strong shadow to clearly separate it from the map. Inside, it shows a condensed view of provider information: name, rating, primary category, distance, and a 'View details' or 'Open in list' link, plus a favorites-indicator-inline icon. The popup never crosses beyond the visible map viewport; when close to the edges, its anchoring adjusts to keep the card fully visible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Floating overlay within the map-panel’s coordinate space, following the pin as the user pans or zooms the map. It does not affect the layout of other components and disappears or changes anchor when another pin is selected.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-controls are placed at the bottom of the main results column, directly below the last provider-card in the results-list. They span roughly the width of the list content (aligned with card edges, not necessarily full-bleed to the column) and sit with 24–32px vertical spacing above and below, visually anchoring the end of the current page of results. The controls are arranged horizontally and centered within the column: a left arrow, numbered pages, ellipsis for overflow, and a right arrow, all with 8–12px horizontal gaps. A compact label such as 'Page 1 of 5' may sit to the left or above the numbers. The background is the same as the page body (white), with only the button treatments (bordered or filled blue for the current page) creating emphasis. When a map-only view is active (no stacked list), these controls either hide or dock to the bottom of any visible list section if present.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the flow of the results-list. As the user scrolls new pages, the controls move with the content and appear again after each page of results. No sticky behavior by default so as not to compete with the compare-bar at the bottom.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The compare-bar is a horizontally oriented strip that hugs the bottom of the viewport, aligned to the central max-width container so that its left and right edges match the main content column. It is about 56–72px tall with 12–16px vertical padding, and it spans nearly the full width of the content (10–12 columns), stopping short of the window edges on large screens to maintain the centered layout. By default (when no items selected), it appears either hidden or as a very subtle, semi-transparent handle. Once one or more provider-card comparison toggles are enabled, the compare-bar slides up into view, with a solid white or dark surface and a slight shadow, clearly floating above the page content. Inside, selected providers are represented as small, pill-like tokens or thumbnails aligned from left to right, with an 'x' icon to remove them. On the right side, a prominent 'Compare' button in the brand blue anchors the bar and acts as the primary action. This bar overlaps the lower edge of the results-list or map-panel visually but leaves enough bottom margin in those components so content is not obscured.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the bottom of the viewport (sticky footer) within the horizontal constraints of the main content container. It animates in/out based on comparison selection state and stays visible while the user scrolls through results, ensuring quick access to the compare action.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The location-control is embedded within other components rather than free-floating. Its primary placement is inside the search-header-form: occupying the central portion of the form’s horizontal layout, it appears as a text input with a location pin icon on the left and possibly a 'Use current location' icon or button on the right. It takes up roughly 30–40% of the form’s width, matching the height of the other input fields. A secondary, more compact version may appear on the right side of the sort-and-view-bar as a small pill or dropdown showing the current city or radius, aligned on the same baseline as the sort dropdown and view toggles. In all cases, it respects the base spacing system (8–12px internal padding, 8–16px gaps to adjacent controls) and visually aligns with the grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within its parent containers, flowing horizontally with other controls. It does not have independent sticky or floating behavior, but when inside sticky components (like the sort-and-view-bar), it effectively remains visible as part of them.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites-indicator-inline appears as a small icon button (e.g., outlined heart that fills when active) embedded in both the provider-card and map-pin-popup. In provider-card, it is positioned near the top-right corner inside the card’s padding, aligned horizontally with the provider name or rating row, offset by about 8px from the card’s right edge and 8–12px from the top edge. In the map-pin-popup, it sits in the top-right corner of the popup card, aligned with the provider name on the left. The icon size is modest (16–20px), with a clear interactive affordance (hover state, blue when active). It does not disrupt the overall layout of text and actions but remains visually noticeable for quick tapping or clicking.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element within its host components. It does not affect overall layout structure, only local alignment within cards and popups. It scrolls with the results-list or moves as part of the map-pin-popup when the map pans.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-results-controller is a conceptual layer that surfaces as a combination of controls across the top of the results area rather than a separate visible block. Its main visible elements are the sort-and-view-bar, applied-filters-bar, and any quick filter or toggle controls tied to state (e.g., 'Open now', 'Top rated'). If surfaced explicitly, it could appear as a thin, subtle strip just above or integrated into the sort-and-view-bar, aligned to the same grid, with small text labels like 'Showing results for' and dynamic values (query, location). This strip would span the full width of the main results column and sit about 8–16px above the sort-and-view-bar, using small typography and minimal height so as not to clutter the layout. More typically, its functions are distributed so that the user perceives a cohesive control area at the top of the results rather than a separate component.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Logically sticky via the components it governs: because it is manifested through the sort-and-view-bar and applied-filters-bar (which may be sticky), the controller’s influence remains available as users scroll. It does not have its own independent frame or floating behavior beyond its host elements.

name: Provider Detail
title: Provider Detail
description: Shows detailed information about a repair service provider and allows users to book repairs, request quotes, schedule home visits or pickups, contact the provider, and manage reviews, favorites, and comparison.

core_features: Load and display provider details (contact information, address, service coverage, opening hours, key policies) using getProviderDetail with providerId from the URL.
Display a structured list of provider services with indicative pricing, warranty information, and availability options (in-store, home visit, pickup/delivery) from getProviderDetail.services.

Show key decision attributes (ratings, review counts, warranty duration, diagnostic fee and no fix-no fee policies) from getProviderDetail.review_summary, max_warranty_months, and diagnostic_fee_policy.
Allow users to book in-store (or virtual) repair appointments for specific services by submitting appointment details via createAppointmentBooking.
Allow users to request price estimates for repairs by submitting device and issue information via createQuoteRequest, using getBrands for brand dropdowns.
Allow users to request home/on-site visits for appliance repairs by submitting address, time preference, and issue via createHomeVisitRequest.
Allow users to schedule pickup and delivery repair services by submitting device, issue, address, and time window via createPickupRequest, using getBrands when needed.
Provide a general contact form to send messages or questions to the provider via sendProviderMessage, prefilling contact details with getProfile where available.
Provide a ‘Call’ action that initiates a phone call on supported devices using the provider.phone value from getProviderDetail.
Allow users to save or unsave the provider as a favorite using addProviderToFavorites and removeProviderFromFavorites, and navigate to favorites.html with highlightProviderId when desired.
Allow users to add or remove the provider from a comparison list using addProviderToCompare and removeProviderFromCompare, and navigate to compare.html.
Display a review summary (average rating, count, breakdown) using getProviderDetail.review_summary and a paginated list of reviews using getProviderReviews.
Provide a review submission form where users can rate the provider, write a review, choose visit type and service, and submit via submitProviderReview.
Support pre-focusing on a specific service section using the serviceCode URL parameter to highlight or scroll to relevant services and related forms.
Show navigation context (e.g., back to search, favorites, or compare) based on the from URL parameter.
user_workflows:
View provider details:
1. User opens provider_detail.html with a providerId URL parameter.
2. On page initialization, the page calls getProviderDetail(providerId).
3. The provider header and key information components display provider.name, address, phone, website_url, opening_hours, and key policies.
4. The service list component renders the services returned in getProviderDetail.services.
5. The rating summary component displays review_summary and provider.rating_average/provider.rating_count.
6. If a serviceCode URL parameter is present, the service list focuses or scrolls to the matching service_code and marks it as selected.
7. The back navigation component reads the from parameter (if present) to configure a contextual 'Back to results' or similar navigation link.

Toggle favorite status and navigate to favorites:
1. After provider detail is loaded, the page reads is_favorite from getProviderDetail.
2. The favorites toggle component shows the current state (saved or not) based on is_favorite.
3. When the user clicks the favorites toggle:
   - If the provider is not currently a favorite, the component calls addProviderToFavorites(providerId).
   - If the provider is currently a favorite, the component calls removeProviderFromFavorites(providerId).
4. On success, the component updates its local favorite state and may show the updated favorites_count from the response.
5. If the provider is successfully added to favorites, the user can click 'View all favorites', which navigates to favorites.html with highlightProviderId set to the current providerId in the querystring.

Add/remove provider from comparison and navigate to comparison list:
1. When provider details are loaded, the user sees a comparison toggle/button.
2. When the user turns on comparison for this provider, the page calls addProviderToCompare(providerId).
3. On success, the component updates its state to reflect that the provider is in the compare_list and may show how many providers are currently in comparison using compare_list.providerIds.length.
4. When the user turns off comparison, the page calls removeProviderFromCompare(providerId) and updates the state based on the returned compare_list.
5. The user can click 'View comparison list' to navigate to compare.html without additional parameters (the compare page resolves the current comparison list).

Book an in-store appointment:
1. User scrolls to or selects a specific service in the service list (optionally preselected by serviceCode URL parameter).
2. The booking form component displays fields for appointment_datetime, device_type, device_brand, device_model, issue_description, notes, and customer contact information.
3. The form can prefill customer_name, customer_phone, and customer_email using getProfile if available.
4. The user selects or confirms booking_type (e.g., in_store) and fills in required fields.
5. On form submission, the component constructs a booking object including providerId from the URL, optionally providerServiceId from the selected service, booking_type, appointment_datetime, device details, issue_description, notes, customer contact fields, and an initial status (e.g., pending).
6. The page calls createAppointmentBooking(booking).
