components.functionality: Displays a single favorite provider with summary information and exposes actions: view provider details, remove from favorites (with undo support via parent), and navigate to similar services search.
event_handlers: onClickCard(providerId): navigate to provider_detail.html with URL parameters providerId={provider.id} and from=favorites
onClickViewDetails(providerId): same as onClickCard; explicit button for clarity, using provider.id to construct the target URL
onClickRemoveFavorite(providerId): call removeProviderFromFavorites(providerId) and, on success, emit an event to favorites-list/favorites-page-controller to update the list and trigger undo-toast
onRemoveFavoriteSuccess(response): evaluate response.success and favorites_count to adjust any local indicators (e.g., disabled state) before notifying parent components
onRemoveFavoriteError(error): notify parent components to display an error message without making assumptions about error content

onClickFindSimilar(provider): derive a search query term from bound Provider data (e.g., provider.name or another available dynamic label) and navigate to search_results.html?q={derivedQuery}&location={provider.postal_code}&view=list

components.functionality: Displays guidance when there are no favorites returned by getFavoriteProviders, explaining that the user can add favorites from provider detail or comparison pages.
event_handlers: onFavoritesDataChange(favorites): if favorites array is empty, show the empty-state; otherwise hide it

components.functionality: Manages and displays loading and error states around the favorites list, ensuring the user receives feedback while getFavoriteProviders, removeProviderFromFavorites, or addProviderToFavorites are in progress or when they fail.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: show loading indicator while getFavoriteProviders is in progress
onGetFavoriteProvidersSuccess: hide loading indicator and any previous error messages
onGetFavoriteProvidersError(error): hide loading indicator and show a generic error message, allowing retry via a reload or dedicated retry control
onRemoveFavoriteStart(providerId): optionally show a subtle progress state for the affected card
onRemoveFavoriteComplete(providerId): clear the per-card progress state
onAddFavoriteStart(providerId): set a progress state for undo actions
onAddFavoriteComplete(providerId): clear the undo-related progress state
onActionError(error): show a non-hardcoded, generic error notification and allow the user to try the action again

components.functionality: Appears after a provider is removed from favorites, offering the user an opportunity to undo the removal. Uses addProviderToFavorites to restore the provider if the user clicks undo.
event_handlers: onFavoriteRemoved(providerId, providerSnapshot): store the removed providerId and any necessary provider snapshot in local component state and display the notification with an 'Undo' action

onClickUndo(providerId): call addProviderToFavorites(providerId) and, on success, emit an event to favorites-page-controller/favorites-list so the favorite is reinserted into the list using the returned FavoriteProvider data
onAddProviderToFavoritesSuccess(response): if response.success is true, hide the notification and propagate favorite and updated favorites_count to parent
onAddProviderToFavoritesError(error): keep or hide the notification based on configuration and show an error indicator; do not silently fail
onTimeout: if the notification is not acted upon within a configured duration, automatically dismiss it without calling addProviderToFavorites

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px). Nav, headline, search bar, and category pills all appear horizontally aligned to this central grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom flow as the hero mockup: fixed global navigation at the very top (outside this spec), then a visually strong header band that plays the role of the hero (favorites-header-summary as the headline), directly followed by a prominent control bar (favorites-page-controller, analogous to the search module), and finally the main content area (favorites-list or empty/loading/error states). Within the list, favorite-provider-card items are stacked vertically in a single column, aligned to the same centered 12-column grid used in the hero design.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom flow as the hero mockup: fixed global navigation at the very top (outside this spec), then a visually strong header band that plays the role of the hero (favorites-header-summary as the headline), directly followed by a prominent control bar (favorites-page-controller, analogous to the search module), and finally the main content area (favorites-list or empty/loading/error states). Within the list, favorite-provider-card items are stacked vertically in a single column, aligned to the same centered 12-column grid used in the hero design.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header band (favorites-header-summary) occupies roughly the top 25–35% of the initial viewport height, with generous top padding below the global nav and enough vertical space for a large title and brief description. The favorites-page-controller sits immediately below, taking up about 10–15% of the initial viewport height as a compact but prominent horizontal bar. The remaining 50–60% (and all scrolling space beyond the fold) is dedicated to the favorites-list area, where cards or states (empty/loading/error) fill the width of the central content container. Horizontal space is constrained to a centered max-width of ~1100–1200px, while the background (subtle map/tech imagery with a dark gradient) stretches full-width behind the top segment of the page, fading into a more neutral background behind the scrollable list.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains a moderately spacious feel: the header summary uses a few short lines of text with large type and ample breathing room; the controller bar is visually compact but still generous in padding around controls (8–12px vertical, 16–24px horizontal). The list of favorite-provider-card items uses clear separation: each card has internal padding of 16–24px and vertical gaps of 16–24px between cards. Overall text density is low in the header, moderate in cards (enough details to be useful but not cluttered), and the states (empty/error/loading) are visually simple and centered to avoid noise.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the global nav logo in the top-left, moves across the nav items to the right, and then drops into the header band where a large, white page title and any highlighted numeric counts act as the primary focal point. From there, the flow moves down to the horizontally prominent favorites-page-controller, which is styled as a raised bar with high-contrast buttons and pill-like filters. The user’s gaze is then drawn directly into the vertical stack of favorite-provider-card entries; each card is scanned left-to-right (logo/avatar and name first, then meta info, then primary actions like 'View provider' and 'Remove'). Secondary attention points are the undo toast at the bottom center of the viewport and any highlighted metrics or badges within cards, but they do not compete with the main headline → controller → list path.

layout.overall_description: The Favorites page opens beneath the site’s fixed top navigation into a full-width header band that softly echoes the hero aesthetic from the homepage: a subtle dark gradient over a blurred map/tech background stretches edge to edge, while all actual content is contained in a centered, max-width (~1140px) grid. Near the top of this band, the favorites-header-summary anchors the page with a large, bold, white title on the left (e.g., “Your Favorites”) and concise supporting text and counts aligned on the right, both sitting with generous top and bottom padding so the area feels airy rather than crowded. Roughly 24–32px below this, a raised, pill-edged favorites-page-controller bar spans most of the content width, aligned horizontally and visually analogous to the homepage search bar: filters, sort controls, and view toggles sit side by side with balanced spacing and a primary blue control or button on the right, all against a white or very light surface with a subtle shadow that makes it float above the darker header background. As you scroll down, the background transitions to a cleaner, neutral tone while the favorites-list area extends vertically, stacking favorite-provider-card items from top to bottom, each full-width within the grid, with rounded white cards, soft shadows, and 16–24px gaps between them to maintain clarity. If there are no favorites, a centered favorites-empty-state occupies this same list region, sitting roughly in the middle of the viewport below the controller, with an illustration or icon, headline, explanatory copy, and a single strong CTA. During loading or error conditions, the favorites-loading-and-error-state replaces the list with either stacked skeleton cards or a concise, centered message, without shifting the header or controller. Whenever a user removes a favorite, a dark, pill-shaped favorites-undo-toast appears floating above all content, fixed near the bottom center of the viewport with enough margin from the edge to feel deliberate, allowing quick undo without disrupting the main vertical reading and interaction flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites-page-controller sits directly below the favorites-header-summary, aligned to the same centered content container and grid. On desktop it spans roughly 80–90% of the container width, leaving comfortable left and right margins so it doesn’t feel edge-to-edge. Vertically, it appears about 24–32px beneath the bottom of the header summary text block, creating a clear separation but still feeling part of the same hero region. Visually, it is a horizontal bar with rounded corners and a slight drop shadow, floating above the darker header background similar to the homepage’s search bar. Inside, controls are arranged left-to-right: on the left, a compact group of pill-shaped filters or view toggles; in the center, optional secondary controls (e.g., sort dropdown); on the right, a prominent primary action (like a blue button or toggle) aligned to the grid’s right edge. The bar’s height is around 56–64px including padding, with 8–12px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding around its inner controls. As the user scrolls past the hero band, the controller remains visually attached to the top of the content area, maintaining consistent horizontal alignment with the cards below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky: when the user scrolls down and the header band starts to move out of view, the favorites-page-controller sticks to the top of the viewport, just below the fixed global navigation, maintaining a constant horizontal position and width. It keeps its floating card appearance, ensuring quick access to filters and sorting while the list scrolls beneath it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites-header-summary occupies the upper portion of the main content area, visually acting like a hero header. It is anchored within the same centered content container (max-width ~1100–1200px) but sits over a full-width background that features a dark gradient overlay and subtle map/tech imagery, creating depth while maintaining readability. There is generous top padding of around 64–80px from the bottom of the global nav to the top of the summary content, and about 32–48px padding below the last line of summary text before the controller. The main page title is placed on the left, aligned to the left grid edge, spanning about 6–7 columns of the 12-column grid, with large, bold, white typography and optional blue-highlighted words to echo the original hero’s accent. To the right, occupying roughly 5–6 columns, sits supporting information: a concise line showing the number of saved providers, maybe a short description about what the favorites section does, and possibly a small, subtle icon. This right-side content is right-aligned or gently centered within its grid columns to create a balanced two-column header feel on larger screens. Overall height of this band is roughly 280–360px on desktop, ensuring the header feels substantial but not as tall as the main homepage hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scrollable content: it scrolls out of view as the user moves down the page, ceding prominence to the sticky favorites-page-controller and the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites-list occupies the main content region beneath the favorites-page-controller. It is constrained to the same centered max-width container and aligned with the 12-column grid. It begins approximately 24px below the bottom edge of the controller bar, so the transition between controls and the list feels tight and connected but not cramped. The list is a single vertical column of favorite-provider-card items that each span nearly the full width of the content container (roughly 10–12 columns wide depending on internal card margins), with consistent horizontal alignment from card to card. Vertically, cards are separated by 16–24px of whitespace, creating clear individual blocks and respecting the moderately spacious density. The list area extends well below the initial viewport and scrolls naturally; on longer lists, it forms a clean, repeating rhythm of white cards over a neutral page background. The list also acts as the canvas for empty, loading, and error states, with those states centered in the same horizontal alignment when they replace the cards.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls normally as part of the page’s main content. It fully respects the sticky behavior of the favorites-page-controller, sliding underneath it as the user scrolls while maintaining its own vertical rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each favorite-provider-card is a horizontally oriented, full-width card within the favorites-list, positioned in a vertical stack. The card’s outer width is nearly the full width of the container with 16–24px margin on the left and right if needed to maintain alignment and breathing room. Internally, it has 16–24px of padding on all sides, with a white or very light background, softly rounded corners, and a subtle shadow to float above the neutral page background, echoing the elevated search bar aesthetic. The content inside is arranged left-to-right: on the far left, occupying roughly 2–3 grid columns, a logo, service avatar, or small image thumbnail; in the central area (about 6–7 columns), the provider’s name in a strong, medium-large font, followed by one or two lines of key details (category, rating, city, response time) arranged in a compact vertical stack with 4–8px spacing. On the far right, spanning 2–3 columns, a vertical grouping of actions: a prominent primary button (e.g., “Open provider”) in a blue accent aligned at the top, and a more subtle icon/button for removing from favorites (e.g., a heart or trash icon) aligned either to the top-right or just below the main action with 8–12px spacing. The card height is flexible but typically falls in the 120–180px range depending on text length, with content vertically centered to look balanced. On hover, the card may slightly elevate and increase shadow intensity, reinforcing its clickability without overwhelming the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the list: cards flow as a regular vertical stack and expand or contract based on content. When a card is removed, the list smoothly collapses, and the undo-toast may appear over the bottom of the viewport without causing the cards to reflow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites-empty-state replaces the favorites-list content when there are no saved providers, occupying the same central area beneath the favorites-page-controller. Horizontally, it is centered within the max-width container and typically limited to about 60–70% of the container width, so it feels like a focused card rather than a full-bleed section. Vertically, it is placed with generous spacing: approximately 64–96px below the controller, and it is visually centered within the remaining viewport height if possible, giving a calm, open feel. The empty state usually consists of a light, rounded card or a clean, neutral background section with an illustration or icon at the top, a concise headline (e.g., “No favorites yet”) below it, a brief explanatory line, and one primary CTA button (e.g., “Browse repair providers”) centered beneath the text. Vertical spacing between these elements follows the 16–24px increments, maintaining the same semi-minimalist aesthetic. The overall effect is a single, clear focal point that draws attention without cluttering the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and mutually exclusive with the regular list: when active, it fully replaces the card stack within the list region while the header summary and page controller remain unchanged above.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites-loading-and-error-state occupies the same spatial footprint as the favorites-list, starting approximately 24px below the favorites-page-controller and centered within the max-width container. In loading mode, the area is filled with 3–6 vertically stacked skeleton placeholders that mimic the size and spacing of favorite-provider-card items, using grey blocks with rounded corners and 16–24px gaps between them, reinforcing that content is forthcoming while preserving the familiar rhythm. In error mode, the layout switches to a more compact, centered message block: a small icon or status indicator on the left and a short error message with an optional retry button on the right, or a compact vertical stack centered horizontally in the container. This block typically spans 40–60% of the container width with ~24–32px padding and sits about 64px below the controller so it doesn’t feel cramped. The header summary and controller above remain visible in both states, clearly separating persistent page context from transient loading or error conditions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the list area and mutually exclusive with the normal list and empty states. It scrolls like normal content if it exceeds the viewport height but generally aims to keep its key message within the first viewport beneath the controller.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites-undo-toast is a floating notification that appears over the main content when a favorite is removed. It is horizontally centered relative to the viewport (not just the content container) and positioned near the bottom, typically 24–32px above the bottom edge of the screen. The toast is a pill- or rounded-rectangle-shaped bar with a dark, semi-opaque background and light text, about 360–420px wide on desktop, with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding. Inside, the message text (“Removed from favorites”) appears on the left, and a high-contrast “Undo” action in blue is aligned on the right, separated by ~8–16px of internal space. The toast casts a subtle shadow to float clearly above the page and does not push any other content around; the favorites-list and cards remain fully scrollable underneath. It automatically dismisses after a short delay or when the user interacts elsewhere, staying present just long enough to be noticeable but not intrusive.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed relative to the viewport, layered above all other page content. It overlays the bottom portion of the screen without affecting the layout or scroll position of underlying elements and appears/disappears with a brief fade or slide animation.

name: Profile & Preferences
title: Profile & Preferences
description: Page for viewing, creating, and updating the single user profile and preferences used to prefill contact and request forms across the site.

core_features: Load the existing Profile record using getProfile on page initialization and populate the profile form fields.
Create or update the Profile record using saveProfile when the user submits the profile form.
Allow the user to enter and edit basic contact information: full_name, email, and phone.
Allow the user to set a preferred_contact_method (email, phone, either) that can be used by other flows when contacting providers.

Allow the user to define an optional preferred location via preferred_location_type (none, zip_code, city_state) and corresponding fields (preferred_zip or preferred_city and preferred_state) to be used as default search location elsewhere.
Allow the user to set communication_consent indicating whether they agree to be contacted by providers or the platform.
Handle navigation after saving the profile, including a main action to save and go to the home page (index.html) and optional behavior that respects an incoming redirect query parameter.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Open profile page from header link
1. User clicks the 'Create Account / Profile' link in the site header.
2. Browser navigates to profile.html without additional query parameters.
3. On load, the page calls getProfile.
4. If getProfile returns an existing Profile, the form fields are populated with the returned data.
5. If getProfile indicates no existing Profile, the form is shown with empty values (no prefilled data).
6. User edits or fills in contact information, preferred contact method, location preferences, and communication consent.
7. User clicks 'Save profile' (stay on page) or 'Save profile and go to home'.
8. The page validates the form client-side according to required/optional constraints for the Profile fields.
9. If validation passes, the page calls saveProfile with the current form data.
10. On success:
    - If the user selected 'Save profile and go to home', navigate to index.html.
    - If the user selected 'Save profile', remain on profile.html, show a success confirmation, and keep the form populated with the saved data.
11. On failure, show an error indication and allow the user to retry saving.

Workflow: Arrive from another page with redirect parameter (e.g., from provider_detail.html)
1. User initiates an action on another page (such as booking, quote request, or review) that requires profile information.
2. That page detects missing or incomplete profile data and navigates the user to profile.html with a redirect query parameter containing the original target path.
3. Browser loads profile.html?redirect=<some-encoded-path>.
4. On load, the page reads the redirect parameter value from the URL and stores it in page state.
5. The page calls getProfile and populates the form as in the previous workflow.
6. Optionally, a contextual notice is shown explaining that the user is updating profile information before returning to their previous task; this notice reads the redirect value for navigation purposes but does not hardcode any target URL.
7. User completes or updates the profile and clicks either:
    - 'Save profile and go to home' (always goes to index.html after save), or
    - A contextual action such as 'Save profile and continue' that uses the redirect parameter as the navigation target.
8. The page validates input and calls saveProfile with the form data.
9. On success:
    - If the user chose to continue their previous task and a redirect value is present, navigate the browser to the redirect path.
    - Otherwise, if the user chose to go home, navigate to index.html.
10. On failure, show an error message and keep the user on profile.html, preserving entered values.

Workflow: Update preferences later
1. User returns to profile.html at any time (e.g., from the header link) to update their profile.
2. profile.html loads, reads any redirect parameter if present, and calls getProfile.
3. Existing Profile data is shown in the form; user can adjust contact info, preferred contact method, preferred location, and consent.
4. User modifies the fields and clicks 'Save profile'.
5. Page validates the form and calls saveProfile with updated data.
6. On success, a non-disruptive success confirmation is shown; the user remains on profile.html and can navigate away using global navigation (e.g., header links).
7. All subsequent flows that use getProfile (booking, quote requests, home visit, pickup, reviews, provider messages) will receive the updated Profile values for prefilling their own forms.
interactions:
Interaction: Initial data loading
- Event: Page load (profile.html is requested).
- Action: Read the redirect query parameter from the URL if present and store it in local state.
- Action: Trigger getProfile to retrieve the current Profile record.
- Result: When getProfile resolves:
  - If a Profile object is returned, populate the form fields with the corresponding values.
  - If no profile data exists, keep all form fields empty, ready for first-time entry.
- Error handling: If getProfile fails, display a non-blocking error message and show an empty form so the user can still create a profile.

Interaction: Editing contact information
- Event: User types into the full_name, email, or phone fields.
- Action: Update the internal form state for the corresponding field.
- Validation: Optionally perform inline validation (e.g., basic format checks) without calling any interface.
- Result: The save buttons remain enabled as long as minimal requirements are met (if any are enforced client-side).

Interaction: Setting preferred contact method
- Event: User selects one of the preferred_contact_method options (email, phone, either).
- Action: Update the form state for preferred_contact_method.
- Result: This preference is included in the payload when saveProfile is called.
- Downstream usage: Other pages may use this value to decide which contact fields to emphasize or mark as preferred when prefilling forms via getProfile.

Interaction: Setting preferred location
- Event: User changes the preferred_location_type field to one of: none, zip_code, city_state.
- Action: Update the form state for preferred_location_type.
- Conditional behavior:
  - If set to none: clear or ignore preferred_zip, preferred_city, and preferred_state fields in the current form state.
  - If set to zip_code: show the preferred_zip input; hide or disable preferred_city and preferred_state; clear their values in internal state.
  - If set to city_state: show preferred_city and preferred_state inputs; hide or disable preferred_zip; clear its value in internal state.
- Result: Only the relevant location fields are populated and sent in the saveProfile payload, consistent with the selected preferred_location_type.

Interaction: Setting communication consent
- Event: User toggles the communication_consent checkbox (or equivalent control).
- Action: Update the form state for communication_consent to true or false.
- Result: consent state is included in the payload for saveProfile; other flows may later respect this value when deciding whether or how to initiate communication through providers.

Interaction: Submitting the form to save profile (stay on page)
- Event: User clicks the 'Save profile' button or triggers the form submission without requesting navigation.
- Action: Client-side validation runs over the current form state.
- If validation fails: display error messages and do not call saveProfile.
- If validation passes: construct a Profile object from the form state and call saveProfile(profile).
- While saveProfile is pending: disable save buttons and show a saving state indicator.
- On success: update the page’s current Profile state with the returned data, clear any previous error messages, re-enable controls, and show a success confirmation while remaining on profile.html.
- On failure: show an error, re-enable controls, and keep user input intact.

Interaction: Submitting the form to save profile and go to home
- Event: User clicks 'Save profile and go to home'.
- Action: Run the same validation as for 'Save profile'.
- If validation passes: call saveProfile(profile).
- On success: navigate the browser to index.html (no redirect parameter required).
- On failure: show errors and remain on profile.html without navigation.

Interaction: Submitting the form to save profile and continue previous task (when redirect is present)
- Event: If a redirect query parameter is present, user may click a button (e.g., 'Save profile and continue').
- Action: Validate the form.
- If validation passes: call saveProfile(profile).
- On success: navigate the browser to the path contained in the redirect parameter.
- On failure: show errors and remain on profile.html.

Interaction: Cancel or navigate away without saving
- Event: User navigates away via browser controls or global navigation.
- Action: No calls to saveProfile are made automatically; unsaved changes are discarded.
- Optional: The implementation may detect unsaved changes and warn the user before unloading the page; this does not involve backend interfaces.
page_functionality.state_logic: On load, profile.html reads the redirect query parameter from the URL, if present, and stores it in in-page state (e.g., redirectTarget). This value is not persisted beyond the page and is not written via any interface. The page then calls getProfile once. The response from getProfile determines whether the form is in 'create' or 'edit' mode: if a Profile object is returned, the form state is initialized with those field values; otherwise, the form state starts with empty values. The page maintains internal UI state flags such as isLoadingProfile (true while getProfile is pending), loadError (set if getProfile fails), isSaving (true while saveProfile is pending), saveError (set on save failure), and lastSaveSucceeded (set on successful save). These flags control component behavior (e.g., disabling buttons, showing loaders or messages) but are not reflected in the URL or persisted via any interfaces. When the user submits the form, the current form state is transformed into a Profile object and passed to saveProfile. The response from saveProfile replaces the local Profile state so that the form displays exactly what was saved. Navigation after saving depends on user intent and the redirect URL parameter: if the user chooses 'Save profile and go to home', the page always navigates to index.html regardless of redirectTarget; if the user chooses a 'continue' action and redirectTarget exists, the browser navigates to redirectTarget; otherwise, the page remains on profile.html. No localStorage or other client-side persistence is used; only URL parameters and the two interfaces (getProfile, saveProfile) determine the page’s data state.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading the redirect URL parameter, calling getProfile to retrieve the current Profile, and propagating the loaded data and loading/error state to downstream components.

Editable form that displays and allows modification of Profile fields including full_name, email, phone, preferred_contact_method, preferred_location_type, preferred_zip, preferred_city, preferred_state, and communication_consent. It maintains local form state, performs validation, and prepares the payload for saveProfile.

Coordinates saving actions initiated from the profile form. Receives validated Profile data from the form, calls saveProfile, manages isSaving and error states, and communicates save results back to the form and navigation components.

Renders and manages the action buttons for saving and navigating, such as 'Save profile', 'Save profile and go to home', and, when a redirect URL is present, a 'Save profile and continue' action. It triggers the appropriate save flows in the profile-save-controller and handles post-save navigation decisions.

Reads the redirect URL parameter captured by profile-loader and displays contextual information that the user is updating their profile as part of another flow. It shows or hides any 'continue previous task' action in the profile-actions-bar based on the presence of a redirect target.

Displays status and feedback messages related to loading and saving operations, including loading indicators, load errors, save-in-progress indicators, save success confirmations, and save errors. It reacts to state changes from profile-loader and profile-save-controller.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px). Nav, headline, search bar, and category pills all appear horizontally aligned to this central grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the hero pattern from the mockup but repurpose it for a settings-style page: a fixed global nav at the very top, then a full-width hero section with a dark, tech-flavored background (e.g., subtle circuitry or city/map imagery) and gradient overlay. Inside a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px), stack elements vertically in this order: redirect-context-banner (when present), page title/subtitle (Profile & Preferences; implicit, surrounding the components), profile-status-messages, a horizontal profile-actions-bar (acting like category pills/tabs), and then the primary profile-form card with the profile-save-controller anchored in its header area. The profile-loader appears as an overlay centered on top of this stack when active.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the hero pattern from the mockup but repurpose it for a settings-style page: a fixed global nav at the very top, then a full-width hero section with a dark, tech-flavored background (e.g., subtle circuitry or city/map imagery) and gradient overlay. Inside a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px), stack elements vertically in this order: redirect-context-banner (when present), page title/subtitle (Profile & Preferences; implicit, surrounding the components), profile-status-messages, a horizontal profile-actions-bar (acting like category pills/tabs), and then the primary profile-form card with the profile-save-controller anchored in its header area. The profile-loader appears as an overlay centered on top of this stack when active.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Let the hero section fill roughly the first viewport height on desktop, with generous vertical padding (64–96px top and bottom). Allocate roughly the upper 30–35% of the hero’s height to the title area, context banner, and status messages, the central 45–50% to the profile-form card (the main interaction surface), and the lower 15–20% to the profile-actions-bar plus breathing room. Inside the content container, key blocks (banner, status messages, actions bar, form) are separated by 24–32px vertical gaps. The form card itself uses about 65–75% of the container width and is centered, while the actions bar spans nearly the full content width horizontally.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately spacious layout echoing the hero design: few competing elements, large tap/click targets, and clear separations. Components use the 8px spacing scale: ~24–32px margin between stacked components, 16–24px padding inside banners, pills, and cards, and 8–16px gaps between items within a component. Text remains concise (short statuses, minimal helper copy), and key interactive controls (save button, tab-like actions) are visually prominent without cluttering the screen.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Mimic the mockup’s flow: the eye starts at the global logo and nav, then drops into the hero where the page title and any redirect-context-banner establish context. From there, attention moves down to the profile-status-messages (feedback about the profile), then into the horizontal profile-actions-bar (section shortcuts) and finally settles on the large, elevated profile-form card. Within the form header, the primary blue save button (profile-save-controller) becomes the main interaction focal point. When the loader appears, it briefly overrides this flow by centering a loading indicator over the form area, then returns focus to the form and save button once complete.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a fixed, slim navigation bar at the top, spanning the full width with the logo on the left and a few nav items and a bright call-to-action on the right, matching the startup-like feel of the original mockup. Directly beneath, a full-width hero section fills the initial viewport, using a dark, slightly blurred tech background overlaid by a deep gradient. Within this hero, all profile-related content sits in a centered, max-width container aligned to an implicit 12-column grid. At the very top of this container, a narrow contextual banner (when present) stretches horizontally, followed by a strong page title and short description that sit left-aligned but visually anchored near the center of the screen. Below that, any profile status messages appear as compact but noticeable banners. A bit further down, a horizontal strip of pill-like action items forms the profile-actions-bar, mirroring the categories row from the mockup and inviting quick navigation between profile sections. The lower central portion of the hero is dominated by a large, white, elevated profile-form card with soft corners and a subtle shadow, centered and occupying roughly two-thirds of the content width. The top-right corner of this card houses a prominent blue save button, which becomes the main interactive focal point. When data is loading, a semi-opaque overlay dims the form area and surfaces a centered loader, while the rest of the hero remains visible but de-emphasized. Overall, the layout feels modern, spacious, and focused, with a clear vertical journey from context to feedback to action, all framed within a tech-forward hero background.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The profile loader appears as an overlay centered over the hero’s main content area, specifically covering the region where the profile-form card sits. When active, it introduces a semi-transparent dark scrim (about 40–50% opacity) that spans the entire content container width and extends from just below the page title area down to slightly below the bottom edge of the form card, effectively dimming the form and profile-actions-bar without obscuring the global navigation. A circular spinner or loading indicator is placed dead center horizontally and vertically within this scrim, aligned with the center of the form card. Around the loader, there is generous padding (at least 64px) before the scrim edges, maintaining the airy feel of the hero. The rest of the hero background and nav remain visible but subtly muted behind the overlay, ensuring the user’s focus is drawn to the loading state in the middle of the screen.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an absolute-positioned overlay within the hero section, anchored to the edges of the main content container. It floats above the profile-form and profile-actions-bar, intercepting interaction while visible but allowing the fixed global nav to remain interactive if desired. The overlay is non-sticky and appears/disappears based on loading state, without affecting the underlying layout’s spacing (no reflow).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The profile-form is the dominant card-like element in the middle of the hero. On desktop, it is centered horizontally within the max-width container, spanning roughly 65–75% of the container’s width (about 720–880px within ~1120px), leaving balanced whitespace on both left and right. Vertically, it sits in the center-third of the hero: there is about 64px of vertical space between the profile-actions-bar above and the top of the form, and another 64–96px of padding below the form before the hero ends or the next section begins. The form itself is a white or very light card with slightly rounded corners and a soft shadow, visually elevated above the dark background. Inside, the form fields are arranged in a simple, legible vertical stack with optional two-column groupings, each row separated by 16–24px. The card has generous internal padding: ~32px top and bottom, and 24–32px left and right. At the very top of the card, a header row runs horizontally: on the left, the form title or section name (implicit), and on the right, the profile-save-controller button. This header aligns to the same 12-column grid as the rest of the page. The card extends downward to accommodate all fields; if the content exceeds the initial viewport height, the page scrolls normally, with the card stretching further down the page but maintaining its centered alignment and relative width.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal document flow, centered within the hero’s content container. It does not stick or float; as the user scrolls, the card moves naturally with the page, maintaining its horizontal centering and width relative to the container. The internal header row provides an anchor for the save button but does not change position on scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The profile-save-controller presents as a prominent primary button integrated into the top-right corner of the profile-form card’s header row. Horizontally, it is aligned flush with the right padding edge of the card, sitting about 24–32px from the card’s inner right edge and 16–24px from the top edge. Vertically, it shares a baseline with the form’s header text on the left, forming a clean, single horizontal line across the top of the card. The button itself is wide enough to feel substantial (e.g., 140–200px) with 10–12px vertical and 20–24px horizontal padding, and uses a bright blue fill consistent with the primary CTAs in the mockup, contrasting strongly against the white card. There is at least 16px of space between this button and any secondary actions or labels that may also sit in the header. On wider screens, it remains anchored to the right edge of the form card, not the viewport, so it always appears visually tied to the form. As the user scans down from title to actions bar to card, the blue save button becomes the primary focal point at the top of the interaction area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned within the static header row of the profile-form, aligned to the top-right. It scrolls with the form content and does not detach or become sticky at the viewport level. Its position is responsive: on narrower screens (e.g., mobile), it may wrap below the header title but remains right-aligned within the card, keeping a 16–24px top margin from the card’s upper edge.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The profile-actions-bar sits directly above the profile-form card, occupying a horizontal strip that visually echoes the category pills row from the original hero. It is placed within the same centered content container, spanning nearly its full width (about 90–100% of the container, stopping short of the edges by 16–24px on each side). There is a 24–32px vertical gap between the bottom of any profile-status-messages and the top of this actions bar, and another 24–32px gap between the bottom of the actions bar and the top of the profile-form card. Within this strip, a series of pill-shaped action items (e.g., tabs for "Contact info", "Notification preferences", etc.) are arranged horizontally from left to right. Each pill has 8–10px vertical and 16–20px horizontal padding, rounded corners, and a subtle shadow or border, with the active pill using a filled blue or higher-contrast style. The pills align to the 12-column grid, starting near the left margin; empty space on the right lets the layout breathe. On smaller screens, the row can scroll horizontally. This bar visually anchors the middle of the hero, breaking the vertical stack and providing a clear navigation band before the main form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the flow, positioned between status messages and the form. It does not stick to the top on scroll but remains close to the form so that switching actions feels directly connected to the form content below. On narrow viewports, the pill row becomes horizontally scrollable within its container, while still occupying a single line and maintaining its vertical spacing from surrounding components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The redirect-context-banner sits at the top of the hero’s content stack, just below the fixed global navigation and initial top padding. It appears as a full-width strip within the content container, spanning 100% of that container’s width but respecting its left and right margins. Vertically, it’s relatively shallow but visually distinct: about 40–64px tall depending on content, with 12–16px internal padding on all sides. It uses a semi-transparent or softly colored background (e.g., a muted blue or amber overlay on white) and slightly rounded corners, creating a pill-like but elongated bar. The banner is positioned about 64–80px below the bottom edge of the global nav, aligning with the hero’s top padding and establishing the top of the profile content. When present, it pushes the page title and all subsequent elements (status messages, actions bar, form) downward, maintaining a 16–24px gap between its bottom edge and the title. Typically, it runs across the upper third of the hero, giving context without overwhelming the main form area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and inline within the document flow. It does not stick to the viewport; if the user scrolls, it moves off-screen with the rest of the hero content. When hidden, the space it would occupy collapses, allowing the title to move up and make use of the vertical padding at the top of the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The profile-status-messages component is placed between the page title area and the profile-actions-bar, functioning as a stack of compact alerts or confirmation banners. It is aligned to the same content container as the other components, spanning roughly 80–100% of that width depending on design choice; for a lighter feel, the banners can be slightly inset compared to the actions bar and form, e.g., 16px narrower on each side. There is a 8–16px gap between the page title/subtitle (above) and the first status message, and 16–24px between the bottom-most status message and the top of the profile-actions-bar. If multiple messages are shown, they stack vertically with 8–12px spacing between them. Each message is a shallow pill or card with 8–12px vertical and 12–16px horizontal internal padding, colored according to its type (green for success, red for error, yellow for warning), but kept compact to maintain low text density. Positioned in the upper-middle of the hero, these messages are immediately visible after context but before interaction, subtly guiding the user before they engage with the form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal flow, expanding and collapsing vertically depending on how many messages are present. When messages appear, they push down the profile-actions-bar and profile-form uniformly, preserving the overall vertical rhythm. They scroll off-screen as the user moves down the page and do not have sticky behavior.

name: Browse Categories
title: Browse Repair Categories
description: Page for browsing repair service categories and subcategories, setting a location, and opening pre-filtered search results based on the selected category.

core_features: Load and display repair service categories using getServiceCategories, using the parentCategoryId URL parameter to determine whether to show top-level categories or subcategories.
Allow users to drill down into subcategories either via deep-linked URLs (categories.html?parentCategoryId=...) or by expanding a category to lazily load its subcategories with getServiceCategories.
Provide a location input that calls setCurrentLocation so users can set or change their current location; the resolved location is used when navigating to search_results.html.
Enable one-click navigation from any category or subcategory to search_results.html with appropriate query parameters (categoryId, derived serviceCode, q, location, view=list).
Expose a shareable, bookmarkable state via the parentCategoryId URL parameter so users can return to or share specific category views.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Browse from top-level category to subcategory to search results: 1) User opens categories.html without a parentCategoryId parameter. 2) The page controller reads the URL, detects no parentCategoryId, and calls getServiceCategories with an empty/undefined parentCategoryId to load top-level categories. 3) The user scans the list and expands a category to see its subcategories; this triggers getServiceCategories with parentCategoryId equal to the selected category id. 4) The user clicks on a subcategory item. 5) The navigation handler constructs search_results.html with query parameters categoryId=subcategory.id, serviceCode=derived from subcategory.slug, q=subcategory.name, location=current resolved location display name if available, view=list. 6) Browser navigates to search_results.html with those parameters.

Workflow 2 – Deep link into a specific parent category: 1) User opens categories.html?parentCategoryId={someCategoryId} from index.html or a shared link. 2) The page controller reads parentCategoryId from the URL and calls getServiceCategories with this value to load subcategories of that parent. 3) The user sees only the subcategories for the given parent and optionally uses a link to go back to categories.html without parentCategoryId to view all top-level categories. 4) The user clicks on a subcategory to navigate to search_results.html with categoryId set to the chosen subcategory.id and other parameters derived from the subcategory and current location.

Workflow 3 – Set or change location before navigating: 1) User opens categories.html (with or without parentCategoryId). 2) The user types a ZIP code or city/state into the location input. 3) On form submit, the page calls setCurrentLocation(location_text). 4) If the response.success is true, the page stores the resolved_location object in memory and shows its display_name near the page title. 5) The user then clicks a category or subcategory; the navigation handler includes the resolved_location.display_name as the location parameter in the search_results.html URL. 6) If setCurrentLocation returns success=false, the page shows the returned message as an error and does not update the current location.
interactions: Click on a main category card: triggers navigation to search_results.html with the selected category as the filter and view=list, using any current resolved location if available.

Click on a subcategory item within an expanded category: triggers navigation to search_results.html with the selected subcategory as the filter and view=list, using any current resolved location if available.

Click on an expand/collapse control for a category: when expanding, triggers a call to getServiceCategories with parentCategoryId set to that category’s id to load its subcategories; when collapsing, hides already-loaded subcategories without additional API calls.

Submit the location form: triggers setCurrentLocation with the entered location_text; on success, updates in-memory current location state and the header display; on failure, shows an error message and keeps the previous location state.

Click on a breadcrumb or 'All categories' link: updates the browser URL to categories.html without a parentCategoryId parameter and triggers reloading of top-level categories via getServiceCategories.

Reload or share the URL: when the page loads, it reads parentCategoryId from the query string and calls getServiceCategories appropriately, reproducing the same category level view for any user opening that URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: On page initialization, the controller reads the parentCategoryId query parameter from the URL. If parentCategoryId is present and non-empty, the page calls getServiceCategories(parentCategoryId) to load a list of subcategories. If parentCategoryId is absent, the page calls getServiceCategories with an undefined/empty parentCategoryId to load top-level categories. The current list of categories displayed on the page is always the direct result of the most recent getServiceCategories call and is not hardcoded. When a user chooses to view subcategories for a specific category via a deep-link or breadcrumb interaction, the browser URL is updated accordingly (adding or removing parentCategoryId), and the page re-evaluates which categories to fetch. Location state is managed in memory per page load: when setCurrentLocation(location_text) returns success, the resolved_location object is stored in a transient state variable (e.g., currentLocation) and used only while the page is open. This state is not persisted via localStorage. When constructing navigation to search_results.html, the navigation handler reads the selected category or subcategory object (including its id, name, and slug) and the currentLocation display_name if available. It then builds a search_results.html URL with the following parameters: categoryId is the selected category’s id; serviceCode is derived from the ServiceCategory.slug by matching it against the allowed ProviderService.service_code enum values and falling back to 'other_service' if there is no match; q is the selected category’s name; location is currentLocation.display_name if currentLocation exists, otherwise the parameter may be omitted or left empty; view is set to the literal string 'list'. All state transitions that should be shareable rely on URL parameters; no state is stored in localStorage or similar browser storage.

components.functionality: Displays the functional page title and, if available, the currently resolved location (from setCurrentLocation) so users know where their subsequent searches will be focused.

Provides a single text input for ZIP code or city/state and a submit action that calls setCurrentLocation. Handles success and error responses, updates the in-memory current location, and informs other components (such as the header and navigation handler) of the updated location.

Central controller for loading and refreshing the category list. On initialization, reads the parentCategoryId query parameter from the URL and calls getServiceCategories with that value (or with an undefined/empty value when no parentCategoryId is present). Manages loading, success, empty, and error states, and passes the resulting categories array to the category-list component.

Renders the array of ServiceCategory objects returned by the category-browser-controller as clickable items. For each category, it shows the name, optional description, and optional icon (all coming directly from the ServiceCategory fields). Each category item supports two main actions: clicking the primary area of the card to navigate directly to search_results.html for that category, and toggling an expand/collapse control to show or hide its subcategories using the subcategory-loader component.

When a user expands a specific category, this component calls getServiceCategories with parentCategoryId set to that category's id to fetch its immediate subcategories. It caches the result for that parent category during the current page session so that collapsing and re-expanding does not cause repeated API calls. It passes the loaded subcategory list back to the category-list (or an inline subcategory-list) for rendering, and enables navigation when a subcategory is clicked.

Displays the subcategories for a given parent category in a compact, clickable list. Each item is a ServiceCategory instance obtained via the subcategory-loader. Clicking an item triggers navigation to search_results.html with that subcategory as the selected category.

Provides simple navigation between the top-level categories view and a subcategory view. When a parentCategoryId parameter is present in the URL, the breadcrumb shows a link back to the root categories view (categories.html without parentCategoryId). Clicking this link updates the URL and triggers the category-browser-controller to reload top-level categories via getServiceCategories.

Constructs and triggers navigation URLs to search_results.html based on the selected ServiceCategory (either a main category or subcategory) and the current resolved location. For a given category object, it sets categoryId to category.id, q to category.name, view to 'list', and location to the current resolved_location.display_name if a location has been set. It derives serviceCode by comparing category.slug to the allowed ProviderService.service_code enumeration values; if category.slug matches one of those values, that slug is used as serviceCode, otherwise 'other_service' is used. It is invoked by category-list and subcategory-inline-list click events, and by breadcrumb interactions when moving back to categories.html.

Handles the situation where getServiceCategories returns an empty array for the current parentCategoryId. Displays a neutral message indicating that no categories are available for this level and may suggest returning to the root categories view via the breadcrumb.

Displays error information when getServiceCategories or setCurrentLocation fails (for example, due to network issues or invalid location input). For category-loading errors, it allows the user to retry the last getServiceCategories call; for location errors, it shows the message returned by setCurrentLocation and keeps the location input available for correction.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px). Nav, headline, search bar, and category pills all appear horizontally aligned to this central grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s hero-first pattern: a full-width hero band with a dark gradient over a map/tech background, containing the page header, breadcrumb, and a prominent location selector that visually echoes the original search bar. Below the hero sits the main browsing area where category cards are laid out in a centered grid. Subcategories appear inline beneath their parent category. Empty and error states replace or sit above the category list within this main content band. Non-visual controller components wrap or govern these visible elements without taking up physical space.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s hero-first pattern: a full-width hero band with a dark gradient over a map/tech background, containing the page header, breadcrumb, and a prominent location selector that visually echoes the original search bar. Below the hero sits the main browsing area where category cards are laid out in a centered grid. Subcategories appear inline beneath their parent category. Empty and error states replace or sit above the category list within this main content band. Non-visual controller components wrap or govern these visible elements without taking up physical space.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly the upper 50–60% of the initial viewport height on desktop, with a max-width content container (~1100–1200px) centered horizontally. Inside the hero, the top slice is reserved for breadcrumb navigation, the central slice for the page header text, and the lower slice for the location selector. Below the hero, the category grid fills the remaining viewport and scrolls, with generous top and bottom padding (~64px). Category cards occupy the grid width with 3–4 columns depending on screen size. Empty and error states occupy the same main-content region, centered and given ample white space to breathe.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density remains moderate and airy: a single prominent page title and short description in the hero, one primary form element (location selector) with ample padding, and a grid of category cards with clear separation and readable labels. Subcategory lists are more information dense but remain organized with clear spacing between items. Empty and error messaging use succinct text with visual emphasis rather than long paragraphs.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mimics the mockup’s top-to-bottom pattern: the eye travels from the fixed site navigation (outside this spec) down to the breadcrumb at the top of the hero, then to the bold white page title, then straight down to the bright, pill-shaped location selector. From there, the eye naturally drops into the main content area where the category grid is the next focal point. Within the grid, expanded categories pull attention downward into inline subcategory lists. Blue accent elements (interactive text, buttons, active breadcrumb states) guide attention toward actions like changing location or selecting a category.

layout.overall_description: The page opens on a full-width hero section overlaid on a darkened map-of-Romania/tech background, giving a modern, directory-like feel. Centered on the page is a max-width content column aligned to an implicit 12-column grid. At the top of this column, a subtle breadcrumb line anchors the user’s location in the site. Just below, a large, bold, white page title—“Browse Categories” and perhaps a short supporting line—sits in the middle of the hero, occupying the central horizontal band and leaving generous negative space around it. Directly beneath the title, a wide, pill-shaped location selector spans more than half the content width, visually echoing a search bar with clean fields and a prominent confirm/update control. The hero’s lower edge fades smoothly into a light main content section where the centered category grid begins. Category cards, each a bright, elevated tile with an icon and label, are arranged in 3–4 columns across the grid with consistent gutters, creating a clear, scannable overview of repair types. When a category is expanded, its subcategories appear inline directly under that row, maintaining context while slightly increasing local density. If no categories are available for the chosen location, a clean empty state card appears centered where the grid would be; if there’s a loading or data error, a clear error state message appears prominently toward the top of the content area. Throughout, vertical spacing follows a consistent rhythm based on an 8px scale, preserving the semi-minimal, conversion-focused aesthetic of the original hero design.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page header sits within the hero section, centered horizontally inside the max-width content container and positioned roughly in the middle of the hero’s vertical height. It occupies about the central third of the hero vertically. The main title is a large, multi-line headline aligned left within the content column, extending across roughly 6–8 grid columns (about 60–70% of the container width), with the text wrapping naturally. If present, a short supporting description sits directly beneath the title, left-aligned and spanning a slightly narrower width. There is approximately 24–32px of vertical space above the header (to the breadcrumb) and another 24–32px below (before the location selector), creating a clear vertical stack of breadcrumb → header → location selector. The header text is in high-contrast white against the dark gradient, making it the hero’s primary focal point.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the hero; it scrolls away with the hero as the user scrolls down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The location selector is placed directly beneath the page header inside the hero, following the same centered content column. It is a horizontally oriented, pill-shaped form that visually mirrors the mockup’s search bar. The component spans about 60–70% of the content container’s width on desktop and is centered within it, leaving equal whitespace on the left and right. Internally, it uses a subtle card elevation and rounds the corners prominently. Within the pill, the main location input field sits on the left, occupying roughly two-thirds of the component width, with a clear label and possibly an icon (e.g., a map pin). On the right, a primary action (such as a blue “Update” or “Apply” button) takes up the remaining third, aligned flush to the right with consistent internal padding (about 12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal). The pill sits with about 24–32px of vertical space below the page header and 32–40px above the bottom edge of the hero, making it the lower focal element of the hero area. On smaller screens, it can expand to nearly full width within the container while maintaining generous top and bottom margin to avoid crowding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the hero that resizes responsively; no sticky behavior, but retains its centered alignment and relative spacing as the viewport changes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category-browser-controller is a non-visual wrapper that governs the loading and state of the category list and subcategories. Conceptually, it occupies the same horizontal band as the main category grid, directly below the hero. Its ‘area’ begins at the bottom of the hero’s padding and extends downward through the scrollable list of categories. It does not present any UI by itself; instead, it determines whether the category-list, categories-empty-state, or categories-error-state is rendered in the central content region beneath the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller with no explicit spatial footprint; it influences which visible components appear in the main content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category list is the core content block immediately below the hero. It begins with about 48–64px of top margin from the bottom edge of the hero, maintaining the mockup’s spacious separation between stacked sections. It is centered horizontally within the same max-width content container, following the 12-column grid. Categories are displayed as a grid of elevated cards or pills with rounded corners, each card occupying roughly 3–4 columns per row on desktop (about 25–33% of the container width, minus gutters). There is consistent horizontal gutter space (~24px) between cards and vertical spacing (~24–32px) between rows. Each card has internal padding of 16–24px, with an icon aligned to the left or top-center and the category label next to or below it. When no category is expanded, the list forms a clean, multi-row grid that fills the width of the content container up to its max width, leaving generous whitespace on the sides before the viewport edges. As the user scrolls, this grid runs down the page with consistent vertical rhythm, acting as the main scrollable content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable content block that grows vertically as more categories are present; it remains centered and respects the grid and container width at all times.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The subcategory-loader is another non-visual controller that conceptually sits inline with each category row in the main grid. For each category, its ‘location’ is directly beneath that category’s card or row of cards. When a category is expanded, this loader is responsible for fetching and then revealing the subcategory-inline-list in the precise vertical slot under the relevant category. Visually, the loader itself is invisible; at most, it may trigger a subtle inline loading indicator in the space where subcategories will appear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, inline controller that dynamically inserts or removes subcategory-inline-lists below specific categories without shifting the overall page structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The subcategory-inline-list appears directly below the corresponding parent category within the main category list area. When a user selects or expands a category card, the grid row containing that card is followed immediately by an inline subcategory section. This section spans the full width of the content container below that row (across all 12 columns), creating a clear band tied to that category. Inside this band, subcategories may be displayed as a horizontal row of smaller pills or a multi-column list with concise text labels. There is a subtle background differentiation (e.g., a slightly lighter or tinted panel with rounded corners and 16–24px of internal padding) to distinguish it from the main page background while keeping it visually subordinate to the main category cards. The inline list has about 16–24px of vertical spacing above and below it relative to neighboring rows, ensuring the expanded state feels deliberate but not cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline expandable/collapsible section that pushes surrounding content down when opened and collapses to zero height when closed; it remains tethered to its parent category’s position in the scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb navigation sits at the top of the hero’s content column, directly below the global top navigation bar and within the dark gradient overlay. It is aligned to the left edge of the main content grid and spans roughly 6–8 columns, matching the header text width. Vertically, it occupies a shallow band in the upper 15–20% of the hero’s height, with about 24–32px of padding between it and the top of the hero’s content area, and another 16–24px separating it from the page header below. The breadcrumbs are rendered as a small, low-contrast (relative to the headline) horizontal sequence with subtle separators (e.g., “Home / Browse Categories”), keeping them visually lightweight and not competing with the main title. On smaller viewports, the breadcrumb wraps gracefully to a second line if necessary while remaining left-aligned within the same content column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero; it scrolls away with the hero and does not stick to the top once the user scrolls past.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation-handler is purely logical and does not render visible UI. Conceptually, it is associated with interactive elements such as category cards, subcategory items, and possibly breadcrumb links. Its ‘position’ is thus distributed wherever these interactive components exist, but it has no visible shape, size, or spacing of its own. It simply responds to user interactions by triggering navigation or updating the URL, while the visual layout remains governed by the other components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual behavioral layer with no spatial footprint; it does not affect layout beyond causing page or view transitions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The categories-empty-state occupies the same main content region as the category-list and appears when no categories are available for the chosen location or filters. It is centered within the max-width content container directly below the hero, beginning at roughly the same vertical position where the category grid would normally start (about 48–64px below the hero). The empty state is presented as a single, elevated card or panel that spans about 40–60% of the container width on desktop, centered horizontally. Inside the card, an illustration or icon sits at the top center, followed by a concise message and a possible secondary action (e.g., ‘Change location’). There is generous internal padding (24–32px) and ample empty space around the card to emphasize the state without clutter. No category cards are rendered when this state is active, keeping the area visually simple and focused on guidance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that replaces the category-list in the main content area when triggered; it grows with its content but remains centered and non-sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The categories-error-state appears in the main content region beneath the hero when there is a loading or data error affecting categories or subcategories. It is positioned near the top of the content area, starting about 32–48px below the hero’s bottom edge, so it’s seen immediately after the hero when the user scrolls slightly or if the viewport is tall enough, without requiring much scrolling. The error is presented as a full-width alert band within the content container, spanning essentially all 12 columns but constrained by the same max-width as other content. The band uses a high-visibility style (e.g., a colored background with an error icon on the left and concise explanatory text to the right, possibly with a retry button). There is 16–24px of internal padding and 16–24px of margin below the band before any other elements (like a degraded category list or empty space). If the error prevents any list from rendering, the band may be combined with an empty-style panel, still centered within the same region, but the error band remains the topmost element in the content area for clarity.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static alert-type block that occupies the top of the main content area when present; it pushes the category-list or empty-state content downward but does not float or remain sticky while scrolling.

name: About
title: About
description: The About page loads structured content from the backend to explain what the electronic repair directory is, which services and devices it covers, and how providers, listings, and ratings are managed. Users can navigate to specific sections via in-page links and URL parameters.

core_features: Load About page content (page title and multiple sections) from the getAboutPageContent interface when the page initializes.
Render the About page title using the title value returned by getAboutPageContent.
Render all About sections using the sections array from getAboutPageContent, displaying each section.heading and section.body_html.

Generate an in-page table of contents (TOC) from the sections returned by getAboutPageContent to let users jump to specific topics (e.g., mission, devices and services covered, provider and rating management).
Support deep-linking to a specific section via URL parameters (e.g., about.html?section=...), so a user can land directly on a specific About topic.
Highlight the currently active section (based on scroll position or URL parameter) and keep the URL in sync with the active section without reloading the page.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Basic visit from footer link: 1) User clicks the 'About' link in the footer, which navigates to about.html with no additional parameters. 2) The page initializes and calls getAboutPageContent. 3) When the response returns, the page title is set from response.title. 4) The page renders a table of contents derived from response.sections[].heading. 5) The page renders all sections using response.sections[].heading and response.sections[].body_html. 6) User scrolls through the content to read details about the directory, devices and services covered, and how providers and ratings are managed.

Workflow 2 – Deep-linked section navigation: 1) User opens a URL pointing to about.html with a section parameter (e.g., about.html?section=<section-slug>). 2) The page initializes and reads the section parameter from the URL. 3) The page calls getAboutPageContent. 4) When the response returns, the page derives a slug for each section from sections[].heading (e.g., by normalizing the heading) and finds the section whose slug matches the section URL parameter. 5) The page scrolls to that section and marks it as active in the TOC. 6) As the user scrolls, the active section indicator updates, and the section URL parameter in the address bar is updated accordingly (without reloading the page).

Workflow 3 – In-page section switching via TOC: 1) User is on about.html and sees the table of contents listing all available sections (based on response.sections). 2) User clicks a TOC item corresponding to a section heading. 3) The page prevents full reload, updates the URL section parameter to the clicked section’s slug, and scrolls to the selected section. 4) The clicked section is marked as active; other sections remain visible but not active. 5) If the user uses the browser back/forward buttons, the URL section parameter changes, and the page scrolls to the corresponding section again.

Workflow 4 – Print-friendly or condensed view (optional URL-controlled behavior): 1) User or another page links to about.html with an additional view parameter (e.g., about.html?view=print or about.html?view=summary). 2) The page reads the view parameter and applies client-side rules on how to present the content (for example, in print view hide the TOC, or in summary view initially collapse section bodies and show only headings). 3) The page still calls getAboutPageContent and renders all sections, but changes how they are displayed based solely on the view URL parameter. 4) User can toggle between views (e.g., via a control on the page) which updates the view parameter in the URL without reloading the page.
interactions: On page load, the About page automatically calls getAboutPageContent with no parameters and waits for the title and sections data before rendering content.
If the API call fails, the page shows a generic error state driven purely by client logic (e.g., a message that content could not be loaded) without hardcoded About copy.

Clicking on a table of contents entry triggers an in-page navigation: the page scrolls to the relevant section, updates the active section styling, and sets the section URL parameter for deep-linking.

When the user scrolls manually, an intersection or scroll listener detects which section is currently in view, updates the active TOC entry, and updates the section URL parameter in the address bar to match that section.

If the URL contains a section parameter on initial load or when the browser back/forward buttons are used, the page interprets that parameter and scrolls to the matching section after getAboutPageContent data has been loaded.

If the URL contains an optional view parameter, the page adjusts client-side presentation (such as showing all content expanded or hiding non-essential navigation) while still using the same data from getAboutPageContent.
page_functionality.state_logic: The About page state is entirely driven by the URL and the response from getAboutPageContent. The getAboutPageContent interface has no incoming parameters, so it always returns the full About content. The URL can include optional query parameters to control client-side behavior: 1) section=<section-slug>: Indicates which section should be considered active and scrolled into view. On initial load and on popstate (back/forward navigation), the page reads this parameter, matches it against a slug derived from each section.heading, and scrolls to the matching section. When the active section changes (via TOC click or scroll), the page updates this parameter using the History API without reloading the page. 2) view=<mode>: Controls purely client-side presentation modes, such as a print-friendly or summary view. The page reads this parameter on load and whenever it changes, and toggles internal UI flags (e.g., hide/show TOC, collapse section bodies). No additional API calls are made for different views. The page does not use localStorage or other persistent client storage; all shareable state is encoded in the URL so that the same state can be reconstructed from a direct link.
