components.functionality: Responsible for calling getAboutPageContent on page initialization, handling the loading and error states, and providing the resulting title and sections data to child components once the promise resolves.
event_handlers: onInit (triggered when about.html is loaded; calls getAboutPageContent)
onFetchSuccess (called when getAboutPageContent returns; passes response.title and response.sections to dependent components)
onFetchError (called when the API call fails; signals an error state for content components)

components.functionality: Displays the page title using the title value returned by getAboutPageContent. If the API is still loading or errors, it uses a loading or fallback caption defined in client logic (not hardcoded About content).
event_handlers: onTitleChange (called when about-page-loader provides or updates the title value)
onClick (optional: could scroll back to top when the title is clicked)

components.functionality: Renders the list of About sections returned by getAboutPageContent. For each item in sections, it displays the section.heading and injects section.body_html into the page. Each section gets a stable DOM id derived from its heading (e.g., a slug) to enable in-page navigation and URL parameter mapping.
event_handlers: onSectionsChange (called when the sections array is available or updated from about-page-loader)
onSectionRendered (called after each section is rendered; used by scroll tracking logic to attach observers)
onActiveSectionChange (called by scroll or TOC interactions to toggle the active section state for styling)

components.functionality: Generates a table of contents from the sections returned by getAboutPageContent, using section.heading values for labels. Clicking an item scrolls to the corresponding section, updates the active section indicator, and sets the section URL parameter to the associated slug.
event_handlers: onSectionsChange (rebuilds the TOC when the sections list is loaded or updated)
onTocItemClick (receives a section slug, scrolls to the section element, sets it as active, and updates the section URL parameter via history.pushState or history.replaceState)
onUrlParamChange (responds to external changes to the section URL parameter, such as browser back/forward, and updates the active TOC item accordingly)

components.functionality: Centralizes reading and writing of URL parameters that affect the About page (e.g., section and view). On load, it parses the current URL parameters and exposes them to other components. It also updates the URL when the active section or view mode changes, and listens for browser navigation events to keep the UI in sync.
event_handlers: onInit (reads existing URL parameters and notifies interested components such as about-section-list and about-toc-navigation)
onActiveSectionRequestChange (called by about-toc-navigation or scroll tracking logic to request an update to the section URL parameter)
onViewModeChange (called by any control toggling the view mode; updates the view URL parameter and notifies content components)
onPopState (handles browser back/forward; re-reads URL parameters and updates active section and view mode without reloading the page)

components.functionality: Observes the visibility and scroll position of each rendered section to determine which section is currently in view. It then notifies the URL state manager and TOC to update the active section and URL parameter.
event_handlers: onSectionElementRegister (called by about-section-list when a section DOM element is available to be tracked)
onScroll (or onIntersectionChange; determines which section is most visible and triggers an active section change)
onActiveSectionDetected (notifies about-url-state-manager and about-toc-navigation to update state and URL)

components.functionality: Optionally provides controls (such as buttons or links) for switching between different view modes controlled by the view URL parameter (for example, print-friendly vs default). It uses only client-side logic to change layout while still rendering all data returned by getAboutPageContent.
event_handlers: onClickDefaultView (sets view parameter to a default mode or removes it from the URL, and notifies other components to render default layout)
onClickAlternateView (sets view parameter to an alternate mode such as print or summary, and notifies other components to adjust layout)
onUrlParamChange (updates the toggle control’s selected state when the view parameter changes due to direct linking or navigation)

components.functionality: Displays loading indicators while getAboutPageContent is in progress and shows a generic error message if the API request fails. It does not include any hardcoded About content; it only reflects the status of the data-fetch operation.
event_handlers: onFetchStart (shows loading state when about-page-loader begins requesting data)
onFetchSuccess (hides loading state when data has been successfully loaded)
onFetchError (displays an error state when the API call to getAboutPageContent fails)

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px). Nav, headline, search bar, and category pills all appear horizontally aligned to this central grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The About page reuses the homepage hero pattern but replaces the search bar with structured informational controls. At the top is the global fixed header (logo left, nav + CTA right). Below it, a full-width hero band uses the same darkened map/tech background with a gradient overlay. Inside the hero, content is vertically stacked and center-aligned within a max-width (~1120px) container: first the About page title (and optional short intro), then a compact view-mode toggle aligned along the same grid, and at the bottom of the hero a horizontal in-page TOC row styled like pill-shaped category shortcuts. Below the hero, the main About content sections are stacked in a single central column, aligned to the same 12-column grid. Invisible functional components (loader, URL state manager, scroll tracker) live at the top of the main content wrapper and do not consume visible space.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The About page reuses the homepage hero pattern but replaces the search bar with structured informational controls. At the top is the global fixed header (logo left, nav + CTA right). Below it, a full-width hero band uses the same darkened map/tech background with a gradient overlay. Inside the hero, content is vertically stacked and center-aligned within a max-width (~1120px) container: first the About page title (and optional short intro), then a compact view-mode toggle aligned along the same grid, and at the bottom of the hero a horizontal in-page TOC row styled like pill-shaped category shortcuts. Below the hero, the main About content sections are stacked in a single central column, aligned to the same 12-column grid. Invisible functional components (loader, URL state manager, scroll tracker) live at the top of the main content wrapper and do not consume visible space.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly 70–80% of the initial viewport height on desktop. The top ~40% of the hero is dedicated to vertical padding plus the About title and optional intro. The middle ~20% contains the view-mode toggle and any supporting metadata, keeping plenty of breathing room. The lower ~20% of the hero is reserved for the in-page TOC pill row, visually anchored just above the fold. Below the hero, the content section list fills the remaining page height with generous vertical separation (48–64px) between major sections. All foreground content is contained within a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px) with side paddings of 24–32px on desktop. Buttons, toggles, and pills respect the 8px spacing system (e.g., 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding).
layout_strategies.content_density: The hero remains low-density: one main title, possibly a brief subtitle, a compact view-mode control, and a clean single-row TOC. No dense paragraphs appear in the hero; explanatory copy is pushed into the sections below. The main About content is medium density: clearly separated sections, each with a heading and 1–3 short paragraphs or lists, keeping line length comfortable within the central column. The TOC uses a small number of concise labels in pill form, spaced out with 8–16px gaps so it feels scannable rather than crowded. Invisible components do not affect content density.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow starts with the fixed header (logo left to nav right), then drops into the hero where the eye is pulled to the large white About title. From the title, the eye moves slightly down and right to the view-mode toggle (styled with a subtle bright accent when active), then continues down to the high-contrast pill-shaped TOC row at the bottom of the hero. As users scroll, the TOC becomes a sticky horizontal bar under the header, guiding attention to each section. The scroll tracker highlights the currently active TOC pill, reinforcing movement through the content. If loading or error states occur, a centralized overlay intercepts the flow, focusing attention on status messaging before users re-engage with the hero and section content.

layout.overall_description: The About page sits within the same modern, conversion-focused frame as the homepage: a fixed, slim header at the very top and a full-width hero below, with a dark, tech-flavored background map overlaid by a subtle gradient. Centered within this hero, the About title is large and bright, aligned to a 12-column grid inside a max-width content container, with generous top padding so it breathes above the background. Directly beneath the title, a compact, pill-like view-mode toggle aligns to the right half of the grid on desktop, hinting at a product-like interface. Near the bottom of the hero, a horizontal strip of in-page navigation pills stretches across the central width, echoing the categories row from the homepage; it lightly floats above the background with a soft shadow. Scrolling down, this TOC bar snaps to a sticky position just below the global header, while the main About sections unfold vertically in a clean, centered column, each block separated by a calm amount of whitespace. Invisible logic components sit behind the scenes—controlling loading, URL-driven section focus, and scroll-based highlighting—so the user experiences a smooth, guided progression from title to TOC to detailed explanations about the directory, providers, and ratings. When loading or error states appear, a semi-opaque overlay gently dims the hero and content, centering a status card that momentarily becomes the primary focal point.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-page-loader is a non-visual component positioned logically at the top of the About page’s main content hierarchy, just inside the central page container beneath the global header and hero wrapper. It occupies no visible width or height and does not disrupt the grid. Instead, it sits in the background, controlling whether the hero content (title, view toggle, TOC) and the section list are rendered or replaced by the loading/error overlay from the status component. From a layout perspective, think of it as an invisible layer aligned with the left edge of the 12-column grid, spanning the full content width but not affecting spacing or flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendering logic element; it does not participate in the visual layout and only toggles visibility of other components based on data-fetching states.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-title-display sits inside the hero area, centered within the max-width content container that is horizontally centered on the page. It spans roughly 8–10 of the 12 grid columns on desktop, leaving breathable margins on both sides. Vertically, it is placed in the upper half of the hero: there is approximately 64–96px of padding from the bottom edge of the global header to the top of the title block. The title itself appears as a multi-line heading in white, with any brief subtitle or supporting line directly beneath it, separated by 16–24px of vertical space. The text is left-aligned to match the site’s tech/startup feel but visually centered within the container. Below the subtitle, a 24–32px gap is left before the view-mode toggle, creating a clear stacked relationship that echoes the homepage’s headline-to-searchbar spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element within the hero, flowing with normal document layout and resizing responsively; it reflows to full-width on smaller screens while maintaining top-centered positioning in the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-section-list is the main content body and sits directly below the hero, separated from it by a 48–64px vertical gap to clearly mark the transition from hero to content. It is contained within the same centered max-width (~1100–1200px) container and occupies roughly the full width of the 12-column grid on desktop, though the actual text column for paragraphs should read like 7–9 columns wide to keep line length comfortable, optionally with slightly wider background width for visual balance. Each individual section within this list is stacked vertically with 48–64px of spacing between them. Section headings are aligned with the hero title’s left edge, maintaining a strong visual column, and scroll vertically beneath the now-sticky TOC bar. As the user scrolls, this list becomes the main vertical scroll content, extending down the page while keeping generous margins on the left and right so the layout stays airy and readable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable content column that occupies the main vertical flow of the page; its sections are used as anchor targets for the TOC and as reference points for the scroll tracker.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-toc-navigation is rendered as a horizontal strip of pill-shaped links that sits at the bottom of the hero area, aligned to the same central max-width container. On initial load, it appears roughly in the lower 20–25% of the hero’s height, leaving 24–32px of space above it from the view-mode toggle or any bottom hero copy. It spans about 80–90% of the container width, with each pill having 16–24px horizontal padding and 8–12px vertical padding, separated by 8–16px gaps. The strip has a subtle elevated feel—either via a light translucent background or blurred container with a soft shadow—so it appears to float above the dark hero background, similar to the category pills in the homepage mockup. When the user scrolls past the hero, this TOC bar becomes sticky: it pins itself just below the global fixed header and stretches across the full width of the page while keeping the pills centered within the max-width container. This creates a persistent, slim navigation band that tracks the user’s position in the About sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially part of the hero’s normal flow, then transitions to a sticky horizontal bar that remains fixed just below the main header once the top of the hero scrolls out of view.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-url-state-manager is an invisible, zero-size component that resides near the top of the About page’s DOM structure, conceptually aligned with the hero but not visible on screen. It does not occupy any portion of the grid and introduces no extra padding or margins. Its role is to synchronize the currently selected section or view mode with URL parameters, so interacting with the TOC or view-mode toggle updates the URL, and loading the page with specific parameters scrolls or focuses the appropriate section. From a mental layout perspective, you can imagine it as a hidden controller positioned just behind the hero and section list, listening to navigation events without altering their visual placement.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual state layer with no spatial footprint; it only affects scroll positions and active states in other components without influencing layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-scroll-tracker is another invisible component that conceptually overlays the vertical span of the about-section-list. It attaches to each section in the list but does not add any visible markers, spacing, or containers. As the user scrolls through the sections, it determines which section is currently in view and communicates that to the TOC navigation, causing the corresponding pill to be highlighted. Spatially, think of it as an intangible layer that runs from just below the hero to the bottom of the last section, following the same central column but remaining entirely out of sight.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual tracking layer that scrolls with the page content; it has no explicit position or dimensions and simply observes scroll positions of section elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-view-mode-toggle is a compact, pill- or segmented-control-style toggle placed directly beneath the about-title-display inside the hero. It aligns to the right side of the hero’s grid on desktop: roughly occupying 3–4 of the 12 columns on the right, while still within the central max-width container, creating a subtle horizontal tension between the left-aligned title and right-aligned control. There is approximately 24–32px of vertical space between the bottom of the subtitle/intro text and the top of this toggle. On narrower viewports, it drops below the title and centers horizontally. The control has internal padding of 8–12px vertically and 16–24px horizontally for each toggle option, matching the button padding from the mockup. It feels like a small, elevated UI element placed in the mid–upper portion of the hero, visually distinct but not competing with the main title or TOC row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero’s normal flow, aligned to the grid; it reflows from right-aligned on desktop to centered on smaller screens but does not become sticky or fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-loading-and-error-state overlays the main About layout when active. During loading, it presents a translucent dark layer that covers the entire hero and extends over the top portion of the section list (or optionally the full viewport), subtly dimming the background map and content while keeping their structure visible. At the center of the viewport (or hero, on shorter pages), a compact card or spinner is displayed: the card spans roughly 3–4 columns on desktop, centered horizontally within the max-width container, with 24–32px internal padding and slightly rounded corners, visually floating above the dimmed background with a soft shadow. For error states, the same central card layout is used but with error messaging and possibly a retry button. When this component is visible, it temporarily becomes the main focal point, sitting above the title, TOC, and content sections. When inactive, it occupies no space and is completely removed from the visual flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay sitting above all other page content; it covers most or all of the viewport with a semi-transparent backdrop and a centered status card, and disappears entirely when not in use.

name: How It Works
title: How It Works
description: Informational page that dynamically explains how to use the directory (search, filters, comparison, bookings, quote and visit requests) and what happens after actions are submitted.

core_features: Fetch and render the page title and ordered steps from getHowItWorksContent, including safe rendering of rich body_html for each step.
Provide an auto-generated table of contents based on the fetched steps, enabling quick navigation within the page.
Support deep links to specific topics or steps using URL parameters, auto-focusing and scrolling to the relevant step on load.

Expose contextual 'Start your search' navigation controls that send the user to index.html with URL parameters indicating the context (e.g., search, compare, booking, quote, home visit, pickup, general question).

Allow in-page navigation between different user journeys (searching/filtering, comparing providers, booking appointments, requesting quotes, arranging home visits or pickups) purely by filtering/focusing the fetched steps.

Explain, within the dynamic content, how to interpret ratings, reviews, prices, warranties, and what happens after submitting bookings, quote requests, home visit requests, pickup requests, or messages.
user_workflows: 1) User opens how_it_works.html with no URL parameters. 2) The page component calls getHowItWorksContent. 3) Once data is returned, the page shows the title and full list of steps explaining all journeys (search/filter, compare, booking, quote, home visit, pickup, messaging, interpreting ratings/prices/warranties). 4) User optionally clicks items in the table of contents to jump to specific steps. 5) At any point, user clicks a 'Start your search' control to navigate to index.html, where they can perform an actual search.

1) User opens how_it_works.html with a topic parameter (e.g., how_it_works.html?topic=booking). 2) The page calls getHowItWorksContent and receives title and steps. 3) After load, the client locates the step whose heading best matches the topic parameter (case-insensitive search in headings). 4) That step is marked as active, the page auto-scrolls to it, and it may be visually emphasized. 5) Within or near that step, a 'Start your search' control links to index.html with a query string that includes source=how_it_works and context equal to the topic value, allowing the search page to preconfigure its UI for that journey.

1) From a provider or search page, the user clicks a help link like 'How bookings work', which navigates to how_it_works.html with topic set (e.g., topic=booking_flow). 2) After getHowItWorksContent resolves, the relevant step that explains what happens after booking is focused. 3) The user reads about statuses (pending, confirmed, etc.) and expected next steps, as described in the dynamic content. 4) User can return to the search or provider listing by using browser back or by clicking a contextual 'Start your search' control that sends them to index.html with parameters indicating the original source.

1) User is unsure how ratings, reviews, prices, and warranties should influence their provider choice and opens how_it_works.html?topic=reviews. 2) The page loads content via getHowItWorksContent, finds the step explaining ratings/reviews/prices/warranties, and focuses it. 3) The user learns how the directory aggregates rating_average and rating_count, what review details mean, how price ranges and warranties are presented, and how to compare providers. 4) User then clicks a 'Start your search' control which navigates to index.html with context=reviews to allow the search page to, if desired, highlight sorting/filtering by rating or warranty.

1) A support email links a user to how_it_works.html?topic=home_visit to explain home visit and pickup flows. 2) The page loads steps via getHowItWorksContent, then focuses the appropriate step describing home visit or pickup workflows (including what happens after HomeVisitRequest or PickupRequest forms are submitted, as described in the content). 3) The user reviews the explanation and then uses 'Start your search' to open index.html with context=home_visit or context=pickup so the search page can preselect providers offering those services.
interactions: On initial page load, the main page component parses URL parameters (e.g., topic, step) and then calls getHowItWorksContent to fetch the dynamic title and steps.
When getHowItWorksContent resolves successfully, the title and all steps are rendered; the table of contents is built from the steps array using each step's heading.

If a topic URL parameter exists, the page attempts to match it against the headings of the steps (case-insensitive string comparison). If a match is found, that step index becomes the active step and the page scrolls into view; if no match is found but a step parameter exists, that numeric step index is used instead.
Clicking a table-of-contents item sets the active step, updates the URL parameter (e.g., ?step=<index>) via history API without full reload, and scrolls to the selected step.

Clicking a 'Start your search' button constructs a URL to index.html including query parameters such as source=how_it_works, context=<current-topic-or-active-step-key>, and optionally step=<index>, then navigates the browser to that URL.

If fetching content from getHowItWorksContent fails, the page shows a generic, non-hardcoded error state (e.g., using a message container that is not bound to specific static explanatory text) and exposes a retry action that re-calls getHowItWorksContent.
If the steps array is empty or missing, the page shows a fallback state that hides the table of contents and step navigation controls, while still displaying the fetched title if available.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven exclusively by URL parameters and the result of getHowItWorksContent, with no use of localStorage or other client persistence. The key URL parameters are: (1) topic: optional string indicating which journey the user is interested in (e.g., searching, comparing, booking, quote, home_visit, pickup, messaging, reviews). After getHowItWorksContent returns, the client searches steps[i].heading for a case-insensitive match to the topic value. The first matching step index is set as the active step. The topic value is also forwarded to index.html as context when the user clicks 'Start your search'. (2) step: optional integer representing a 0-based or 1-based index into the steps array (implementation-defined but consistent across the app). If topic is not present or no headings match the topic, the step value is parsed; if valid and within range, that step index becomes active and is scrolled into view. The step parameter is updated when the user clicks items in the table of contents, enabling direct deep links to specific steps. (3) source: optional string representing where the user came from (e.g., index, provider, email). The page does not change behavior based on source, but if present it is preserved and passed along to index.html when 'Start your search' is clicked, so downstream pages can adapt their UI. Internal UI state such as the current active step is always derivable from these URL parameters and the fetched steps, allowing the page to be fully restorable from the URL alone.

components.functionality: Root component for how_it_works.html. Handles initial data fetch from getHowItWorksContent, URL parameter parsing, error/loading states, and wiring of child components (title, table of contents, steps list, and navigation controls).
Displays the page title returned by getHowItWorksContent and exposes it to assistive technologies as the main heading of the page.

Generates a table of contents for the page based on the steps array. Each entry corresponds to a step heading and, when clicked, sets the active step, updates the URL step parameter, and scrolls to the associated step.

Renders the ordered list of explanatory steps, each with a heading and rich HTML body. Supports highlighting the active step determined by URL parameters and user interactions, and exposes internal anchor IDs for deep linking.

Interprets URL parameters (topic, step) after getHowItWorksContent has loaded and resolves them into a single activeStepIndex state. Implements the matching logic between topic and step headings and notifies other components when the active step changes.

Provides a 'Start your search' control that navigates the user to index.html. Reads the current URL parameters and active step context, and constructs a query string (e.g., index.html?source=how_it_works&context=<topic-or-derived-key>&step=<index>) before performing navigation.
Shows and hides a loading indicator while getHowItWorksContent is in progress. Prevents interaction with table of contents and steps until content is available.

Displays a generic error container when getHowItWorksContent fails and provides a retry action that triggers a new call to getHowItWorksContent. The error message is not hardcoded to any specific content from the API.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px). Nav, headline, search bar, and category pills all appear horizontally aligned to this central grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page reuses the hero-style, top-to-bottom central column from the mockup. Under the fixed global header, a full-width hero section with a darkened map background introduces the 'How It Works' content. Inside this hero, elements are stacked in a single vertical column aligned to a 12-column grid: dynamic title at the top, a horizontally arranged steps table of contents (TOC) just below, and the first portion of the steps list starting within the hero and continuing into the lighter main body below. A strong, centered call-to-action (Start Search) appears at the bottom of the steps as a clear end-of-flow action, visually echoing the prominent search CTA from the homepage. State-related components (router, loading, error) live within the same central content column, overlaying or replacing the steps region as needed.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page reuses the hero-style, top-to-bottom central column from the mockup. Under the fixed global header, a full-width hero section with a darkened map background introduces the 'How It Works' content. Inside this hero, elements are stacked in a single vertical column aligned to a 12-column grid: dynamic title at the top, a horizontally arranged steps table of contents (TOC) just below, and the first portion of the steps list starting within the hero and continuing into the lighter main body below. A strong, centered call-to-action (Start Search) appears at the bottom of the steps as a clear end-of-flow action, visually echoing the prominent search CTA from the homepage. State-related components (router, loading, error) live within the same central content column, overlaying or replacing the steps region as needed.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero section occupies roughly 70–80% of the initial viewport height on desktop, with generous top padding (around 80–96px below the fixed nav) and bottom padding (around 64px) to keep the content comfortably centered over the background image. The central content column is constrained to ~1100–1200px max-width, aligned to a 12-column grid, and centered horizontally. The dynamic title and TOC together take up the top 40–45% of the hero’s vertical space, while the upper steps content uses the lower 30–35%, with the remaining steps continuing into a solid light background below the hero. The Start Search button sits in its own high-contrast band taking about 15–20% of the visible vertical space near the bottom of the page once you scroll through the steps. Loading and error states occupy the same central width as the steps, sitting in the middle of that column.
layout_strategies.content_density: The hero follows a low-density, high-clarity pattern: one key title, no large paragraphs, and a concise TOC row. Text becomes denser as users move down into the steps list; each step card is self-contained with a short heading, 2–4 lines of description, and possibly bullet points, separated by generous vertical spacing (24–32px gaps between cards). Interactive elements (TOC pills and the Start Search button) are comfortably sized with ample padding (around 12px vertical, 20–24px horizontal) similar to the category pills and search CTA in the mockup. Loading and error messages are concise and prominent but not text-heavy, occupying a single card-like area.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s vertical journey with strategic horizontal scans: users start at the top-left global logo and nav, then drop into the large, center-aligned How It Works title. From there, attention moves straight down to the horizontal TOC pills, inviting a quick left-to-right scan of topics. Selecting or hovering over TOC items guides the eye down into the corresponding step cards. After consuming the steps, the eye is naturally led to the large, high-contrast Start Search button centered at the bottom as the primary conversion action. Blue accents in the title and button reinforce the flow from understanding (title/steps) to doing (Start Search). Loading overlays and error messages temporarily take focus by appearing centered over or in place of the steps content.

layout.overall_description: The How It Works page feels like a guided, narrative version of the homepage hero. A darkened, full-width map background with a subtle gradient overlay sits behind a clean, centered content column. Near the top, a bold, multi-line title explains how the directory works, with key words in blue mirroring the brand’s main CTA color. Just below, a horizontal row of softly glowing, pill-shaped TOC items floats over the background, similar to category chips in the original hero, letting users jump between key parts of the process (search, filters, comparison, bookings, quotes, visits). As you scroll, you move into a light background area where vertically stacked, card-like steps lay out the process with clear numbers, icons, and compact text, aligned to the same 12-column grid to keep everything structured and spacious. The TOC can remain visible at the top edge as you move through the steps, subtly anchoring orientation. At the bottom of the narrative, a prominent, wide Start Search button in bright blue sits centered with generous white space around it, acting like a condensed hero search CTA that invites users to put their new understanding into action. When the app is loading or when there’s an error, a semi-transparent overlay or prominent inline card appears in the center of the steps region, briefly taking over the focus while still respecting the tidy, minimalist aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page container spans the full browser width and at least the full viewport height, starting immediately below the global fixed header. It is divided visually into two main vertical zones: a top hero-style section with a dark gradient over the map-of-Romania background and a lower body section with a solid, light background (off-white or very light gray). Within both zones, all main content is constrained to a centered, max-width column (~1100–1200px) aligned to an implicit 12-column grid. Horizontal padding of about 24–32px on each side ensures breathing room on smaller screens. Vertical padding from the top of the hero content (beneath the site header) is around 80–96px, with 48–64px of bottom padding before transitioning into the lighter body section where the remaining steps and the bottom CTA sit. All other components (title, TOC, steps, CTA, states) are children aligned within this central column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level container in normal flow that defines the central grid and background transitions; it scrolls with the page and does not itself fix or stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The dynamic title sits near the top of the hero section, centered horizontally within the max-width column. It starts roughly 80–96px below the global header, occupying the central 8–10 columns of the 12-column grid to create generous left and right margins. The text is multi-line, with the primary line in large, bold white type and key phrases (e.g., 'Find repair services' or 'How it works') highlighted in a bright blue matching the brand CTA color. Vertical space above and below the title is generous: about 24–32px below it before the TOC row begins, preserving a clear, airy feel consistent with the mockup’s hero headline spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element positioned in the normal flow within the hero; it scrolls away with the hero as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The steps TOC appears directly beneath the header title as a horizontal row of pill-shaped buttons, centered within the same max-width column. It spans roughly 70–80% of the column width (about 8–9 grid columns), leaving soft margins on both sides. Each TOC item is a rounded pill with 12px vertical and 20–24px horizontal padding, lightly elevated with a subtle shadow or blur over the dark background, echoing the semi-transparent category pills from the mockup. Items are left-aligned within the row but the row itself is centered. Horizontal gaps of about 8–16px separate the pills; if there are many, they wrap to a second line while preserving 8–12px vertical spacing between lines. The TOC area as a whole has about 24px of vertical margin above (to the title) and 24–32px below before the first step content begins, making it feel connected but distinct.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially placed in normal flow below the title; on scroll, it can become a subtle sticky bar that pins to the top of the viewport (just under the global header) so users can always jump between sections while reading the steps.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The steps list begins just below the TOC, with the first one or two steps visible over the dark hero background and the rest flowing seamlessly into the light body background as the user scrolls down. The list is a vertical stack of card-like step blocks aligned to the 12-column grid and centered within the page container. Each step card uses about 8–10 columns in width, leaving 1–2 columns of margin on each side, and has internal padding of 24–32px. Cards are separated by 24–32px of vertical space to maintain a moderate density. A typical card layout: a small step label or number and possibly an icon on the left (aligned within the first 2–3 columns), with the step title and a short paragraph or bullets extending across the remaining columns. The top edge of the first step sits about 24–32px below the TOC. As the user navigates via the TOC or scrolls, the currently active section can be subtly highlighted with a stronger card shadow, slightly brighter background, or a blue accent border on the left edge, guiding the eye down the column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Normal-flow vertical stack that scrolls with the page. Individual steps may serve as anchors for TOC items, so clicking a TOC pill scrolls the list to bring the corresponding card near the top of the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The topic router itself is not visually prominent; it acts as an invisible controller for the content inside the steps list. Conceptually, it sits just around or above the steps list region within the central column. When a user selects a TOC item or changes context (e.g., type of repair or use-case), the router decides which step cards are visible or in what order they appear, but it does not create additional visible spacing or a separate block. Any visual indication of the current topic (such as a highlighted TOC pill or a label above the first step) is applied to the TOC and the steps list rather than to this component directly.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual state controller that does not occupy its own space; it dynamically updates which steps are rendered within the existing steps list area without altering the overall layout structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Start Search button is positioned near the bottom of the How It Works content, directly after the final step card. It sits centered within the same max-width column, occupying about 3–4 grid columns in width on desktop (roughly 30–40% of the content width), but the hit area is generously padded—about 12–16px vertically and 32–40px horizontally—forming a prominent pill-shaped button. The button is bright blue with white text, elevated with a strong but soft shadow similar to the homepage search CTA, ensuring it stands out against the light background. There is a generous vertical gap above it (32–48px from the last step card) and around 64px of bottom padding below, creating a clear sense that this is the final action after understanding how the system works.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in normal flow at the end of the steps section. Optionally, on very long pages or in interactive states, a subtle secondary version of this CTA could mirror inside a sticky footer bar, but the main layout assumes it remains as a large, central end-of-flow button.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading indicator appears over the steps list area when content is being fetched or recalculated. Visually, it is centered within the same max-width content column, roughly in the vertical middle of the current viewport portion of the steps region. It might be a circular spinner or animated dots inside a small, semi-transparent dark or light card (depending on background) with 16–24px padding and faint rounding. The card’s width is about 2–3 columns (20–30% of the content width), keeping it compact yet noticeable. The surrounding steps content can be dimmed with a light overlay or skeleton placeholders that respect the same card dimensions and spacing as the actual steps, maintaining the structural rhythm of the layout while indicating a transient state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays or replaces the steps list region, positioned absolutely or fixed within the page container so that it remains centered over the steps area while loading; it scrolls only if the page is scrolled significantly during the loading period.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error state appears in place of the main steps content when something goes wrong (e.g., failing to load explanation data). It occupies the same central width as the steps cards—about 8–10 columns—creating a cohesive substitution rather than a separate section. Visually, it is a card with 24–32px internal padding, a light background, and a clearly colored left border or icon in a red or warm accent to signal the error. The card is centered horizontally and typically positioned where the top of the first step would be, about 24–32px below the TOC. Short, direct error text sits at the top of the card, with optional supporting guidance and a smaller secondary action (e.g., retry) aligned to the right or centered at the bottom. Vertical spacing above and below the card mimics the spacing between step cards, so the layout remains consistent even when in an error state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline replacement for the steps list in the normal flow. When present, it pushes subsequent content (such as the Start Search button) down the page; when resolved, it disappears and the regular steps list reclaims that space.

name: Help & FAQ
title: Help & FAQ
description: Page that loads Help & FAQ content about using the directory (search, filters, map view, booking) and lets users browse, search, deep-link to specific questions, and navigate to the contact page when needed.

core_features: Load all FAQ entries from the getHelpFaqContent interface on page load.
Group FAQ entries by their returned category value and allow users to filter by category.
Provide a keyword search field that filters the loaded FAQs client-side by question and answer content.
Support deep-linking to a specific FAQ question via URL parameters, automatically expanding and scrolling to that question.
Render FAQ answers as HTML from the answer_html field returned by getHelpFaqContent.
Handle clicks on any rendered 'Contact support' links inside FAQ answers to navigate to contact.html.
Show contextual guidance when no FAQ matches the current filters or search, with a link to contact.html for additional support.
user_workflows: Browse FAQs by category: (1) User opens help.html with no URL parameters. (2) Page calls getHelpFaqContent and stores the returned faqs in memory. (3) The category filter component builds its list of selectable categories based on the category values returned by the API. (4) The default view shows either all categories or the category indicated by the category URL parameter if present. (5) User clicks a category in the filter; the page updates the category parameter in the URL and filters the visible FAQ list to that category only. (6) User clicks on a question to toggle its answer open or closed.

Search within FAQs: (1) User opens help.html or is already on the page. (2) User types a keyword or phrase into the FAQ search input and submits or pauses typing. (3) The page updates the q URL parameter to reflect the current search term. (4) The FAQ list component filters the in-memory faqs to those whose question or answer_html content matches the search term. (5) If at least one match exists, only those FAQs are shown; otherwise, the page displays a no-results message with a link to contact.html.

Deep-link to a specific question: (1) A user follows a link to help.html with a question parameter in the URL that encodes a normalized identifier for a specific FAQ question. (2) On load, the page calls getHelpFaqContent and builds normalized identifiers (slugs) for each question based on their text. (3) The page matches the question URL parameter to one of these normalized identifiers. (4) If a match is found, the corresponding FAQ item is automatically expanded and scrolled into view, and any related category filter is applied by setting the category parameter. (5) If no match is found, the page falls back to the default category and list view.

Escalate to contact support from FAQs: (1) User reads an FAQ answer rendered from answer_html. (2) The answer content may contain a link to contact support whose href points to contact.html or an internal anchor within that page. (3) User clicks the link; the click event is handled as a normal navigation to contact.html, potentially preserving the browser history so the user can return to the same FAQ with the same URL parameters. (4) On returning via the back button, the page re-reads the current URL parameters (category, q, question) and restores the same filtered and expanded FAQ state.
interactions: On page load, call getHelpFaqContent with no parameters, and initialize in-memory FAQ state once the promise resolves.
When the user selects a category in the category filter, update the category URL parameter (without reloading the page) and re-filter the FAQ list based on that category.
When the user clears the category selection (if such action exists), remove the category URL parameter and show FAQs from all categories subject to any active search query.
When the user types into the FAQ search field and submits or stops typing for a configurable debounce period, update the q URL parameter and filter the FAQs by that term.

When the user clicks on a question row in the FAQ list, toggle the expanded state of that question, update the question URL parameter to the normalized identifier for that question, and optionally update the URL hash for direct anchoring.

When the user collapses a question (e.g., by clicking it again or clicking another question), clear the question URL parameter if no question is expanded, or update it to the currently expanded question.
When the browser back or forward buttons are used, listen to the popstate event, re-parse category, q, and question URL parameters, and update the visible FAQ state to match those parameters.

When answer_html content contains a link whose destination is contact.html, allow normal navigation to contact.html, ensuring that history is preserved so the user can go back to help.html in the same state.
When no FAQ matches the current combination of category and q parameters, show a no-results message component with a button or link to contact.html.
page_functionality.state_logic: The help.html page maintains its state entirely via URL parameters and in-memory variables. The primary URL parameters are: (1) category: a normalized version of a category value returned by getHelpFaqContent.faqs[].category (for example, processed by lowercasing, trimming, and replacing spaces with hyphens). If present, the FAQ list is filtered to FAQs whose category matches this normalized value. If absent, all categories are eligible. (2) q: a free-text search query. If present and non-empty, the FAQ list is filtered client-side to FAQs whose question or answer_html content includes the query (case-insensitive). If empty or absent, no search filtering is applied. (3) question: a normalized identifier (slug) derived from getHelpFaqContent.faqs[].question using a consistent normalization function. On load or when this parameter changes, the page attempts to match the value to one of the derived identifiers. If a match is found, that FAQ is expanded and scrolled into view, and its category is applied by updating the category parameter if needed. If no matching question is found, the page ignores the parameter and shows the default FAQ state. On initial load, the page reads these parameters from the URL, calls getHelpFaqContent once, and then derives its display state (selected category, active search query, expanded question) purely from the data and the parameters. Any user interaction that changes these aspects of state (choosing a category, running a search, expanding a question) updates the corresponding URL parameters using the History API (pushState/replaceState) without reloading the page. The popstate event is handled to ensure that navigating via back/forward re-applies category, q, and question parameters to the in-memory FAQ data. No localStorage or other persistent client-side storage is used.

components.functionality: Coordinates loading FAQ data, reading and updating URL parameters, and orchestrating child components such as category filters, search, and FAQ list.
Calls getHelpFaqContent on page initialization, stores the returned faqs array in an in-memory state, and notifies dependent components when data is ready.
Builds a unique, sorted list of categories from the loaded FAQs and lets the user select or clear a category to filter the list of visible FAQs.
Provides a text input for users to search within FAQs; synchronizes the input value with the q URL parameter and triggers client-side filtering in the FAQ list.

Displays the current list of FAQs as an expandable/collapsible list, filtered according to the active category and search query, and marks the question corresponding to the question URL parameter as expanded.
Represents a single FAQ question and its answer; toggles expanded/collapsed state, updates the question URL parameter when opened, and exposes an anchor target for deep linking.
Safely renders the answer_html field as HTML, delegating click events on links that target contact.html to normal navigation so users can contact support from within an FAQ answer.

Converts category and question values from the loaded FAQ data into normalized identifiers (slugs), maps them to and from URL parameters, and listens to popstate to keep component state synchronized with the URL.
Displays when the combination of category filter and search query yields no matching FAQs, and offers a direct link to contact.html for further assistance.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px). Nav, headline, search bar, and category pills all appear horizontally aligned to this central grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same linear, top‑to‑bottom structure as the hero mockup: a fixed header at the very top, then a full‑width hero section with a dark overlay over the map background. Inside that hero, center a vertical stack: page title and short intro at the top, the FAQ search module directly beneath, then the FAQ category filter as a horizontal row near the bottom of the hero. Below the hero, place the FAQ results area (accordion list / no‑results message) in a white content section that aligns to the same central grid.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same linear, top‑to‑bottom structure as the hero mockup: a fixed header at the very top, then a full‑width hero section with a dark overlay over the map background. Inside that hero, center a vertical stack: page title and short intro at the top, the FAQ search module directly beneath, then the FAQ category filter as a horizontal row near the bottom of the hero. Below the hero, place the FAQ results area (accordion list / no‑results message) in a white content section that aligns to the same central grid.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Let the hero occupy roughly 60–70% of the initial viewport height on desktop (slightly shallower than the home hero, since this is more functional than promotional). Allocate the top ~25–30% of the hero’s internal height to the title and intro, the middle ~30–35% to the FAQ search input, and the lower ~20–25% to the category filter pills. The FAQ list section starts just below the hero and can extend vertically as needed. Maintain a max content width of around 1100–1200px, centered, with generous vertical spacing between stacked elements (24–32px) and larger section separations (48–64px).
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep the hero visually spacious and low‑density: one main title, a single short line of supporting copy, then the search input and a clear but not crowded row of category pills. The FAQ list below is moderately dense: multiple accordion items stacked vertically, each with ample padding and clear separation. Inputs and buttons remain large and touch‑friendly with 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding. Avoid side‑by‑side text blocks; stick to single columns for readability.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Maintain the mockup’s visual flow: the eye first catches the fixed top nav (logo left, minimal links and a CTA to the right), then drops to the bold white “Help & FAQ” title against the dark hero background. From there, attention moves downward to the bright, elevated FAQ search input in the center, then further down to the horizontal category filter pills. Once a category or search is used, the eye is naturally drawn down into the white FAQ content area where accordion questions invite interaction. Blue accent colors in the search button, focused inputs, and active category pills act as focal points, guiding users toward search and navigation actions.

layout.overall_description: The Help & FAQ page feels like a focused, functional variant of the main hero layout. At the very top, a slim fixed header spans the full width with the logo anchored on the left and a compact set of navigation links plus a primary CTA button aligned to the right, all resting over the darkened map‑of‑Romania background that subtly shows through beneath the overlay. Below this, the hero section fills most of the first viewport with a rich, dark gradient over the map, letting central white text and blue accents stand out. In the center of the hero, a bold “Help & FAQ” heading and a short line of supporting copy introduce the page. Directly beneath, a wide, pill‑like FAQ search bar sits prominently across the central grid, slightly elevated with a soft shadow, framed by generous top and bottom whitespace. Just below the search bar, a row of rounded category filter pills stretches horizontally across the content width, echoing the home page’s category shortcuts but tuned to FAQ topics like “Searching,” “Filters,” or “Bookings.” As the hero ends, the dark background transitions into a clean white main content area; the top of the FAQ list appears to slightly overlap or closely follow the hero, reinforcing continuity. In this white section, a stack of accordion question items stretches down the page within the same centered max‑width container, each item separated by comfortable vertical spacing and subtle lines or shadows. When expanded, answers appear directly below their questions, aligned to the same column, with generous padding for readability. If no results are found for the current search/filter, a simple, centered message block appears in this same area instead of the list. The entire layout preserves a clear top‑to‑bottom reading path, minimal distractions, and large, touch‑friendly interactive areas consistent with the modern, semi‑minimalist hero design.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page shell spans the full browser width and height, providing the structural frame for the fixed header, hero, and main content. At the very top, a fixed-height navigation bar (approximately 64–72px tall) is pinned to the top edge of the viewport. Inside this bar, a centered max‑width container (~1100–1200px) aligns all content to a 12‑column grid: the logo sits at the far left, navigation links are grouped toward the right, and a primary CTA button (e.g., “Contact” or “Add Listing”) is anchored at the far right. Beneath the header, the hero section fills about 60–70% of the initial viewport height on desktop, stretching edge‑to‑edge with the map background and dark gradient overlay. Within the hero, content is centered both horizontally and slightly above the vertical middle: a max‑width container is used so that all inner elements (title, intro text, FAQ search, category filter) align with the header’s grid. The hero’s internal padding uses large top spacing (accounting for header height plus ~64–80px) and bottom spacing (~48–64px) so elements breathe. Directly below the hero, the main content section (FAQ list / no results) spans the full width with a solid white background, while the content itself is again constrained to the same central container, maintaining alignment with elements above. The page shell allows vertical scrolling for the FAQ content while keeping the nav bar fixed at the top.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Header region is fixed to the top of the viewport; hero and main content scroll normally beneath it. The shell defines a central max‑width container for all interactive elements and ensures the background hero image with gradient covers the entire width and height of the hero area (background-size: cover).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ data loader operates behind the scenes within the main content area. It does not render visible UI of its own but conceptually sits at the top of the FAQ content section, just below the hero, managing the retrieval of FAQs before the list appears. During loading states, the space that will be occupied by the FAQ list can display skeleton rows or a simple loading indicator within the same central container, maintaining the same left and right alignment as the eventual list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; it reserves no explicit layout beyond the space used by whatever loading or fallback content is displayed. It behaves inline within the FAQ content flow, appearing where the FAQ list would otherwise appear.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ category filter appears inside the hero, horizontally centered beneath the FAQ search input. It sits in the lower third of the hero’s content area, aligned to the same max‑width container as the title and search bar. On desktop, the filter occupies roughly 80–90% of the container width, leaving comfortable left and right margins. Category options are presented as a horizontal row (or two rows if needed) of rounded pill buttons, each with internal padding of about 8–10px vertically and 16–20px horizontally and gaps of 8–12px between pills. The row is positioned with about 24–32px vertical space below the search bar, and 24–40px above the bottom of the hero, ensuring it feels visually anchored but not cramped. The pills sit on the hero’s dark background; active or hovered pills use bright blue fills or borders to stand out, while inactive pills are lighter or partially transparent. On narrower screens, the pill row becomes horizontally scrollable within the same centered container, keeping the left alignment consistent with the grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard block-level element within the hero’s vertical stack. It scrolls out of view with the hero as the user moves down the page. On mobile, the pill row may allow horizontal scrolling, but it remains vertically fixed within the hero area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ search input is the focal interaction element in the hero, positioned roughly in the middle of the hero section, directly below the page title and intro copy. It is horizontally centered within the max‑width content container and spans about 60–70% of that width on desktop, leaving significant whitespace on both sides to mirror the main directory hero’s search bar proportions. Vertically, it is placed with about 24–32px of space below the explanatory text and about 24–32px above the category filter row. The search input itself appears as a single, pill‑shaped or softly rounded rectangular container with a subtle shadow to give it elevation over the dark background. Internally, it contains a search icon aligned to the far left, the text input field occupying most of the central space, and an optional compact clear button or keyboard hint near the right, with overall vertical padding of 10–12px and horizontal padding of 18–24px. On smaller viewports, the search bar stretches closer to 90–100% of the container width while maintaining the same centered alignment and stacking order.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero’s vertical flow, scrolling normally with the page. It does not stick on scroll by default, but remains visually dominant while in view due to size, elevation, and contrast.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ list occupies the main portion of the white content section immediately below the hero. It is positioned at the top of this section within the same centered max‑width container, starting about 48–64px below the bottom edge of the hero (or overlapping slightly visually if desired with a subtle card effect). The list extends vertically down the page and is single‑column, with each FAQ question item spanning the full width of the container minus standard horizontal padding. There is consistent vertical spacing (16–24px) between items, and the entire list maintains a visual hierarchy that is clearly subordinate to the hero but dominant within the content area. As users expand questions, the list grows downward; any pagination or load‑more behavior would occur at the bottom of this column. On very long lists, the list simply scrolls; it does not attempt to fix itself or create dual columns, preserving readability and simplicity.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows as a normal block-level element within the main content area, taking up the full height needed. It scrolls with the page and adjusts height dynamically based on the number of question items and their expanded states.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each FAQ question item appears as a horizontally full‑width row within the list’s central container, with generous internal padding (about 16–24px on all sides) and a clear separation from neighboring items, either via a subtle bottom border, a faint dividing line, or a very light card shadow. The question text is aligned to the left, starting at the same left margin as other content in the container, while an expand/collapse icon (chevron or plus/minus) is aligned to the far right on the same baseline. The row’s height in collapsed state is governed by the text and padding, resulting in a compact but comfortable touch target. When the item is expanded, the answer content appears directly beneath the question line with additional top padding (8–12px) to distinguish the two. The item remains within the same visual column, and the expansion pushes down subsequent items in the vertical stack, maintaining clarity and hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an accordion row within the list. The question header area is clickable and toggles the visibility of the answer area directly underneath. It does not float or detach from the list; expands inline, increasing its own height and shifting subsequent items downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ answer renderer occupies the area directly under each question line within the question item when expanded. It is indented visually within the same width, typically by maintaining the same left edge as the question text but using a slightly smaller font size and increased line spacing for readability. The answer area uses additional padding: about 8–12px of top padding where it meets the question line, 0–8px on the sides (mirroring the question’s internal padding), and 12–16px at the bottom to give the content room to breathe. If a lighter background is used to distinguish answers, it is a very pale shade that contrasts gently with the white list background. Any inline links (e.g., “Contact us” or “Open the map view”) are styled with blue accent colors consistent with the rest of the site. The renderer ensures that multi‑paragraph answers, bullet lists, and inline links wrap neatly within the same text column width for optimal legibility.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within each question item, only occupying space when that question is expanded. It does not scroll independently; it behaves as normal flowing text content, expanding the height of the parent question item.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ state sync component is non‑visual and does not occupy a distinct area in the layout. Conceptually, it sits alongside the FAQ list and category/search controls, synchronizing the currently selected category, search query, and open question items with the URL. Any effect it has on layout is indirect: for example, when a deep link is opened, it causes the relevant question item to be expanded and, if necessary, scrolled into view within the main content column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; it does not reserve space or alter the structural layout, but it may programmatically trigger scrolling (e.g., scroll to a specific question) and expansion of items within the existing layout flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The no‑results message appears in the same main content section where the FAQ list normally resides. When active (i.e., when the current search query and filters yield no matching FAQs), it replaces the list visually. The message block is centered within the max‑width container and limited to about 60–70% of that width on desktop to maintain a clean, focused appearance. It is positioned with ample top margin (about 32–48px below the hero or below the search/filter section, depending on scroll) so it does not feel cramped against preceding elements. The block may include a simple icon or subtle illustration at the top, a clear headline (e.g., “No answers found”), and one or two lines of explanatory text, with a suggested action such as a link to the contact page. Internally, it uses comfortable padding (16–24px) and may be rendered as a light card against the white background to distinguish it from the rest of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears inline in place of the FAQ list within the main content flow. It occupies vertical space only when shown and scrolls with the page like other content. There is no fixed or floating behavior; it respects the same horizontal alignment and container as the list.

name: Contact Us
title: Contact Us
description: Page for users to contact directory support, submit general questions or feedback about the site, and request help with problems such as bookings or messages.

core_features: Load and display support contact information (support email and business mailing address) using getContactUsInfo when the page is opened.
Provide a general Contact Us form that allows users to submit inquiries and feedback about the directory, sending the data through submitSiteContactMessage.

Support pre-filling of the contact form subject (and optional issue type selection) based on URL parameters so that other pages can deep-link into this page with context (e.g., booking issue, messaging issue).
Display confirmation or error states for form submission while keeping the user on contact.html, using the response from submitSiteContactMessage and optional URL parameters.
Offer alternative contact methods based on the data from getContactUsInfo, such as a clickable support email link and a formatted business mailing address for offline contact.
Present guidance for common help topics (e.g., issues with bookings, provider messages, or using search and filters) and encourage users to include relevant details in the contact form.
user_workflows: Submit a general question or feedback: 1) User navigates to contact.html (via footer link or direct URL). 2) Page initialization calls getContactUsInfo to retrieve support_email and business_address. 3) The Contact Us form is displayed with empty fields for name, email, subject, and message. 4) The user enters their name, email, subject, and message. 5) The user clicks the form submit button. 6) Client-side validation runs; if fields are valid, the page calls submitSiteContactMessage with { name, email, subject, message }. 7) When submitSiteContactMessage returns success, the page shows a success confirmation with the acknowledgement text and may update the URL (e.g., ?status=success&messageId={message_id}) while staying on contact.html. 8) The user can continue browsing using site navigation or submit another message.

Report a problem with bookings, messages, or site features via deep-link: 1) User clicks a "Need help?" or similar link from another page that points to contact.html with query parameters such as contact.html?type=booking_issue&subject=Problem%20with%20appointment. 2) On load, the page parses URL parameters (e.g., type, subject, status, messageId) and calls getContactUsInfo. 3) The Contact Us form pre-fills the subject field with the value from the URL and optionally highlights the relevant help topic (e.g., booking issue instructions). 4) The user reads guidance under the appropriate help topic, adds details (e.g., provider name, appointment date) into the message field, and submits the form. 5) The page calls submitSiteContactMessage and, on success, displays a confirmation and may set or update status-related URL parameters (e.g., ?type=booking_issue&status=success&messageId={message_id}).

Use alternative contact information when the form is not suitable: 1) User opens contact.html. 2) getContactUsInfo returns support_email and business_address. 3) The Support Info component displays a clickable support email link composed from support_email and the mailing address formatted from business_address. 4) The user clicks the support email link, which opens their default mail client with the address populated using support_email. 5) Alternatively, the user copies the mailing address for offline correspondence using a copy action associated with the address block.
interactions: Page load: When the user navigates to contact.html, an onPageLoad handler triggers a call to getContactUsInfo, and the returned support_email and business_address are stored in page state for display in the Support Info component. The same handler parses URL parameters (e.g., type, subject, status, messageId) and initializes the Contact Us form and status display accordingly.
Form field input: As the user types into the name, email, subject, and message fields, onChangeField handlers update the in-memory form state and clear any validation errors for the edited field.

Form submission: When the user activates the "Submit" button on the Contact Us form, an onSubmit handler performs client-side validation (e.g., required fields not empty, email format). If validation fails, field-level error messages are shown and submitSiteContactMessage is not called. If validation succeeds, the handler calls submitSiteContactMessage with the current form values { name, email, subject, message } and temporarily disables the submit button to prevent duplicate submissions.

Form submission response handling (success): When submitSiteContactMessage resolves with success=true, the form submission handler re-enables the submit button, clears the form fields (optional), stores the returned { success, message_id, acknowledgement } in state, and shows a success message (using the acknowledgement text). The handler may also update the browser URL to include or modify query parameters such as status=success and messageId={message_id} without navigating away from contact.html.

Form submission response handling (error): If submitSiteContactMessage fails (e.g., network error or response with success=false), the handler displays an error state message, allows the user to retry, and does not change the success-related URL parameters. The form values remain intact so the user does not lose their typed content.

Support email click: When the user clicks the support email link displayed by the Support Info component, an onClickEmailLink handler generates a mailto link using the dynamically loaded support_email and initiates navigation to that mailto URL so that the user’s mail client opens with the correct recipient.

Copy mailing address: When the user activates a "Copy address" control in the Support Info component, an onClickCopyAddress handler composes the full address string from the business_address fields and copies it to the clipboard (where user agent permissions allow). A temporary confirmation message may be shown in-page indicating that the address has been copied.

Help topic selection or highlighting: When the page detects a type URL parameter (e.g., booking_issue, provider_message_issue, site_bug), or when the user clicks on a help topic link within the Help Topics component, an onClickHelpTopic handler updates internal state to mark the selected topic. The component adjusts which guidance text is expanded or highlighted, and may also suggest a template for the subject field that the form can adopt when requested by the user.

Status message dismissal: When the user closes the success or error banner related to form submission, an onDismissStatus handler clears the status indicator from the in-memory state and may optionally update the URL (e.g., remove status and messageId parameters using history.replaceState) so that refreshing the page does not re-display the old status.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Contact Us page uses URL query parameters to control initial state and to reflect some post-submission states, without relying on localStorage. Recognized parameters include: 1) subject (string): If provided, the Contact Us form's subject field is pre-filled with this value on initial load. This is commonly used when deep-linking from other pages (e.g., contact.html?subject=Problem%20with%20my%20booking). 2) type (string): Represents the general category of the user's issue, such as booking_issue, provider_message_issue, or site_feature_help (exact values are interpreted by the page logic, not hardcoded business data). On load, this parameter influences which help topic is expanded or highlighted in the Help Topics component, and may be used to propose a default subject text if the subject parameter is absent. 3) status (string): Used to reflect the outcome of the most recent form submission, typically set to success or error after submitSiteContactMessage completes. When status=success and a messageId parameter is present, the page displays a success state showing the acknowledgement text from the most recent submitSiteContactMessage response and can associate that state with the given messageId. If status indicates an error, an appropriate error banner is shown instead. The page should avoid calling submitSiteContactMessage again solely based on URL parameters to prevent duplicate submissions. 4) messageId (string): If status=success and messageId is present, it is used only for display or tracking context in the confirmation message; it is not used to refetch or mutate data via any interface, as submitSiteContactMessage is write-only and getContactUsInfo does not accept identifiers. When the user dismisses the status banner, the page may remove status and messageId from the URL using history.replaceState while staying on contact.html. All URL parameters are parsed once on initial load by the Page Init component; changes initiated by in-page actions (e.g., after a successful submission) are coordinated with in-memory state to keep the URL and UI consistent.

components.functionality: Initializes the Contact Us page by fetching support contact information, parsing URL parameters, and seeding the initial UI state for the form, help topics, and status messages.
Displays the directory's support email address and business mailing address returned by getContactUsInfo, and provides actions to email support or copy the mailing address.

Provides input fields for name, email, subject, and message so users can send general inquiries or feedback about the directory. Validates inputs and submits the data via submitSiteContactMessage while keeping the user on contact.html.

Shows success or error messages related to the Contact Us form submission, using the acknowledgement and message_id from submitSiteContactMessage and the status/messageId URL parameters to control whether the banner is visible.

Provides categorized guidance for common issues (such as help with bookings, provider messages, or using site features) and suggests what information users should include in their message. Highlights or expands a topic based on the type URL parameter to contextualize the Contact Us form.

Keeps the browser URL in sync with key page states without leaving contact.html. After successful or failed submissions, it updates or clears URL parameters such as status and messageId to accurately reflect the current status display, avoiding the use of localStorage.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px). Nav, headline, search bar, and category pills all appear horizontally aligned to this central grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Keep the fixed top navigation and full-width hero background with dark overlay. Inside the hero, use the same centered, stacked structure: page title and short supporting text at the top, then the main interactive area (support info + contact form) in the middle, and a horizontal row of help-topic shortcuts below. All foreground content sits in a centered max-width container aligned to a 12-column grid.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Keep the fixed top navigation and full-width hero background with dark overlay. Inside the hero, use the same centered, stacked structure: page title and short supporting text at the top, then the main interactive area (support info + contact form) in the middle, and a horizontal row of help-topic shortcuts below. All foreground content sits in a centered max-width container aligned to a 12-column grid.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero section nearly fills the initial viewport height on desktop. Roughly the top 30–35% is used for the headline and intro copy, the middle 40–50% for the support info and contact form (with the form occupying the visual center), and the lower 20–25% for the help topics row.
