Internal vertical spacing between these stacked sections follows 24–32px increments; section padding top and bottom is about 64–96px.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately spacious density: large, readable headline, short descriptive text, visually prominent form fields with generous padding, and compact but tappable help-topic cards. Avoid text-heavy blocks; prefer concise labels and short paragraphs. Allow ample whitespace between the support-info panel, the form, and the help topics to keep the page feeling clean and focused.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the logo in the fixed header, sweeps across the main nav and CTA, then drops to the bold hero headline. From there it moves downward into the main interaction zone, landing first on the contact form (primary action) framed by brief support info. Finally it flows to the help-topic row at the bottom of the hero, which works like the category pills in the original mockup, offering quick alternative paths to common issues.

layout.overall_description: The Contact Us page sits over the same full-width map-style hero background with a dark gradient overlay, giving a tech-savvy directory feel. A fixed, shallow header bar runs across the top with the logo on the left and a small set of navigation links plus a blue CTA on the right, all aligned to a centered 1100–1200px grid. Below this, the hero content is vertically centered within the viewport and constrained to the same grid. At the top of this content column, a large, multi-line white headline announces support for the electronics repair directory, with a short, one- or two-line description underneath. Beneath the text, the main interaction area forms a horizontally oriented block: on the left, a compact support-info panel card; on the right, a wider, elevated contact form card. Both cards use bright, white surfaces and subtle shadows, visually floating above the dark background. Just under the form, a slim status area appears when needed, while still feeling part of the same card. At the bottom of the hero, a full-width but horizontally centered row of rounded help-topic pills or cards stretches across, echoing the category shortcuts in the original design. Generous vertical spacing between headline, form area, and help topics keeps the layout open and easy to scan, while the bright blue accents on buttons and links pull attention toward key actions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component initializes the overall page shell. It sets up a full-width canvas with a fixed-height top navigation bar and a hero section beneath. The hero spans nearly the full initial viewport height and uses the darkened map/tech background image. Within the hero, all visible content is centered in a max-width container (about 1100–1200px wide) aligned to a 12-column grid. Vertical padding of roughly 80–96px at the top and bottom of the hero ensures that the headline and main interaction area sit comfortably away from the edges. The component also establishes the primary vertical stack order: header at the very top, then hero headline and description, then the combined support-info + contact-form area, and finally the help topics at the bottom of the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The header portion is fixed to the top of the viewport, while the hero content scrolls beneath once the user moves down. The hero background image is full-bleed and remains anchored to the viewport width, with its overlay applied consistently regardless of scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The support-info panel appears within the hero's main interaction zone, aligned in the same horizontal row as the contact form. It is positioned on the left side of the central content container, occupying roughly 4 out of 12 columns (about 30–35% of the container width) on desktop. The card has a white or very light surface, rounded corners, and internal padding of 16–24px all around, creating a compact but clear information block. It sits about 24–32px below the headline/intro text, with its top visually aligned to the top of the contact form card on the right. Vertically, it may be slightly shorter than the form, giving the impression that the form is the dominant element while this panel provides quick context: contact hours, response time, and maybe alternative support channels. There is at least 24px of horizontal gap between this panel and the contact form, keeping both distinct but clearly part of a single interaction area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The panel is static within the hero content; on smaller screens it should stack above the contact form with full-width, but on desktop it remains left-aligned in a two-column layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact form is the central interactive element of the page and sits within a prominent card to the right of the support-info panel. On desktop, it occupies roughly 8 out of 12 columns (about 60–65% of the container width), aligning its left edge with the right edge of the support-info panel plus a 24–32px gutter. The form card uses a bright white background, rounded corners, and a subtle drop shadow to visually lift it off the dark hero overlay. It has generous internal padding (24–32px) around all sides, with vertical spacing of 16–24px between form fields. The form’s top edge aligns horizontally with the top of the support-info panel to create a clean row; its bottom edge typically extends lower, reinforcing its importance. A concise form title and possibly a short line of helper text appear at the top of the card, followed by stacked input fields (name, email, subject, message, etc.) and a large primary submit button aligned to the right or full-width at the bottom. The button uses the same vivid blue treatment as the main CTA in the navigation to draw attention.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The form is static within the hero content and scrolls normally with the page. On narrow screens, it collapses to a full-width block stacked directly below the support-info panel, maintaining the same internal spacings and form field layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The form-status area is visually integrated with the contact form card, appearing just inside the top of the card, directly under the form title and above the first input field. It spans the full internal width of the form (minus card padding) and has a variable height depending on the message length, typically 40–80px tall. When active, it appears as a pill-shaped or bar-like strip with subtle but clear background color (e.g., soft green for success, soft red/orange for errors) and 8–16px of vertical padding, matching the 8px base spacing scale. There is about 8–12px of margin above and below this status area, ensuring it is visually distinct yet tightly associated with the form. When there is no message, this component collapses entirely, allowing the first form field to sit closer to the title and preserving the clean, spacious look.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: It is non-fixed and lives inside the vertical flow of the form card, expanding and collapsing dynamically as form state changes without shifting the overall position of the form itself.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The help-topics panel occupies the lower portion of the hero, mirroring the category shortcut row from the original mockup. It is positioned directly below the combined support-info + contact-form row, separated by about 32–40px of vertical space. The panel is centered in the same max-width container and spans most of the width (roughly 80–90% of the container), but its content appears as a horizontal row (or two rows) of rounded pill-like cards or compact tiles. Each pill has an icon and label (e.g., “Account & Login,” “Bookings & Messages,” “Listing Issues”), with internal padding of around 12–16px vertically and 20–24px horizontally, and a small gap of 8–16px between pills. The pills either sit on a subtle translucent strip or standalone on the hero background, but in either case they are visually elevated with light borders and/or shadows. On desktop, they align left-to-right starting from the left of the content column, wrapping as needed, while keeping the whole group visually anchored to the center of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The panel is static and scrolls with the page. On smaller screens, the pills reflow into multiple lines or a horizontally scrollable row while remaining directly beneath the contact form area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual, behavioral component that influences how the page’s visual sections respond to URL parameters or hash fragments. It anchors specific states (e.g., pre-selecting a help topic, opening the contact form, or showing an error status) to the URL. Spatially, it has no rendered footprint and does not consume any space within the layout; instead, it programmatically directs focus or scroll position to the relevant visible component, typically aligning the viewport so that the contact form card or a specific help topic sits comfortably in the upper-middle area of the screen.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No visible spatial behavior; it adjusts scroll position and component states silently in response to URL changes, ensuring that layout transitions remain smooth and consistent with the hero-centric design.

name: Terms of Use
title: Terms of Use
description: Page that retrieves and displays the website’s Terms of Use content, including section-based navigation and deep links, using dynamic data from the backend.

core_features: Fetch Terms of Use content from the backend using the getTermsOfUseContent interface on page load.
Display the last updated date for the Terms of Use based on the last_updated field returned by getTermsOfUseContent.
Render all terms sections using the sections array returned by getTermsOfUseContent, using each section’s heading and body_html.
Provide an automatically generated table of contents (TOC) based on the sections headings for quick in-page navigation.
Support deep-linking to a specific section via a URL query parameter (e.g., terms.html?section=<section-identifier>), automatically scrolling to and highlighting that section.
Update the URL query parameter when the user navigates between sections using the table of contents, without reloading the page.
Provide per-section actions such as copying a direct link to the section (using the current URL with the appropriate section query parameter) and optional back-to-top navigation.
Handle browser back/forward navigation to keep the visible section in sync with the section query parameter.
user_workflows:
View the full Terms of Use:
1) User opens terms.html (e.g., via footer link).
2) The page controller component calls getTermsOfUseContent with no parameters.
3) The response’s last_updated and sections are stored in in-memory page state.
4) The header component shows the page title and formats the last_updated value.
5) The section list component renders all sections with their heading and body_html.
6) The user scrolls through the content to read the terms.

Navigate to a specific section from within the page:
1) After terms have loaded, the TOC component lists all section headings in order.
2) The user clicks a heading in the TOC.
3) The click handler prevents full page reload, updates the URL query parameter ?section=<section-identifier> using history.pushState, and scrolls to the selected section.
4) The selected section is visually marked as active (functionally, the component sets an internal activeSectionId state based on the clicked item).

Open the Terms of Use at a specific section via a direct link:
1) The user follows a link such as terms.html?section=<section-identifier> from elsewhere on the site.
2) On initial load, the page controller reads the section query parameter.
3) The page controller calls getTermsOfUseContent.
4) When the sections data is available, the controller resolves the section-identifier to the matching section (by index, slug derived from the heading, or other deterministic mapping defined in the frontend).
5) The section list component scrolls focus to that section and sets it as active.
6) The user can then scroll normally or use the TOC to navigate to other sections, which updates the section query parameter.

Copy a direct link to a specific Terms of Use section:
1) The user scrolls to a section or navigates to it via the TOC.
2) Within that section, the user clicks a 'Copy link' control.
3) The section component computes a URL containing terms.html and the appropriate section query parameter and copies this value to the clipboard using browser APIs.
4) No new API calls are made; this uses the existing in-memory section identifier.
5) The user can share this link, and recipients will land on the same section via the deep-link workflow.

Use browser back/forward navigation between sections:
1) The user navigates between sections via the TOC, which updates the section query parameter with history.pushState.
2) The user presses the browser Back or Forward buttons.
3) The page controller listens to the popstate event, reads the current section query parameter, and updates the activeSectionId in its state.
4) The section list component scrolls to the corresponding section and updates which TOC entry is marked active, staying synchronized with the URL.
interactions: On page load, the page controller triggers getTermsOfUseContent and passes the resulting last_updated and sections to child components for rendering.
On successful data load, the TOC component generates a list item for each element in sections, binding the displayed text to each section’s heading field.

When the user clicks a TOC entry, the TOC click handler computes a section identifier (for example, using the section index or a slug derived from heading), updates the section query parameter in the URL with history.pushState, sets activeSectionId in state, and instructs the section list component to scroll to the corresponding section.

When the page detects a section query parameter on initial load or on popstate, it maps that identifier to a specific section in the sections array and sets the activeSectionId state accordingly, then scrolls to that section.

When the user clicks a per-section 'Copy link' control, the section component constructs a URL containing the current location origin, path (terms.html), and the section query parameter, and then calls the clipboard API to copy it; no additional API interfaces are invoked.

When the user clicks a 'Back to top' control within a section or at the bottom of the page, the page scrolls to the top and the section query parameter can be cleared or set to a neutral value using history.pushState or replaceState.
If getTermsOfUseContent fails, the page controller sets an error state and the page displays an error message component allowing the user to retry, which re-invokes getTermsOfUseContent when clicked.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page uses only URL query parameters and in-memory state (no localStorage or other persistent client storage). The key query parameter is section. On initial load:
1) The page reads window.location.search and extracts the value of the section query parameter, if present.
2) It stores this value in a transient variable such as requestedSectionIdentifier.
3) The page calls getTermsOfUseContent and stores its response in state properties such as termsLastUpdated and termsSections.
4) Once sections are loaded, the page attempts to match requestedSectionIdentifier to one of the sections:
   - If section is numeric, it can be interpreted as an index into sections.
   - If section is non-numeric, the frontend can deterministically derive identifiers from headings (e.g., by lowercasing and replacing whitespace with separators) and compare them.
5) If a matching section is found, activeSectionId is set to that section’s identifier and the UI scrolls to that section. If no match is found or section is absent, activeSectionId is set to a default (e.g., the first section or a special 'none' state).
When the user interacts with the TOC or section controls:
1) Clicking a TOC item updates activeSectionId and uses history.pushState to set or change the section query parameter to the identifier of that section.
2) Clicking a 'Back to top' control can update the URL by removing the section query parameter (using history.replaceState or pushState with a URL that omits section) and set activeSectionId to a neutral value.
3) The window.onpopstate handler listens for back/forward navigation; when triggered, it re-reads the section query parameter, resolves it to a section (if possible), updates activeSectionId, and instructs child components to update scroll position and active highlights.
Throughout, all rendered content (headings and HTML bodies) is bound to the current termsSections state originating from getTermsOfUseContent, with no hardcoded terms text.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL state handling, and communication between the header, table of contents, and section list for the Terms of Use page.
Displays the static page title and the dynamically loaded last updated date for the Terms of Use.
Renders a table of contents derived from the Terms of Use sections, and allows the user to navigate to a specific section. Also updates the URL section query parameter when a TOC item is selected.
Renders each Terms of Use section using its heading and body_html, manages scrolling to a section when requested, and tracks which section is currently active for highlighting and deep-linking.

Represents a single Terms of Use section, binding its heading and body_html, exposing an anchor or identifier used for deep-linking, and providing per-section actions such as copying the section link and navigating back to the top.
Displays an error message and retry control when getTermsOfUseContent fails, allowing the user to attempt loading the Terms of Use again.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px). Nav, headline, search bar, and category pills all appear horizontally aligned to this central grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing fixed global header (logo + nav + CTA, from the main mockup), the Terms of Use page uses the same full-width hero band with a darkened map/tech background. Inside this hero, content is stacked vertically and aligned to the central 12-column grid: first the page title and brief description (terms-header), then a horizontal band of navigation pills representing the table of contents (terms-toc), visually echoing the category pills from the mockup. Beneath the hero, the main terms content (terms-section-list with multiple terms-section-item components) sits in a white sheet-like area centered on the page. Any error state (terms-error-state) appears in the main content area, replacing the section list while preserving the overall spacing and alignment.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing fixed global header (logo + nav + CTA, from the main mockup), the Terms of Use page uses the same full-width hero band with a darkened map/tech background. Inside this hero, content is stacked vertically and aligned to the central 12-column grid: first the page title and brief description (terms-header), then a horizontal band of navigation pills representing the table of contents (terms-toc), visually echoing the category pills from the mockup. Beneath the hero, the main terms content (terms-section-list with multiple terms-section-item components) sits in a white sheet-like area centered on the page. Any error state (terms-error-state) appears in the main content area, replacing the section list while preserving the overall spacing and alignment.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero for the terms page occupies roughly 40–50% of the initial viewport height on desktop. The top 60–70% of that hero height is given to the terms-header (page title and intro), with generous top padding (around 64–96px) to clear the fixed global navigation. The bottom 30–40% of the hero is allocated to the horizontal terms-toc band, sitting slightly elevated above the transition into the main white content area. The main terms-section-list then continues vertically down the page, using the full width of the central content container (about 1100–1200px), with each section separated by comfortable vertical spacing (32–48px). Error messages appear in the same central column, with margin top similar to the first section (48–64px), so they feel like the primary content when visible.
layout_strategies.content_density: The hero area remains low-density, mirroring the original mockup: a single strong heading, optional one-line description, and the TOC navigation pills as the only interactive elements. In the content area, the density increases due to the inherently text-heavy nature of terms, but the layout still uses generous vertical spacing and controlled line lengths (around 60–75 characters) to keep reading comfortable. Section titles are clearly separated and given breathing room, and the TOC pills are large, tap-friendly targets with internal padding reflective of the original category chips (16–24px) to maintain the semi-minimal, modern aesthetic.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the fixed global header, then drops into the hero where the bold 'Terms of Use' heading is the first focal point against the dark overlay. From there, attention naturally moves downward to the horizontal terms-toc band, which stands out as a bright, pill-based navigation row similar to the category shortcuts in the mockup. Clicking a TOC item smoothly scrolls the user down into the corresponding section within the main white content area, where section headings act as secondary anchors for the eye. If an error occurs, the elevated, centered error card interrupts this flow in the main content zone, immediately drawing attention due to its contrast and central alignment.

layout.overall_description: The Terms of Use page feels like a focused, content-first extension of the main hero design. A fixed global header remains at the very top, and just beneath it a full-width hero band stretches edge-to-edge, showing the same darkened map-of-Romania background with a subtle gradient overlay. Within this hero, a centered content column aligns to the 12-column grid: the 'Terms of Use' title and a short explanatory sentence sit in the upper portion, left-aligned but visually centered within the available width. Below, anchored toward the bottom of the hero, a horizontal strip of pill-shaped TOC items spans about 70–80% of the content width, slightly elevated with a soft shadow, echoing the category row from the original mockup. As the hero ends, the background transitions from dark to a clean white, where a large, card-like content area begins. This main area holds the vertically stacked terms sections: each section title stands out with clear spacing above and below, followed by well-structured paragraphs and lists. The scroll experience is linear and calm: hero → TOC → sections, with the TOC optionally becoming sticky just below the global nav on scroll to keep navigation close at hand. If something goes wrong while loading the content, a centered error card appears in the place where sections would normally start, maintaining the same alignment and visual weight so the user immediately understands that something in the main content failed to load.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-page-controller functions as the full-page layout wrapper, sitting directly beneath the global fixed navigation bar. It defines a full-width canvas with a max-width central content container of approximately 1100–1200px aligned to the center of the viewport, following a 12-column grid. Vertically, it organizes the page into two main bands: a hero band at the top that occupies roughly the upper 40–50% of the initial viewport height, and a long, scrollable white content band below for the terms sections. All other components (terms-header, terms-toc, terms-section-list, terms-error-state) are placed within this structure, with consistent horizontal padding of around 24–32px on the sides for smaller screens and alignment to the central grid on desktop.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level wrapper that stretches the full width and height of the page, coordinating the hero region and the main content region; it does not scroll independently but contains all scrollable content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-header sits at the top of the hero band, directly below the global fixed navigation, sharing the same full-width background: a dark gradient overlaying the Romania map and tech imagery. Within this band, the header content is confined to the central max-width container and aligned to the left edge of that container, while remaining visually centered within the hero. The top padding between the global nav and the page title is generous (around 64–96px) to create breathing room and echo the spacing of the original homepage hero. The title 'Terms of Use' (or equivalent) is set in large, high-contrast white text, occupying roughly 60–70% of the container width, potentially spanning two lines. A brief descriptive line (if present) sits 16–24px beneath the title in a smaller, slightly dimmer white or light-gray tone. The bottom of the terms-header content leaves space (around 32–40px) before the terms-toc band, ensuring the header and TOC read as distinct but related layers within the same hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero; moves out of view as the user scrolls down, while remaining visually connected to the hero background.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-toc is positioned directly beneath the textual content of the terms-header, still inside the hero band but closer to its lower edge. It occupies the same central 1100–1200px width and is horizontally centered in the viewport. The TOC items are arranged in a horizontal row of pill-shaped buttons that either wrap to a second line or scroll horizontally if there are many sections. This row spans roughly 70–80% of the container width, with equal left and right margins to keep it visually centered. Each pill has generous internal padding (about 8–12px vertically and 16–24px horizontally), rounded corners, and a bright white or light background with blue accents to stand out against the dark hero. The row itself sits on a subtly elevated plane with a soft drop shadow or subtle blur effect, mirroring the elevated search bar and category chips from the mockup. There is approximately 24–32px of vertical spacing above the TOC row (from the header text) and 32–40px of spacing below, leading into the transition where the background shifts from dark hero to the white main content area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially positioned within the hero; as the user scrolls past the hero, the TOC bar can become sticky, pinning itself just below the global navigation bar so that it remains visible at the top of the viewport while the terms-section-list scrolls underneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-section-list begins immediately below the hero band, where the background transitions to solid white. It is aligned to the same central max-width container as the hero content and occupies nearly the full width of that container (leaving only standard horizontal padding of ~24px on either side for readability). The top of the section list sits about 48–64px below the bottom of the terms-toc, creating a clear break between navigation and content. Visually, the section list reads as a single tall, white content column that continues down the page; each child terms-section-item is stacked vertically with consistent spacing (32–48px) between them, forming a rhythm of section title → body copy → gap → next section. The overall effect is that of a clean, legible document laid out in a single scrollable column that feels anchored and orderly within the central grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static scrollable content column; if the terms-toc is sticky, this list scrolls beneath the pinned TOC, maintaining its top margin relative to the TOC’s fixed position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each terms-section-item is a block-level content section within the main white column defined by terms-section-list. It spans the full width of that central column, aligning its left edge with the other components in the grid. At the top of each item, the section heading is displayed in a slightly larger, bold type, occupying roughly 60–70% of the column width, with 8–16px of space below it before any metadata or the first paragraph of body text. Body copy is laid out in a single column with comfortable line lengths and 16–24px of line spacing, and sub-lists or bullet points are indented slightly (16–24px) from the left edge of the text column. There is ample padding around the section content: about 24–32px of space above the heading (separating it from the previous section) and 24–32px below the last paragraph before the next section item begins. For deep-linking, each section item has an anchor point aligned with the section heading, which, when navigated to from the terms-toc, scrolls so that the heading sits just beneath the global nav (and sticky TOC if active), ensuring the title is clearly visible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked blocks within the main content column; each serves as a scroll anchor target for TOC links but otherwise behaves as standard vertical content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-error-state appears in place of the terms-section-list whenever loading or retrieving the terms content fails. It is centered within the same central content container, directly below the hero band and usually below the terms-toc (if present), inheriting the same horizontal alignment. The error is presented as a compact but visually prominent card, roughly 40–60% of the container width, with generous internal padding (24–32px), rounded corners, and a subtle shadow, mimicking the elevated feel of the search module from the original mockup. There is about 48–64px of vertical space between the bottom of the terms-toc and the top of the error card to emphasize that this is the primary content area. The message text is centered or left-aligned inside the card, with a clear headline (e.g., 'Unable to load Terms of Use') and brief explanatory text beneath, plus an optional retry button aligned to the right or centered at the bottom. The surrounding white space reinforces that this component has temporarily become the main focal point of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears conditionally in the main content region; static while visible and does not scroll independently. It replaces or overlays the section list area so the user clearly perceives the failure in the core content zone.

name: Privacy Policy
title: Privacy Policy
description: Displays the dynamically loaded privacy policy content, including data collection, usage, and user rights, and supports direct links to specific sections via URL parameters.

core_features: Fetch and render the latest privacy policy content using the getPrivacyPolicyContent interface.
Display the policy’s last updated date as provided by the API.
Render all policy sections (heading + HTML body) returned by the API.
Provide an in-page navigation list of sections based on API-provided headings.
Support deep-linking to a specific policy section via URL parameters and in-page navigation.
Handle loading and error states when retrieving privacy policy content.
user_workflows:
1. The user navigates to privacy.html from the footer or a direct link.
   1.1. On page load, a data loader component calls getPrivacyPolicyContent.
   1.2. While waiting, the page shows a loading state.
   1.3. On successful response, the page displays the last updated date and all sections.
   1.4. If the call fails, an error message is shown with an option to retry.

2. The user reads the privacy policy sequentially.
   2.1. The user scrolls through the full list of sections rendered from getPrivacyPolicyContent.sections.
   2.2. Each section heading and body is displayed in the order returned by the API.
   2.3. The user can continue scrolling to read all details about data collection, storage, sharing, and user rights.

3. The user jumps to a specific section via in-page navigation.
   3.1. After content loads, a table of contents is generated from the section headings.
   3.2. The user clicks a section link in the navigation list.
   3.3. The click handler updates the URL parameter (e.g., privacy.html?section=<derived-section-id>) using the section heading.
   3.4. The page scrolls to the corresponding section and visually highlights it as the active section.

4. The user opens a direct link to a specific section.
   4.1. The user or another site page links to privacy.html with a section parameter (e.g., privacy.html?section=<derived-section-id>).
   4.2. On page load, the init logic reads the section URL parameter.
   4.3. The page calls getPrivacyPolicyContent.
   4.4. After the policy content is loaded, the page locates the matching section (based on a derived ID from the heading or an index mapping to the parameter) and scrolls to it.
   4.5. The targeted section is marked as active in both the content and the navigation.

5. The user needs to copy a link to a specific section (for support or legal reference).
   5.1. After content loads, each section contains a control (e.g., a link or button) to copy or expose the URL to that section.
   5.2. When the user activates this control, the handler constructs a URL with the section parameter corresponding to that section.
   5.3. The handler updates the browser address bar (without reloading) or copies the URL to the clipboard, enabling the user to share a direct link to that section.
interactions:
On page load (initialization):
  - Trigger: window load or framework mount event.
  - Action: Call getPrivacyPolicyContent with no parameters.
  - Result (success): Store last_updated and sections in page state; render header, navigation, and section list; apply any section URL parameter to scroll/highlight the relevant section.
  - Result (error): Store error state; render an error message and a retry control.

On retry click (if loading failed):
  - Trigger: User clicks a 'Retry loading' control.
  - Action: Re-invoke getPrivacyPolicyContent; reset error state and show loading state.
  - Result: Same behavior as initial load on success or error.

On section navigation click (from table of contents):
  - Trigger: User clicks a section entry in the navigation list.
  - Action: Determine the target section identifier (e.g., derived from the heading text or the section’s index in the sections array); update the browser URL parameter ?section=... via pushState/replaceState; scroll smoothly to the target section element; mark that section as active.
  - Result: The content scrolls to the selected section; navigation list reflects the active section; URL now represents a sharable deep link.

On URL parameter change (back/forward navigation):
  - Trigger: User uses browser back/forward causing the section parameter to change.
  - Action: Read the current section parameter; if content is already loaded, scroll/highlight the corresponding section; if not yet loaded, store the intended section and apply it after getPrivacyPolicyContent resolves.
  - Result: The page content reflects the section indicated by the current URL.

On 'copy link to section' interaction:
  - Trigger: User clicks a control attached to a specific section.
  - Action: Generate a URL including ?section=... for that section; optionally copy it to the clipboard or display it for manual copy; update the URL in the address bar without reloading if needed.
  - Result: User can share or bookmark a link that opens the privacy page at the chosen section.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Privacy Policy page uses URL parameters to manage which section is emphasized and to support deep linking. The primary parameter is `section`, which identifies a specific policy section. The parameter’s value is derived deterministically from the API-provided sections (for example, using a normalized version of the heading or a stable index). When the page loads at privacy.html, it checks for `section` in the query string. If present, the value is stored in page state as `targetSectionId`. The page then calls getPrivacyPolicyContent. Once the API response is received and the `sections` array is available, the page maps `targetSectionId` to the corresponding rendered section and scrolls to it, marking it as active. If the user clicks on a section link in the table of contents, the page determines that section’s identifier, updates the `section` URL parameter via the History API, and updates the active section state without calling getPrivacyPolicyContent again. Browser navigation (back/forward) is handled by listening to URL changes: whenever the `section` parameter changes, the page reads the new value and updates the active section and scroll position accordingly. No user data is persisted via localStorage or other client-side storage; the only stateful behavior is derived from the current URL parameters and the in-memory result of getPrivacyPolicyContent.

components.functionality: Initializes the Privacy Policy page by parsing URL parameters, calling getPrivacyPolicyContent, managing loading and error states, and distributing the resulting data (last_updated and sections) to child components.
event_handlers: onInit: Parse the current URL for the `section` parameter and store it as targetSectionId in the page state.
onInit: Invoke getPrivacyPolicyContent with no parameters and set isLoading=true and error=null before the call.

onApiSuccess: When getPrivacyPolicyContent resolves, set isLoading=false, error=null, lastUpdated=response.last_updated, sections=response.sections; notify dependent components (header, navigation, content list).
onApiError: When getPrivacyPolicyContent fails, set isLoading=false and error to the error object/message; notify the error-state component.

onUrlChange: When the browser history changes and the query string updates, re-read the `section` parameter, update targetSectionId in state, and propagate it to navigation and content components for scrolling/highlighting.

components.functionality: Displays the page title and the 'last updated' information for the privacy policy, using the last_updated value from getPrivacyPolicyContent.
event_handlers: onDataUpdate: When lastUpdated changes (after a successful getPrivacyPolicyContent call), re-render the 'last updated' indicator.
onLoadingStateChange: When isLoading is true, optionally render a placeholder for the last updated date; when false, show the actual date.

components.functionality: Shows an error message and retry control when getPrivacyPolicyContent fails, and hides itself when loading succeeds.
event_handlers: onErrorChange: When error becomes non-null, display an error message area in the page.
onClickRetry: When the user activates the retry control, trigger privacy-policy-page-init to re-call getPrivacyPolicyContent and reset the error.

components.functionality: Indicates to the user that the privacy policy content is being loaded from the server.
event_handlers: onLoadingStateChange: When isLoading becomes true, display the loading indicator; when it becomes false, hide it.

components.functionality: Generates an in-page navigation (table of contents) for the privacy policy based on the headings of sections returned by getPrivacyPolicyContent, and allows users to jump to specific sections while updating the URL parameter.
event_handlers: onSectionsUpdate: When the sections array is updated, re-generate the navigation list with one entry per section, deriving a stable section identifier for each entry (e.g., from heading or index).

onClickNavItem: When the user clicks a navigation item, determine its section identifier and emit an event to update the URL parameter `section` to that identifier using the History API, then request the content component to scroll to that section.
onTargetSectionChange: When targetSectionId changes (through URL changes or user navigation), update the visual state of the navigation list to mark the corresponding entry as active.

components.functionality: Renders all privacy policy sections with headings and HTML bodies returned by getPrivacyPolicyContent, assigns internal identifiers to each section for deep linking, and manages scrolling and active section highlighting.
event_handlers: onSectionsUpdate: When sections are set or updated, render each section in order, attaching a unique DOM id derived from the corresponding section identifier used in navigation.
onTargetSectionChange: When targetSectionId changes, locate the matching section element by its DOM id and scroll into view; apply an 'active' state to that section’s container.
onMounted: After initial render, if targetSectionId is not null and the sections are loaded, immediately scroll to and highlight the matching section.

components.functionality: Provides per-section controls to expose or copy a deep link URL to that section using the `section` URL parameter.
event_handlers: onSectionsUpdate: For each rendered section, generate an associated control that can build a URL containing ?section=<section-id> for that section.

onClickCopyLink: When a user activates a section’s link control, construct the URL as currentBaseUrl plus the appropriate query parameter (preserving existing parameters other than section as needed); copy it to the clipboard or display it for manual copy.
onUrlBaseChange: If the base URL changes (e.g., due to environment differences or routing changes detected at runtime), update the constructed deep link URLs accordingly.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px). Nav, headline, search bar, and category pills all appear horizontally aligned to this central grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same linear top-to-bottom flow as the hero mockup: global fixed header (logo + nav + CTA) at the very top, then a full-width hero/intro band for the Privacy Policy header, and below that a central content zone. Inside this content zone, elements are vertically stacked but with a key horizontal strip just under the hero: a raised card that combines the section navigation (acting like the category pills row in the mockup) and the section link tools (mirroring the right-aligned CTA pattern). Below this strip, the actual privacy sections are stacked vertically in a single main column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same linear top-to-bottom flow as the hero mockup: global fixed header (logo + nav + CTA) at the very top, then a full-width hero/intro band for the Privacy Policy header, and below that a central content zone. Inside this content zone, elements are vertically stacked but with a key horizontal strip just under the hero: a raised card that combines the section navigation (acting like the category pills row in the mockup) and the section link tools (mirroring the right-aligned CTA pattern). Below this strip, the actual privacy sections are stacked vertically in a single main column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero for this policy page is shorter than the home hero but keeps the same visual language: about 35–45% of the initial viewport height, full-width background with dark overlay and the header text centered within a ~1100–1200px container. The content area below uses a max-width container aligned to the same grid. The section navigation + link tools strip occupies a shallow band (roughly 80–120px tall including padding), visually separated and slightly elevated. The main content column beneath it takes up the bulk of the vertical space, with generous top and bottom padding (48–64px) and roomy gaps (24–32px) between sections for readability.
layout_strategies.content_density: Compared to the hero mockup, this page is text-heavy, so density is managed with wide side margins, controlled line length (about 60–75 characters), and clear vertical rhythm: 24–32px between headings and paragraphs, 16–24px between paragraphs, and 32–48px between sections. The navigation chips and link tools remain large, pill-like, and well-spaced, echoing the mockup’s emphasis on generous hit areas and minimal clutter. The layout avoids cramming additional UI into the hero, limiting it to the title and brief description, and keeps the complex interactions in the main content band.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye starts at the fixed site header (logo left, nav right), then drops into the hero band where a bold, white 'Privacy Policy' headline on the dark gradient is the first focal point. From there, the eye is guided downward to the bright, elevated section navigation strip that acts like the category row in the mockup. The left-aligned section chips are the primary interactive focus, while the right-aligned link tools in the same strip form a secondary focal area. Clicking a section chip pulls attention directly into the corresponding section in the content column below, maintaining a strong top-to-bottom reading flow while allowing quick lateral jumps via the sticky navigation strip.

layout.overall_description: The Privacy Policy page sits beneath the same fixed, minimal top navigation as the rest of the site. Immediately below this, a full-width hero band uses the familiar dark gradient over the Romanian map background, but with a more subdued, legal-focused tone: a large 'Privacy Policy' headline and a single line of supporting text are centered within the main content width, left-aligned and surrounded by generous vertical padding. Below the hero, the background transitions to a lighter surface, where a raised, pill-edged strip spans the max-width container. This strip contains a horizontal list of rounded section chips on the left and compact link tools on the right, echoing the mockup’s navigation + CTA composition and category row. As the user scrolls, this strip becomes sticky just under the site header, anchoring the page’s navigation. Beneath it, the main content column presents each privacy section as a clear block with a bold section title, readable body text, and consistent spacing. Error and loading states replace this content column centrally, while the hero header and sticky section strip remain visible, preserving orientation. The overall impression is a modern, clean, tech-directory aesthetic applied to a policy page: visually anchored by the hero, navigable via prominent chips, and highly readable in the main text area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data-loader component is not directly visible but conceptually occupies the root of the page content area beneath the global fixed site header. It governs whether the main privacy content zone below the hero (the section navigation strip, link tools, and section list) is shown, replaced by the loading indicator, or replaced by the error state. In terms of layout, its influence is that it reserves the vertical region from just below the hero band down to the end of the document for either the loading, error, or content components, ensuring that all these states appear centered within the same max-width container and aligned to the same 12-column grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a controller for which child content renders in the main content region; has no direct visual footprint and follows standard document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-policy-header functions as the hero/intro band for this page. It sits immediately below the fixed top navigation bar, spanning the full browser width. The background continues the site’s hero treatment: the map-of-Romania visual with a dark, semi-opaque gradient overlay. Within this band, content is centered horizontally within a max-width container of about 1100–1200px, aligned with the same 12-column grid as the home hero. Vertically, the band has generous padding—approximately 64–96px of top and bottom padding on desktop—so the header feels spacious but not as tall as the homepage hero (roughly 35–45% of the viewport height depending on screen size). The main headline 'Privacy Policy' appears in large, bold white text, left-aligned within the central container and occupying roughly 6–8 columns, with a possible blue accent on a keyword to echo the tech visual language. Directly beneath it, there is room for one short line of supporting copy in a lighter weight, also left-aligned, with about 16–24px of vertical spacing. The overall composition keeps the text in the upper-middle of the hero band, with enough negative space above and below to avoid crowding and to clearly separate the hero from the content strip below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal page flow but visually anchored under the fixed site header; the hero band scrolls away as the user moves down, while the global header remains fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error-handler appears in the main content zone directly beneath the hero band and replaces the section navigation strip, link tools, and content list whenever loading fails. It is centered within the same max-width container as the rest of the page content. Visually, it takes the form of a prominent card: about 60–70% of the central container width on large screens, centered horizontally, with 24–32px of internal padding and rounded corners consistent with other elevated UI elements (e.g., the search bar card in the home hero). The card sits on a subtle, lighter background with a soft shadow, making it clearly stand out but still in harmony with the semi-minimalist aesthetic. Above the card is 32–48px of vertical space from the bottom of the hero, mirroring the spacing that would normally separate the hero from the section navigation strip. Inside the card, the main error message headline is top-left, with supporting text below and action buttons (e.g., 'Retry') aligned bottom-left or bottom-right, leaving plenty of whitespace around them. There is additional vertical margin (48–64px) below the card to keep it feeling balanced within the viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Rendered in place of the normal content area when triggered; scrolls with the page and is not sticky. It occupies the vertical space that the navigation strip and content list would otherwise use.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-indicator appears in the same central content zone beneath the hero, whenever the privacy policy is being fetched. It is centered both horizontally and, where possible, vertically within the visible portion of the content area. The indicator sits inside the max-width container, occupying roughly 30–40% of the container width, with ample whitespace around it. At minimum, it includes a spinner or progress element centered horizontally, with a short loading message beneath in a medium-weight text. There is approximately 32px of space between the bottom of the hero band and the top of the loading indicator, aligning it visually with where the section navigation strip would normally begin. The overall presentation is light and unobtrusive, consistent with the minimalist aesthetic: no heavy borders, just the loader and text sitting on the light page background with generous breathing room to avoid perceived clutter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Rendered in place of the normal content area while data is loading; scrolls with the page and is not sticky or overlayed. It temporarily occupies the space that would be used by the navigation strip and content list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-nav-list forms a prominent, horizontally oriented navigation strip directly beneath the hero band, mirroring the category pills row in the home mockup. It sits inside a raised, pill-edged container that spans nearly the full width of the main content container—about 90–100% of the max-width, aligned to the 12-column grid. This container has 16–24px of vertical padding and 24–32px of horizontal padding, plus a subtle shadow and rounded corners, visually lifting it slightly off the lighter background. Within this strip, the section navigation items are displayed as rounded pills aligned from the left, each with generous internal padding (8–12px vertical, 16–24px horizontal) and spaced apart by 8–16px. On large screens, the pills arrange in a single horizontal row; if there are many sections, the row can scroll horizontally within the container. The strip is positioned about 32px below the bottom edge of the hero band, with about 24–32px of space between its bottom edge and the beginning of the main content list. This navigation strip becomes a key anchor on the page: its elevated styling and central placement draw the eye immediately after the hero headline.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The entire navigation strip (its raised container and pill items) is sticky on scroll: once the user scrolls past its original position, it pins itself just below the fixed global header, maintaining its full width and elevation. This keeps section navigation accessible while the main content list scrolls underneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The content-list is the main body of the Privacy Policy, occupying the central column below the section navigation strip. It is aligned to the same max-width container and grid: on desktop, the text column spans roughly 8–10 of the 12 columns, centered horizontally to maintain an optimal reading width of about 60–75 characters per line. There is 24–32px of vertical space between the bottom of the navigation strip and the top of the first section heading. Each section is stacked vertically, with its title rendered as a distinct heading, followed by one or more paragraphs and possibly bulleted lists. Section headings have 24–32px of space above and 8–16px below, and separate sections are spaced 32–48px apart to create clear visual blocks. Anchor offsets are considered so that when a user clicks a navigation pill, the corresponding section heading scrolls into view slightly below the sticky navigation strip, preserving consistent spacing. The background behind the content is a flat, light tone, with no additional card container, letting the typography and generous margins provide structure and clarity. The entire content list extends downward as the primary scrollable region of the page, with a bottom margin of 64–96px before the page footer, echoing the comfortable vertical breathing space seen in the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow and scrollable as the primary reading area. It sits beneath the sticky navigation strip so that content scrolls underneath that strip while the strip remains fixed under the global header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-link-tools share the same horizontal strip as the section navigation, occupying the right side of the elevated navigation container. Within that container, the left roughly 70% is dedicated to the navigation pills, while the right 30% is reserved for the link controls. These controls appear as a compact group of icon buttons or small pill-shaped buttons aligned to the right edge, vertically centered within the strip. Each control has 8–10px vertical and 12–16px horizontal padding, with 8–12px gaps between them, and uses the same rounded shape language and color accents (e.g., blue icons or outlines) as the site’s primary UI elements. On wider screens, the controls are on a single row; on narrower screens they may wrap beneath the nav pills but remain within the same container, still aligned to the right or full-width below. This right-aligning echoes the header pattern (nav links left, CTA right) and visually balances the nav chips on the left. There is consistent internal padding so that the tools feel integrated, not tacked on, and the entire strip maintains a clean, uncluttered appearance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Because these tools are embedded in the same elevated container as the section navigation, they inherit its sticky behavior: as the navigation strip sticks below the global header on scroll, the link controls remain visible on the right side of that strip. They otherwise follow the normal flow inside the strip, adjusting layout responsively but always staying attached to the navigation band.
